EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few storms developed along a cold front in central Minnesota. While most of the storms remained below severe limits, one storm did reach severe limits in the Brainerd Lakes area and caused some tree damage as it moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small trees and branches were reported down in Nisswa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few storms developed along a cold front in central Minnesota. While most of the storms remained below severe limits, one storm did reach severe limits in the Brainerd Lakes area and caused some tree damage as it moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous small trees and branches were reported down in Brainerd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a very warm and humid air mass, featuring up to 3000 J/kg MLCAPE, an isolated thunderstorm developed mid-afternoon along an outflow boundary from overnight convection which stalled across southwest Minnesota.

Approach of a cold core upper level low resulted in bands of diurnal convective development. Instability was quite limited, as much as 500-1000 J/kg with weak deep-layer shear of around 20 knots, so the favored convective mode was short-lived pulse storms which remained disorganized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drifts of pea-sized hail at Camden State Park.  There were a few as large as nickel to quarter size.  Rainfall was 2.50 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Approach of a cold core upper level low resulted in bands of diurnal convective development. Instability was quite limited, as much as 500-1000 J/kg with weak deep-layer shear of around 20 knots, so the favored convective mode was short-lived pulse storms which remained disorganized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief and small landspout occurred in the northwest side of Pipestone, causing minor damage to three buildings. Two metal sheds sustained damage to small sections of roof and door panels. The tornado then crossed a street and hit an apartment building where a 10 foot by 5 foot section of the south facing roof was removed.  The average path width was 10 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Oppressive temperatures in the mid 90's to around 100 degrees along with dew point readings in the mid to upper 70's produced very dangerous heat conditions with heat index readings in excess of 105 degrees for much of the afternoon and early evening.||Some of the more extreme heat index readings experienced during the afternoon included 113 degrees at Wagner, 112 degrees at Le Mars, Canton and Mitchell, 111 degrees at Cherokee, Sioux City, Huron and Yankton, 110 degrees at Sioux Falls, Colton and White Lake, and 109 degrees near Beaver Creek.
A weak shortwave following a large scale trough earlier in the day provided enough lift to break a weak cap, and sparked an isolated hail-producing storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy damage to crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Approach of a cold core upper level low resulted in bands of diurnal convective development. Instability was quite limited, as much as 500-1000 J/kg with weak deep-layer shear of around 20 knots, so the favored convective mode was short-lived pulse storms which remained disorganized.
A gradual decrease in river levels occurred as water was routed downstream along the Des Moines River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from May, the West Des Moines River at Windom (gage site WDOM5) began the month at 0.72 feet above flood stage. Significant amounts of agricultural land remained flooded, and further impeded spring planting.

Cold Canadian high pressure settled over the Northland the night of the 18th into the 19th. Clear skies and light winds led to good radiational cooling conditions allowing lows to cool into the 30s and 40s below zero. In areas that didn't see winds completely subside, very cold wind wind chills approaching 50 below zero were recorded.

A disorganized trough of low pressure moved over the region starting late in the morning of the 21st and then exited the area during the mid-morning hours of the 22nd. While snow was seen across much of the Northland, a concentrated area of snow was seen from Cass County eastward to the Duluth and Two Harbors area. Snowfall totals in this band ended up in the 8 to 10 inch range while the remainder of the area was below 6.

This was the second day of a prolonged cold period across the CWA. Overnight lows dropped into the 20s and 30s below zero under mainly clear skies with wind chills in 30s and 40s below zero. Some clouds started moving in after midnight capping the cooling and preventing many locations from cooling into warning criteria. The clouds moved out shortly before sunrise allowing a secondary dip in temperatures and wind chills before warming above warning criteria.

High pressure moved over Minnesota following a cold front that brought new snowfall to the area the following day. With cold, Canadian air pouring in, coupled with clear skies, light winds and fresh snow, temperatures plunged into the 30s below zero with wind chills in the 40s below zero. However, many areas were spared from the extreme wind chills as winds completely subsided during the time of coldest temperatures.

This was day 3 of a prolonged cold period across the CWA. A weak system had passed through the area during the day on the 26th with a reinforcing shot of cold air arriving behind it. Skies cleared out by early evening with temperatures dropping into the 20s and 30s below zero over a wide area. Winds were able to remain light through the night leading to wind chills of 40 to 50 below zero in many areas. Some areas remained at warning criteria until midday before warming above criteria.

An area of cold Canadian high pressure began to move over the Northland the night of the 24th in the wake of a low pressure system. This low pressure system brought a layer of fresh snow to much of northern Minnesota. The fresh snow, clear skies and light winds helped plunge temperatures into the 20s and 30s below zero, resulting in wind chills to 40 to 50 below. This was the first night of a string of very cold air and wind chill temperatures that week across the area.

Initially, the morning of Sunday, January 27th, a ridge of high pressure was centered across the Upper Midwest. This ridge moved southeast with an area of low pressure developing over Alberta, Canada, and diving southeast across the Dakotas Sunday afternoon, and over northern Iowa, and northern Illinois through Sunday night/Monday morning.||This was a typical Alberta Clipper which raced southeast, with an initial very dry air mass over the Upper Midwest. Thus, moisture content was low, the speed was high, and the atmosphere had to moisten up before snow reached the surface. In addition, the forcing weakened during the afternoon, and evening across southern Minnesota, before intensifying across Wisconsin Sunday night. ||These factors limited snowfall amounts with narrow bands of 6 to 8 inches of snow across southern Minnesota, and portions of west central Wisconsin. The heavier amounts, or greater than 8 inches, occurred in southeast Minnesota, and into southern Wisconsin. There were also a few locations in central Minnesota, before the system weakened some, that received 6 to 7 inches of snow from Alexandria to Long Prairie. ||The following are some of the heavier snowfall amounts:||7.2 inches in Ellendale.|7.2 inches in Owatonna.|7 inches in Mankato.|6.5 inches near Faribault.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts in Todd County were based on official reports of 6 to 7 inches from Douglas to Morrison Counties, where locally up to 7 inches occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly rare multi-day wind chill warning was issued for eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. Morning temperatures on the 29th ranged from 15 below to 25 below zero, while afternoon temperatures ranged in the 20s below zero. On the 30th, morning temperatures ranged in the 30s below zero, while afternoon temperatures ranged from the teens below to 20s below zero. Finally, on the morning of the 31st, temperatures ranged in the 20s below to low 40s below zero. Combined with wind speeds of 5 to 15 mph, wind chill values ranged from 40 below to 65 below zero.

Heading into the last few days of January, a historically cold air mass settled over the region. This led to a prolonged period a sub-zero temperatures and dangerously cold wind chills. Many schools were closed for 2-3 days due to the cold. The coldest temperatures were seen January 30th into the 31st. All 5 of our climate sites recorded record low high temperatures on the 30th and International Falls set a new record low the morning of the 31st. A run was also made at the Minnesota state record low temperature the morning of the 31st when a COOP site at Cotton, Minnesota, reached -56F, which is good for 5th coldest all-time. Wind chills during this stretch remained in the 30s and 40s below zero with numerous locations reaching into the 50s below zero during the early morning hours. Temperatures finally started warming on the 31st as the core of the high moved to the east and warmer air began to move in ahead of an area of low pressure.

From January 21st to 22nd, one to eight inches of fluffy snow fell across the area. On January 23rd, a strong cold front swept southeast out of Canada, bringing a period of gusty north-northwest winds to most of eastern North Dakota into the Red River Valley, that continued into the 24th. At 3 P. M. CST on the 23rd, temperatures were generally in the teens. By the morning of the 24th, temperatures had fallen to the single digits below zero. Wind speeds gusted up to 56 mph in the immediate Red River Valley, but were slightly lower to the east and west. Very little snow fell during this time, so the blizzard was called a ground blizzard. The worst visibilities occurred in open country, with conditions being better in sheltered areas and cities.

Heading into the last few days of January, a historically cold air mass settled over the region. This led to a prolonged period a sub-zero temperatures and dangerously cold wind chills. Many schools were closed for 2-3 days due to the cold. The coldest temperatures were seen January 30th into the 31st. All 5 of our climate sites recorded record low high temperatures on the 30th and International Falls set a new record low the morning of the 31st. A run was also made at the Minnesota state record low temperature the morning of the 31st when a COOP site at Cotton, Minnesota, reached -56F, which is good for 5th coldest all-time. Wind chills during this stretch remained in the 30s and 40s below zero with numerous locations reaching into the 50s below zero during the early morning hours. Temperatures finally started warming on the 31st as the core of the high moved to the east and warmer air began to move in ahead of an area of low pressure.

Initially, the morning of Sunday, January 27th, a ridge of high pressure was centered across the Upper Midwest. This ridge moved southeast with an area of low pressure developing over Alberta, Canada, and diving southeast across the Dakotas Sunday afternoon, and over northern Iowa, and northern Illinois through Sunday night/Monday morning.||This was a typical Alberta Clipper which raced southeast, with an initial very dry air mass over the Upper Midwest. Thus, moisture content was low, the speed was high, and the atmosphere had to moisten up before snow reached the surface. In addition, the forcing weakened during the afternoon, and evening across southern Minnesota, before intensifying across Wisconsin Sunday night. ||These factors limited snowfall amounts with narrow bands of 6 to 8 inches of snow across southern Minnesota, and portions of west central Wisconsin. The heavier amounts, or greater than 8 inches, occurred in southeast Minnesota, and into southern Wisconsin. There were also a few locations in central Minnesota, before the system weakened some, that received 6 to 7 inches of snow from Alexandria to Long Prairie. ||The following are some of the heavier snowfall amounts:||7.2 inches in Ellendale.|7.2 inches in Owatonna.|7 inches in Mankato.|6.5 inches near Faribault.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts in Nicollet County averaged around 6 inches, with a few measured reports from a Cooperative observer near New Ulm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person died from hypothermia in Rochester (Olmsted County) on January 30th after being stranded outside a house overnight. Automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport recorded a low temperature of 29 below zero with wind chills as low as 60 below zero. ||With arctic air in place over southeast Minnesota, air temperatures dropped into the 20s below zero from January 29th into January 31st. A few sites recorded temperatures in the 30s below zero during the mornings of the 30th and 31st. When combined with wind speeds of 5 to 25 mph, wind chills of 35 to 60 below were common. The coldest wind chill to occur was 63 below zero near Dexter (Mower County) and near Ridgeway (Winona County) during the early morning of January 30th. Most southeast Minnesota schools were closed January 29th through the 31st. The U.S. Post Office suspended delivery operations across all of southeast Minnesota on January 30th and 31st. In Austin (Mower County) utility crews responded to over 140 calls for homes without heat and repaired 3 broken water mains. In Rochester, plans were put in place to use the city buses as a mobile warming shelter but many of the buses could not operate when the diesel fuel gelled after about 3 hours of service in the cold weather. A pipe burst in a furniture store flooding around 3 thousand square feet and destroying numerous pieces of furniture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 to 60 below zero were common across Dodge County from the afternoon of January 29th into the morning of the 31st. The coldest wind chill to occur was 60 below zero at a mesonet station near Kasson.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts in Steele County averaged around 6 to 7 inches, There was a measured report of 7.2 inches in both Owatonna, and Ellendale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person died from hypothermia in Rochester (Olmsted County) on January 30th after being stranded outside a house overnight. Automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport recorded a low temperature of 29 below zero with wind chills as low as 60 below zero. ||With arctic air in place over southeast Minnesota, air temperatures dropped into the 20s below zero from January 29th into January 31st. A few sites recorded temperatures in the 30s below zero during the mornings of the 30th and 31st. When combined with wind speeds of 5 to 25 mph, wind chills of 35 to 60 below were common. The coldest wind chill to occur was 63 below zero near Dexter (Mower County) and near Ridgeway (Winona County) during the early morning of January 30th. Most southeast Minnesota schools were closed January 29th through the 31st. The U.S. Post Office suspended delivery operations across all of southeast Minnesota on January 30th and 31st. In Austin (Mower County) utility crews responded to over 140 calls for homes without heat and repaired 3 broken water mains. In Rochester, plans were put in place to use the city buses as a mobile warming shelter but many of the buses could not operate when the diesel fuel gelled after about 3 hours of service in the cold weather. A pipe burst in a furniture store flooding around 3 thousand square feet and destroying numerous pieces of furniture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 to 60 below zero were common across Mower County from the afternoon of January 29th into the morning of the 31st. The coldest wind chill to occur was 63 below zero at a mesonet station near Dexter. In Austin, utility crews responded to over 140 calls for homes without heat, replaced 32 frozen gas meters and repaired 3 broken water mains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a weekend storm system that brought measurable snowfall to all of central and southern Minnesota, and the adjacent areas of west central Wisconsin, a surge of Arctic air moved southward. ||The core of the coldest air mass (that hadn't been felt across the Upper Midwest since the Winter of 1995-96) moved southward from Monday afternoon, January 28th, through Wednesday evening, January 30th. The center of the cold air mass moved across Minnesota the afternoon of Tuesday, January 29th where 850mb temperatures were around -38F.  Although temperatures slowly modified above the surface past the afternoon of Tuesday, January 29th, the combination of very strong winds and temperatures in the teens and 20s below zero, wind chill values averaged between 45 and 62 degrees below zero from Tuesday afternoon, through Wednesday morning. Although wind speeds lowered on Wednesday, actual temperatures in the 20s and 30s below zero kept wind chill values below -35F through Thursday morning. ||These temperatures during this Arctic outbreak averaged nearly 50 degrees below average for late January. The worst conditions were across west central, southwest, and south central Minnesota where wind chill values averaged near -60F. A wind chill of -62F was measured at both Benson and Glenwood airports, with -60F at New Ulm during the height of the storm.||The -28 degrees F air temperature in the Twin Cities on January 30, 2019 was the coldest ambient air temperature in the Twin Cities since -32 degrees F on February 2, 1996.||The -13 degrees F maximum temperature on January 30, 2019 was the coldest maximum temperature since -17 degrees F on February 2, 1996.||The Twin Cities saw twelve straight hours of -50 degrees F or colder wind chill temperatures from 7pm on January 30 to 6am January 31, with the coldest reading of -55 at 11pm January 30. This was the coldest wind chill reading in the Twin Cities since January 19, 1985.||Unfortunately, a 46 year old woman died due to the extreme cold in Carver County. The individual was found outside of her vehicle in Laketown Township. ||The bitter cold brought some natural gas shortages just north of the metro and power outages to about 7,000 in the southern and western suburbs. There were also broken water mains, and emergency personnel were busy with frostbite reports. Schools were closed for four days for many in the Twin Cities and outstate. The University of Minnesota was closed on the 30th and postal mail service was stopped statewide. At one point, CenterPoint Energy had asked hospitals to use alternate fuel sources during this extreme cold outbreak. The Twin Cities Public TV Now was disseminating extreme cold weather information in four languages on a continuous cycle. This was a collaborative project involving the NWS, State EM and TPT. Garbage collection was suspended. Numerous food services, grocery and department stores closed. The Minneapolis/St. Paul International Airport had numerous issues that were related to the extreme cold which included closing down specific areas of the airport due to heating problems. The airside operations had trouble with water and lav trucks freezing up. The Salvation Army helped 306 households (655 individuals) with more than $94,000 in mortgage/rent, utilities, and fuel cards, as well as over 5000 pounds of food. The Metro Transit in Minneapolis had one of the ���rails��� break due to the extreme cold.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several local weather sources measured wind chill values lower than -35F from Tuesday afternoon, through early Thursday morning. The coldest wind chill was -54F at the Anoka airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid surface high pressure built into the area from southern Canada. Temperatures in the teens below to mid 30s below zero combined with wind speeds of 5 to 15 mph to produce wind chills of 40 below to 60 below zero.

Initially, the morning of Sunday, January 27th, a ridge of high pressure was centered across the Upper Midwest. This ridge moved southeast with an area of low pressure developing over Alberta, Canada, and diving southeast across the Dakotas Sunday afternoon, and over northern Iowa, and northern Illinois through Sunday night/Monday morning.||This was a typical Alberta Clipper which raced southeast, with an initial very dry air mass over the Upper Midwest. Thus, moisture content was low, the speed was high, and the atmosphere had to moisten up before snow reached the surface. In addition, the forcing weakened during the afternoon, and evening across southern Minnesota, before intensifying across Wisconsin Sunday night. ||These factors limited snowfall amounts with narrow bands of 6 to 8 inches of snow across southern Minnesota, and portions of west central Wisconsin. The heavier amounts, or greater than 8 inches, occurred in southeast Minnesota, and into southern Wisconsin. There were also a few locations in central Minnesota, before the system weakened some, that received 6 to 7 inches of snow from Alexandria to Long Prairie. ||The following are some of the heavier snowfall amounts:||7.2 inches in Ellendale.|7.2 inches in Owatonna.|7 inches in Mankato.|6.5 inches near Faribault.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts in Dakota County averaged around 6 inches, There was a unofficial report from the public of 6 inches, near Hastings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person died from hypothermia in Rochester (Olmsted County) on January 30th after being stranded outside a house overnight. Automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport recorded a low temperature of 29 below zero with wind chills as low as 60 below zero. ||With arctic air in place over southeast Minnesota, air temperatures dropped into the 20s below zero from January 29th into January 31st. A few sites recorded temperatures in the 30s below zero during the mornings of the 30th and 31st. When combined with wind speeds of 5 to 25 mph, wind chills of 35 to 60 below were common. The coldest wind chill to occur was 63 below zero near Dexter (Mower County) and near Ridgeway (Winona County) during the early morning of January 30th. Most southeast Minnesota schools were closed January 29th through the 31st. The U.S. Post Office suspended delivery operations across all of southeast Minnesota on January 30th and 31st. In Austin (Mower County) utility crews responded to over 140 calls for homes without heat and repaired 3 broken water mains. In Rochester, plans were put in place to use the city buses as a mobile warming shelter but many of the buses could not operate when the diesel fuel gelled after about 3 hours of service in the cold weather. A pipe burst in a furniture store flooding around 3 thousand square feet and destroying numerous pieces of furniture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Rochester, one person died from hypothermia after being stranded outside a house during the morning of January 30th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta Clipper tracked from northern Alberta during the very early morning hours of January 27th to near Bismarck by the late afternoon. By the late evening of the 27th, it had shifted down into east central South Dakota. Typically, Alberta Clippers track toward the Great Lakes, so this further south track was a bit of an anomaly. The other odd thing with this Clipper track is that winds during the event were gusty from the southeast until the early morning hours of the 28th, when they turned northeast. With the typical Clipper track toward the Great Lakes, winds typically turn to the northwest and become gusty. Snowfall amounts ranged from one to nine inches, but there was a smaller area from Cavalier to Crookston to Park Rapids and Wadena where 6 to 9 inches of snow fell.

After a cold front moved across southeast Minnesota on January 8th, high winds buffeted much of the area. Most of the wind gusts were in the 45 to 55 mph range while the highest recorded gust was 59 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds buffeted Olmsted County following the passage of a cold front. The highest recorded wind gust was 59 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow fell across much of southeast Minnesota on January 18th. Amounts from this winter storm ranged from 4 to 11 inches with the highest totals occurring across Mower, Fillmore and Houston Counties. The largest reported totals were 12 inches in Brownsdale (Mower County) and 11 inches in Spring Valley (Fillmore County), Harmony (Fillmore County) and Spring Grove (Houston County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 6 to 8 inches of snow fell across the southern two-thirds of Dodge County.

The worst conditions were across west central, southwest, and south central Minnesota where wind chill values averaged near -60F. A wind chill of -62F was measured at both Benson and Glenwood airports, with -60F at New Ulm during the height of the storm.||The -28 degrees F air temperature in the Twin Cities on January 30, 2019 was the coldest ambient air temperature in the Twin Cities since -32 degrees F on February 2, 1996.||The -13 degrees F maximum temperature on January 30, 2019 was the coldest maximum temperature since -17 degrees F on February 2, 1996.||The Twin Cities saw twelve straight hours of -50 degrees F or colder wind chill temperatures from 7pm on January 30 to 6am January 31, with the coldest reading of -55 at 11pm January 30. The University of Minnesota was closed on the 30th and postal mail service was stopped statewide. At one point, CenterPoint Energy had asked hospitals to use alternate fuel sources during this extreme cold outbreak. The Twin Cities Public TV Now was disseminating extreme cold weather information in four languages on a continuous cycle. This was a collaborative project involving the NWS, State EM and TPT. Garbage collection was suspended. Numerous food services, grocery and department stores closed. The Minneapolis/St. Paul International Airport had numerous issues that were related to the extreme cold which included closing down specific areas of the airport due to heating problems. The airside operations had trouble with water and lav trucks freezing up. The Salvation Army helped 306 households (655 individuals) with more than $94,000 in mortgage/rent, utilities, and fuel cards, as well as over 5000 pounds of food. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta Clipper tracked from northern Alberta during the very early morning hours of January 27th to near Bismarck by the late afternoon. By the late evening of the 27th, it had shifted down into east central South Dakota. Typically, Alberta Clippers track toward the Great Lakes, so this further south track was a bit of an anomaly. The other odd thing with this Clipper track is that winds during the event were gusty from the southeast until the early morning hours of the 28th, when they turned northeast. With the typical Clipper track toward the Great Lakes, winds typically turn to the northwest and become gusty. Snowfall amounts ranged from one to nine inches, but there was a smaller area from Cavalier to Crookston to Park Rapids and Wadena where 6 to 9 inches of snow fell.

A band of heavy snow fell across much of southeast Minnesota on January 18th. Amounts from this winter storm ranged from 4 to 11 inches with the highest totals occurring across Mower, Fillmore and Houston Counties. The largest reported totals were 12 inches in Brownsdale (Mower County) and 11 inches in Spring Valley (Fillmore County), Harmony (Fillmore County) and Spring Grove (Houston County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 6 and 11 inches of snow fell across Fillmore County. The highest reported total was 11 inches in Spring Valley and Harmony.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta Clipper tracked from northern Alberta during the very early morning hours of January 27th to near Bismarck by the late afternoon. By the late evening of the 27th, it had shifted down into east central South Dakota. Typically, Alberta Clippers track toward the Great Lakes, so this further south track was a bit of an anomaly. The other odd thing with this Clipper track is that winds during the event were gusty from the southeast until the early morning hours of the 28th, when they turned northeast. With the typical Clipper track toward the Great Lakes, winds typically turn to the northwest and become gusty. Snowfall amounts ranged from one to nine inches, but there was a smaller area from Cavalier to Crookston to Park Rapids and Wadena where 6 to 9 inches of snow fell.

Bitter cold arctic air along with northwest winds brought extreme wind chills of 35 degrees below to near 60 degrees below zero from the morning of the 29th to the morning of the 31st. Some record  low maximums were set on the 30th and 31st. The high temperatures for both the 30th and 31st were in the mid to upper teens below zero across west central Minnesota. Schools were closed or started late for at least two days. Some of the lowest wind chills include, 45 degrees below zero at Odessa; 56 degrees below zero at Wheaton; 57 degrees below zero at Ortonville; and 58 degrees below zero at Graceville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Initially, the morning of Sunday, January 27th, a ridge of high pressure was centered across the Upper Midwest. This ridge moved southeast with an area of low pressure developing over Alberta, Canada, and diving southeast across the Dakotas Sunday afternoon, and over northern Iowa, and northern Illinois through Sunday night/Monday morning.||This was a typical Alberta Clipper which raced southeast, with an initial very dry air mass over the Upper Midwest. Thus, moisture content was low, the speed was high, and the atmosphere had to moisten up before snow reached the surface. In addition, the forcing weakened during the afternoon, and evening across southern Minnesota, before intensifying across Wisconsin Sunday night. ||These factors limited snowfall amounts with narrow bands of 6 to 8 inches of snow across southern Minnesota, and portions of west central Wisconsin. The heavier amounts, or greater than 8 inches, occurred in southeast Minnesota, and into southern Wisconsin. There were also a few locations in central Minnesota, before the system weakened some, that received 6 to 7 inches of snow from Alexandria to Long Prairie. ||The following are some of the heavier snowfall amounts:||7.2 inches in Ellendale.|7.2 inches in Owatonna.|7 inches in Mankato.|6.5 inches near Faribault.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts in Douglas County averaged around 6 to 7 inches, with a few measured reports from a Cooperative observer in Carlos.

An area of low pressure moved over the Northland on the 29th bringing light snow to much of the region. Winds off Lake Superior led to enhanced snow totals along the North Shore of Lake Superior 8 to 12 inches were recorded. The highest amount was 15.5 inches 18 miles NNW of Lutsen. Aside from this lake enhancement, it was otherwise an unremarkable system.

From Wednesday, February 6th to Thursday, February 7th, the main surface low passed from the Southern Plains up to the Great Lakes. However, there was plenty of forcing to create areas of heavy snow over the Northern Plains as well. The snow fell in three distinct periods, but the heaviest snow fell on Wednesday in a narrow band. This band may only have been 10 miles wide, but within this band, from 6 to 10 inches of snow occurred. This narrow band fell from Litchville to just north of West Fargo in North Dakota, then from Georgetown to Ada to Fosston to the Lower Red Lake in Minnesota. Combined with the other two periods of snow, most areas picked up a total of 4 to 12 inches of snow. Generally, wind speeds slowly increased on the Wednesday, with most stations reporting sustained 20 to 25 mph winds by the very early morning hours of Thursday in the Red River Valley. The strongest winds east of the Red River Valley occurred during the day on Thursday. Many schools closed and travel was very difficult, especially in open country.

A strong early April Colorado Low took shape over the Central Plains late Wednesday afternoon, April 10th. The surface low tracked nearly straight northward on Thursday, April 11th, reaching the Sioux Falls, South Dakota, area late in the afternoon. As it lifted northward, bands of heavy snow spread into southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota. Some of these bands of snow produced snow rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour. Generally most of this area received 6 to 12 inches of snow, but the Havana, North Dakota, and Bemidji, Minnesota, areas received 14 to 18 inches.

Heavy snow fell across portions of eastern North Dakota and northwest Minnesota from Sunday, February 3rd, to Monday, February 4th. At the surface, an inverted trough extended back into northwest Minnesota, helping to enhance the snowfall in that area. The most snow, 8 to 10 inches, fell from Hallock to Thief River Falls to Crookston in Minnesota, and around Petersburg in North Dakota. Elsewhere, amounts generally ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

A quick moving low pressure system spread snow across much of the Northland during the morning hours. Snowfall became heavy during the afternoon hours with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour and whiteout conditions. The greatest snowfall was across northwest Wisconsin and into adjacent areas of Minnesota with a secondary maximum along the North Shore of Lake Superior due to lake enhancement. Snowfall totals of 3 to 8 inches were seen across northeastern Minnesota with the highest being 8 inches 4 miles south of Bruno in Pine County.

Southeast Minnesota was covered with another six inches or more of snow from a storm system that passed across the region from late on February 19th into the 20th. Most snowfall amounts were in the 5 to 8 inch range with the highest reported total being 8.5 inches in Hayfield (Dodge County).

Most of eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota saw 2 to 6 inches of snow from this event. However, there was a stationary surface boundary that set up from near Aberdeen, South Dakota, northeast to just south of the Fargo-Moorhead area. Bands of steady snow kept rotating along this line, resulting in a fairly narrow band of 6 to 8 inches of snow from Oakes to Lisbon, North Dakota, to Sabin, Minnesota. Similar amounts were also reported in the Williams and Roosevelt areas near Lake of the Woods.

From Thursday night into Friday, April 12th, the low finally turned northeast and tracked toward Duluth. Wind speeds were not intensely strong during this blizzard event, as most sites only gusted into the 40 to 45 mph range. This event did occur during the spring snowmelt flood, when many rivers and ditches were filled with water. This brought an additional threat of vehicles sliding off slippery roads into water filled ditches. Interstate 29 was closed in eastern North Dakota and Interstate 94 was closed from Jamestown, North Dakota, to Osakis, Minnesota. Many other roads were either closed or had travel not recommended.

A strong Colorado Low lifted into the Great Lakes on Sunday, February 24th. Not much snow fell over eastern North Dakota or the northwest quarter of Minnesota, but strong northwest winds lasted for much of the day, which produced ground blizzard conditions in open country. Winds gusted up to 50 mph for many locations. The highest wind in the local area was at Baudette, Minnesota, with a 55 mph wind gust. This is a rare occurrence, since higher wind gusts are harder to come by in the densely treed areas of northwest Minnesota. However, it resulted in big impacts for dozens of anglers who were caught out on Lake of the Woods. These people were stranded on the lake until rescuers could reach them on Monday the 25th.

From Wednesday, February 6th to Thursday, February 7th, the main surface low passed from the Southern Plains up to the Great Lakes. However, there was plenty of forcing to create areas of heavy snow over the Northern Plains as well. The snow fell in three distinct periods, but the heaviest snow fell on Wednesday in a narrow band. This band may only have been 10 miles wide, but within this band, from 6 to 10 inches of snow occurred. This narrow band fell from Litchville to just north of West Fargo in North Dakota, then from Georgetown to Ada to Fosston to the Lower Red Lake in Minnesota. Combined with the other two periods of snow, most areas picked up a total of 4 to 12 inches of snow. Generally, wind speeds slowly increased on the Wednesday, with most stations reporting sustained 20 to 25 mph winds by the very early morning hours of Thursday in the Red River Valley. The strongest winds east of the Red River Valley occurred during the day on Thursday. Many schools closed and travel was very difficult, especially in open country.

Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of Tuesday July 16th across southern Minnesota. A few storms developed over the Twin Cities metro area and produced torrential rainfall, and flash flooding around the west, and southwest side of the downtown Minneapolis area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred on the westbound lane of I-394, near Penn Avenue. Flood waters were up to 2 feet deep.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding with up to two feet of water on Lake Street near Lake Calhoun.

Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation from September 10-12 that resulted in 5 to 10 inches of rainfall near the South Dakota border and 2 to 5 inches toward the east, extreme rises occurred on numerous creeks and rivers mid-month, with many basin locations reaching major to record stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After excessive two-day rainfall during September 10-12 (5.70 inches at Marshall), the Redwood River at Russell (gage site RUSM5) reached the sixth highest stage on record of 1.81 feet above flood stage on September 12. Significant agricultural land was flooded, with numerous county roads under water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation from September 10-12 that resulted in 5 to 10 inches of rainfall near the South Dakota border and 2 to 5 inches toward the east, extreme rises occurred on numerous creeks and rivers mid-month, with many basin locations reaching major to record stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After excessive two-day rainfall during September 10-12 (record 2-day rainfall of 6.75 inches at Pipestone), Split Rock Creek below Jasper (gage site JSPM5) reached the highest stage on record of 9.39 feet above flood stage on September 12. Numerous county roads were under water near Sherman, including 250th Street. Significant amounts of agricultural land were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong early April Colorado Low took shape over the Central Plains late Wednesday afternoon, April 10th. The surface low tracked nearly straight northward on Thursday, April 11th, reaching the Sioux Falls, South Dakota, area late in the afternoon. As it lifted northward, bands of heavy snow spread into southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota. Some of these bands of snow produced snow rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour. Generally most of this area received 6 to 12 inches of snow, but the Havana, North Dakota, and Bemidji, Minnesota, areas received 14 to 18 inches.

As the area of surface low pressure tracked into the Great Lakes on Thursday, February 7th, wind speeds increased even more. In the Red River Valley, wind speeds gusted up to 45 mph. Combined with the light, fluffy fresh snow, visibilities dropped to near zero in open country, and even near zero at times in the sheltered cities. The North Dakota DOT closed Interstate 29 from Grand Forks to the South Dakota border and Interstate 94 from Jamestown to Fargo. The Minnesota DOT closed Interstate 94 from Fergus Falls to Moorhead and portions of state highways near the Red River Valley. Schools were closed, flights were cancelled, and other activities were shut down.

Not much snow fell over eastern North Dakota or the northwest quarter of Minnesota, but strong northwest winds lasted for much of the day, which produced ground blizzard conditions in open country. Winds gusted up to 50 mph for many locations. The highest wind in the local area was at Baudette, Minnesota, with a 55 mph wind gust. This is a rare occurrence, since higher wind gusts are harder to come by in the densely treed areas of northwest Minnesota. However, it resulted in big impacts for dozens of anglers who were caught out on Lake of the Woods. These people were stranded on the lake until rescuers could reach them on Monday the 25th.

A strong early April Colorado Low took shape over the Central Plains late Wednesday afternoon, April 10th. The surface low tracked nearly straight northward on Thursday, April 11th, reaching the Sioux Falls, South Dakota, area late in the afternoon. As it lifted northward, bands of heavy snow spread into southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota. Some of these bands of snow produced snow rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour. Generally most of this area received 6 to 12 inches of snow, but the Havana, North Dakota, and Bemidji, Minnesota, areas received 14 to 18 inches.

From Wednesday, February 6th to Thursday, February 7th, the main surface low passed from the Southern Plains up to the Great Lakes. However, there was plenty of forcing to create areas of heavy snow over the Northern Plains as well. The snow fell in three distinct periods, but the heaviest snow fell on Wednesday in a narrow band. This band may only have been 10 miles wide, but within this band, from 6 to 10 inches of snow occurred. This narrow band fell from Litchville to just north of West Fargo in North Dakota, then from Georgetown to Ada to Fosston to the Lower Red Lake in Minnesota. Combined with the other two periods of snow, most areas picked up a total of 4 to 12 inches of snow. Generally, wind speeds slowly increased on the Wednesday, with most stations reporting sustained 20 to 25 mph winds by the very early morning hours of Thursday in the Red River Valley. The strongest winds east of the Red River Valley occurred during the day on Thursday. Many schools closed and travel was very difficult, especially in open country.

As the area of surface low pressure tracked into the Great Lakes on Thursday, February 7th, wind speeds increased even more. In the Red River Valley, wind speeds gusted up to 45 mph. Combined with the light, fluffy fresh snow, visibilities dropped to near zero in open country, and even near zero at times in the sheltered cities. The North Dakota DOT closed Interstate 29 from Grand Forks to the South Dakota border and Interstate 94 from Jamestown to Fargo. The Minnesota DOT closed Interstate 94 from Fergus Falls to Moorhead and portions of state highways near the Red River Valley. Schools were closed, flights were cancelled, and other activities were shut down.

A trough of low pressure moved over the Northland on the 5th and brought snow to the Northland. Snow was heavier than expected downwind of western Lake Superior where lake enhancement occurred. Areas just inland of the lake from Duluth to Two Harbors received 8 to 9 inches of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 9 of snowfall in Rice Lake and 8 of accumulation just northeast of Arnold.

An area of low pressure moved from the Southern Plains on the 9th to northern lower Michigan on the 10th. This track brought an inverted trough through the Northland and resulted in snow that started on the evening of the 9th and continued into the 10th. The greatest snowfall amounts were in the Brainerd Lakes area eastward into southern Pine County where 6 to 9 inches were recorded.

A Colorado Low tracked into the Central Plains during the evening of Wednesday, March 13th, with an inverted trough extending northward along the Red River of the North. To the east of the trough, temperatures ranged in the middle 30s. Along the trough in the Red River Valley, temperatures ranged either side of 32 degrees. To the west of the Red River Valley, temperatures held in the mid to upper 20s. North winds became quite gusty during the early morning hours of Thursday, March 14th, and remained that way through the day, as the low pushed into the Great Lakes. Gusts up to 60 mph were recorded. Snowfall totals of 6 to 9 inches were reported over most of eastern North Dakota into far northwest Minnesota, with the exception of the Fargo-Moorhead to Wahpeton-Breckenridge corridor, which received more rain than snow. The combination of snow and wind resulted in whiteout conditions in open country and huge drifts of snow. This blizzard produced the greatest impacts of any of the numerous 2018-19 blizzards. Flights were cancelled at the Fargo and Grand Forks airports and many schools were closed on the 14th, and even the 15th. Interstate 29 was closed from Fargo to the Canadian border and Interstate 94 was closed from Bismarck to Fargo. Many other roads across the eastern half of North Dakota were closed and remained that way into the 15th before they could be cleared. Some motorists who tried to travel on these roads got stuck and had to be rescued. Ice accumulated on some power lines, and when the winds hit, they galloped, causing sporadic power outages.

Runoff from rain and melting snow across the Upper Midwest produced high flows along the Mississippi River in late March. Flooding occurred from Lake City (Wabasha County) downstream to Winona (Winona County) and continued into April.

The winter snow melted first across portions of Grant, Wilkin, and Clay counties. Visible satellite imagery confirmed this fact, showing a distinct snow free north to south band that extended roughly 20 or so miles east of the Red River in these three counties. As the snow melted, it flooded low lying areas and roads. Numerous sections of land pooled with water from the snow melt.

A Colorado Low moved into Kansas on Saturday morning, March 9th. An inverted trough extended northward from the low into the Red River Valley. Temperatures were mild for early in the day, generally ranging in the 20s. Dew points were also in the 20s, so decent moisture was in place. By the afternoon of the 9th, a cold front began to push into eastern North Dakota. However, ahead of the front, temperatures had risen into the upper 20s to low 30s. The cold front swept through the rest of the area by the evening into the early morning hours of the 10th, causing temperatures to slowly fall. However, during most of the event, heavy wet snow fell. Snowfall totals generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches. One of the biggest impacts during the event was collapsing roofs. There were multiple reports of roof collapses around the area.

By the afternoon of the 9th, a cold front began to push into eastern North Dakota. However, ahead of the front, temperatures had risen into the upper 20s to low 30s. The cold front swept through the rest of the area by the evening into the early morning hours of the 10th, causing temperatures to slowly fall. However, during most of the event, heavy wet snow fell. Snowfall totals generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches. One of the biggest impacts during the event was collapsing roofs. There were multiple reports of roof collapses around the area.

Surface high pressure built into eastern Montana and western South Dakota on the morning of Sunday, March 3rd. This kept a decent pressure gradient over eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota, which led to steady west-northwest winds of 10 to 20 mph, from the 2nd into the 3rd. A frigid air mass had settled over the area as well, with morning lows on the 3rd generally from 15 below to 25 below zero. Combined with the steady winds, wind chills dipped to the 40s below zero. The coldest wind chill was Fosston and Dilworth, at 46 below zero.

The winter snow melted first across portions of Grant, Wilkin, and Clay counties. Visible satellite imagery confirmed this fact, showing a distinct snow free north to south band that extended roughly 20 or so miles east of the Red River in these three counties. As the snow melted, it flooded low lying areas and roads. Numerous sections of land pooled with water from the snow melt.

A shallow northerly push of colder air strengthened heading through February 3.  As a weak disturbance lifted northeast from the Rockies during the overnight hours, drying aloft on top of the shallow cold and moist layer along with weak lift resulted in widespread freezing drizzle.  A glaze of ice accumulation ranging from a few hundredths to a tenth of an inch impeded travel during the evening and overnight hours.

An area of low pressure moved from the Southern Plains on the 9th to northern lower Michigan on the 10th. This track brought an inverted trough through the Northland and resulted in snow that started on the evening of the 9th and continued into the 10th. The greatest snowfall amounts were in the Brainerd Lakes area eastward into southern Pine County where 6 to 9 inches were recorded.

The combination of rainfall and a melting snowpack, caused some flooding to occur across Wabasha County. There were numerous roads with debris on them that led to car accidents. One road was closed in the city of Wabasha and a mudslide occurred along County Highway 4 near the intersection with State Highway 60 north of Theilman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from rain and melting snow pushed the Cedar River out of its banks near Lansing. The river crested around a half foot above the flood stage at 18.65 feet on March 15th.

One road was closed in the city of Wabasha and a mudslide occurred along County Highway 4 near the intersection with State Highway 60 north of Theilman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from rain and melting snow pushed the Whitewater River out of its banks in the Whitewater River State Park. The river crested just under a foot above the flood stage at 14.42 feet on March 14th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from rain and melting snow pushed the Zumbro River out of its banks in Zumbro Falls. The river crested over four feet above the flood stage at 22.08 feet on March 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of rainfall and a melting snowpack, caused some flooding to occur across Wabasha County. There were numerous roads with debris on them that led to car accidents. One road was closed in the city of Wabasha and a mudslide occurred along County Highway 4 near the intersection with State Highway 60 north of Theilman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from rain and melting snow along with ice jams pushed the Root River out of its banks in Houston. The river crested over four feet above the flood stage at 19.28 feet on March 15th. This established a crest of record for this site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two storm systems, one over the northern Rockies, and another moving out into the southern Plains, merged over the central United States the weekend of March 9-10th. ||Initially, warmer air surged northward across the Upper Midwest and allowed for mostly rain and sleet at the beginning of the event across southern Minnesota, and into portions of west central Wisconsin. The warm layer held through most of Saturday, March 9th, before changing over to snow that evening. Farther to the west, and north, or across west central, and central Minnesota, enough cold air kept the precipitation in the form of snow. This is where the heaviest snow fell, with locally a foot in west central Minnesota before it tapered off Sunday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather information, including the Mora airport, and trained spotters, had indicated light snow developing late Saturday afternoon March 9th. There were a few hours of a mixture of snow and sleet, before changing over to snow. Some heavy snow was reported. Snowfall totals of around 6 to 7 inches fell across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather information, including the local airports, and trained spotters, had indicated a mixture of sleet and snow developing Saturday afternoon March 9th. There were several hours of this mixture before changing over to snow. Some heavy snow was reported. Snowfall totals of around 7 to 9 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were many factors that led to a severe Spring flood melt along the Minnesota and Mississippi Rivers and several of their tributaries during the last two weeks of March. The first factor was above average snowpack, especially over the Minnesota River Valley for mid March. Additionally, the added effect of a cold late Winter kept this snowpack deeper into the early Spring. Once temperatures warmed, and snow began to melt, a few storms, especially around the 12-13th of March, led to additional water on the already heavy snowpack. As area rivers, streams and creeks began to thaw, ice jams developed, which led to more areal flooding beyond the flooded river beds. ||Area streams, creeks, and mainstem rivers started to break up their Winter ice the second week of March, with a sharp rise in these basins during the three week sof March as the storm system around the 12th and 13th of March led to the ripening of the snowpack. Although most of the mainstem rivers along the Minnesota River crested the last week of March, flood waters continued into April. The Mississippi River crested after the last week of March, from St. Paul and points downstream.||During the third week of March, the Minnesota Department of Transportation started to close roads along mainstem rivers. County roads were already impacted as flood waters started to spread out across counties from melted snow and recent rainfall from storms.||There were numerous counties in southern and western Minnesota which were declared disaster areas due to the flooding.  They included Chippewa, Lac Qui Parle, Martin, and Yellow Medicine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed for several days due to snow melt, and the flood waters that develop from it. Rapidly runoff led to impassable roads, especially along streams and creeks along county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic late-season, multi-day winter storm developed rapidly across the central Plains on Wednesday, April 10. This system spiraled several periods of precipitation through the region. The leading precipitation on April 10 was a mix of rain, freezing rain and sleet from far eastern South Dakota through the Interstate 90 areas of southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, changing to snow to the north and west. There were even a few elevated thunderstorms which occurred overnight on April 10-11 and produced some near severe hail, which actually occurred after some initial wintry precipitation. Significant icing was centered around 30 miles either side of the Interstate 90 corridor, with as much as a third to three-quarters inch ice accumulation. ||Heavy snowfall developed from the early morning of April 11 and continued into the early morning of April 12 across much of southeast South Dakota into northern portions of southwest Minnesota, which accumulated to 1 to 2 feet during the storm. The most persistent snowfall occurred for areas west to north of Sioux Falls, but even there, a brief period of freezing rain or sleet occurred at some point ahead of the main upper trough passage. ||The other aspect of this storm was the intense winds, which gusted at the peak from 40 to 60 mph, producing widespread blizzard conditions across southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota. Considerable power outages occurred as transmission lines succumbed to the ice accumulation and wind.  At the peak, electric companies and cooperatives estimated as many as 25,000 customers were without power.  Many areas were without power for a day or two due to the intensity of the storm. ||In fact, the strong winds lofted dust from the Texas/Oklahoma panhandles which caused some of the snowfall toward the latter portions of the event to fall with a brownish color.  Ironically, the blizzard postponed the statewide tornado warning drill which was a part of Minnesota Severe Weather Awareness Week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions and ice accumulations led to a shutdown of government offices and schools, and travel was not recommended with many roads closed due to the widespread whiteout conditions. Storm-total snowfall from 4 to 10 inches occurred, with 6.0 inches near Edgerton and 5.5 inches at Pipestone. With winds gusting at times from 45 to 55 mph, many areas had snow drifts of several feet, making roads impassable. Ice accumulation from a tenth to a third of an inch occurred, and helped to produce scattered power outages as wind increased. Damage costs are rough estimates due to utility costs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter snow melted first across portions of Grant, Wilkin, and Clay counties. Visible satellite imagery confirmed this fact, showing a distinct snow free north to south band that extended roughly 20 or so miles east of the Red River in these three counties. In Fargo, the snow depth fell to zero on April 6th. However, a blizzard from April 10th to 12th brought more snow to the area. This snow finally melted by April 16th. As the snow melted, it flooded low lying areas and roads. Numerous sections of land pooled with water from the snow melt. Conditions were particularly bad from Campbell to Sabin, where drone footage showed many sections of land under water. Norman County was also hit hard. Highway 75 was closed due to high water south of Halstad. High resolution satellite imagery, drone footage, and vehicle reconnaissance showed numerous flooded sections of land over central and western Norman County. In Moorhead, the 12th avenue north and north Broadway bridges were closed due to high water in the Red River.

Two storm systems, one over the northern Rockies, and another moving out into the southern Plains, merged over the central United States the weekend of March 9-10th. ||Initially, warmer air surged northward across the Upper Midwest and allowed for mostly rain and sleet at the beginning of the event across southern Minnesota, and into portions of west central Wisconsin. The warm layer held through most of Saturday, March 9th, before changing over to snow that evening. Farther to the west, and north, or across west central, and central Minnesota, enough cold air kept the precipitation in the form of snow. This is where the heaviest snow fell, with locally a foot in west central Minnesota before it tapered off Sunday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather information, including the local airports, and trained spotters, had indicated light snow developing late Saturday afternoon March 9th. Some heavy snow was reported. Snowfall totals of around 5 to 7 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The snow took longer to melt on the Minnesota side of the northern Red River Valley, than it did in the southern Red River Valley. At the National Weather Service office in Grand Forks, the snow depth fell to zero on April 6th. However, a blizzard from April 10th to 12th brought more snow to the area. This snow finally melted by April 15th. As the snow melted, it flooded low lying areas and roads. Some sections of land pooled with water from the snow melt, but more so closer to the Red River. High resolution satellite imagery showed a lake of water where the Red River normally would be, from northern Polk County to Kittson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge of Arctic high pressure engulfed most of the northern one-third of the United States. As temperatures fell well below zero during the morning of Sunday, March 3rd, wind chill values dropped below -35F for several areas of west central, and central Minnesota. Wind chill values dropped as low as -47F at Appleton airport in west central Minnesota Sunday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The New Ulm airport measured wind chill values below -35F during the morning, with the lowest value of -44F.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Morris airport measured wind chill values below -35F for several hours during the morning of Sunday, March 3rd. The coldest value was -41F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were many factors that led to a severe Spring flood melt along the Minnesota and Mississippi Rivers and several of their tributaries during the last two weeks of March. The first factor was above average snowpack, especially over the Minnesota River Valley for mid March. Additionally, the added effect of a cold late Winter kept this snowpack deeper into the early Spring. Once temperatures warmed, and snow began to melt, a few storms, especially around the 12-13th of March, led to additional water on the already heavy snowpack. As area rivers, streams and creeks began to thaw, ice jams developed, which led to more areal flooding beyond the flooded river beds. ||Area streams, creeks, and mainstem rivers started to break up their Winter ice the second week of March, with a sharp rise in these basins during the three week sof March as the storm system around the 12th and 13th of March led to the ripening of the snowpack. Although most of the mainstem rivers along the Minnesota River crested the last week of March, flood waters continued into April. The Mississippi River crested after the last week of March, from St. Paul and points downstream.||During the third week of March, the Minnesota Department of Transportation started to close roads along mainstem rivers. County roads were already impacted as flood waters started to spread out across counties from melted snow and recent rainfall from storms.||There were numerous counties in southern and western Minnesota which were declared disaster areas due to the flooding.  They included Chippewa, Lac Qui Parle, Martin, and Yellow Medicine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As temperatures started to rise, a recent heavy rainfall event on the 12th and 13th, the snowpack ripened and began melting. This caused the Sand Creek in Jordan to develop an ice jam from the rapid melt. This also led to the evacuation of some residents in the Valley Green Mobile Home Park, in Jordan along this creek. About 1,000 people were affected by the evacuation which caused water to flow over the bank and onto the property. In addition, the Minnesota River along the western and northern county line had numerous road closures due to flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow melt water brought flooding to the Mustinka River and 12-Mile Creek in west central Minnesota in late March. The water rose to just above to nearly 2.6 feet above flood stage from Wheaton to above Wheaton on the creek and river.
Snow melt flooding occurred on the Minnesota River at the end of March. The river rose to almost a foot above the flood stage of 10 feet to 11 feet at Ortonville. The flooding continued into April.
Above normal snow melt water brought some flooding to west central Minnesota. Many roads along with crop land were flooded.
Above normal snow melt water brought some flooding to west central Minnesota. Many roads along with crop land were flooded.

A large ridge of Arctic high pressure engulfed most of the northern one-third of the United States. As temperatures fell well below zero during the morning of Sunday, March 3rd, wind chill values dropped below -35F for several areas of west central, and central Minnesota. Wind chill values dropped as low as -47F at Appleton airport in west central Minnesota Sunday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both the Appleton and Benson airports measured wind chill values below -35F for several hours during the morning of Sunday, March 3rd. The coldest value was -47F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two storm systems, one over the northern Rockies, and another moving out into the southern Plains, merged over the central United States the weekend of March 9-10th. ||Initially, warmer air surged northward across the Upper Midwest and allowed for mostly rain and sleet at the beginning of the event across southern Minnesota, and into portions of west central Wisconsin. Some heavy snow was reported during the afternoon before the snow tapered off early Sunday morning. Snowfall totals of around 6 to 8 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The snow took longer to melt on the Minnesota side of the northern Red River Valley, than it did in the southern Red River Valley. At the National Weather Service office in Grand Forks, the snow depth fell to zero on April 6th. However, a blizzard from April 10th to 12th brought more snow to the area. This snow finally melted by April 15th. As the snow melted, it flooded low lying areas and roads. Some sections of land pooled with water from the snow melt, but more so closer to the Red River. In the city of East Grand Forks, the point bridge and the Sorlie bridge were closed due to high water in the Red River.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and recent heavy rainfall caused the Minnesota River in Dakota County to rise above major stage. The stage rose to the point that the automated river gauge stopped reporting as it was underwater. In addition, Fort Snelling State Park was closed for a long duration due to the flooding along the Minnesota River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding that began in late March along the Mississippi River continued into and in some cases, through the month of April. Flooding occurred the entire month at Wabasha (Wabasha County) and Winona (Winona County). At Lake City (Wabasha County) the flooding temporarily ended in the middle of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding that began in late March along the Mississippi River in Wabasha, continued through the entire month of April. The river first crested around three and half feet above the flood stage on April 1st. The river crested a second time just over three feet above the flood stage on April 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the melting snowpack caused some southeast Minnesota river flooding to occur in late March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from melting snow caused flooding to occur along Turtle Creek near Austin. The river crested just under a foot above the flood stage at 11.31 feet on March 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow fell across southeast Minnesota from March 9th into the 10th with accumulations generally in the 2 to 4 inch range. The weight of this snow on top of the copious amounts that fell in February was too much for some buildings to withstand. In Winona (Winona County), the roof over a pool at a hotel collapsed along with part of the roof at a factory. Near Plainview (Wabasha County), a pole barn used to store collectible cars collapsed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow fell across Winona County from March 9th into the 10th with accumulations of 2 to 3 inches. The weight of this snow, along with the copious amounts that fell in February, caused the roof over a hotel pool and part of a factory roof to collapse in Winona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic late-season, multi-day winter storm developed rapidly across the central Plains on Wednesday, April 10. This system spiraled several periods of precipitation through the region. The leading precipitation on April 10 was a mix of rain, freezing rain and sleet from far eastern South Dakota through the Interstate 90 areas of southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, changing to snow to the north and west. There were even a few elevated thunderstorms which occurred overnight on April 10-11 and produced some near severe hail, which actually occurred after some initial wintry precipitation. Significant icing was centered around 30 miles either side of the Interstate 90 corridor, with as much as a third to three-quarters inch ice accumulation. ||Heavy snowfall developed from the early morning of April 11 and continued into the early morning of April 12 across much of southeast South Dakota into northern portions of southwest Minnesota, which accumulated to 1 to 2 feet during the storm. The most persistent snowfall occurred for areas west to north of Sioux Falls, but even there, a brief period of freezing rain or sleet occurred at some point ahead of the main upper trough passage. ||The other aspect of this storm was the intense winds, which gusted at the peak from 40 to 60 mph, producing widespread blizzard conditions across southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota. Considerable power outages occurred as transmission lines succumbed to the ice accumulation and wind.  At the peak, electric companies and cooperatives estimated as many as 25,000 customers were without power.  Many areas were without power for a day or two due to the intensity of the storm. ||In fact, the strong winds lofted dust from the Texas/Oklahoma panhandles which caused some of the snowfall toward the latter portions of the event to fall with a brownish color.  Ironically, the blizzard postponed the statewide tornado warning drill which was a part of Minnesota Severe Weather Awareness Week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulated to one-half to three quarters of an inch. As winds increased during the night of April 10 into early April 11 with gusts reaching 45 to 50 mph at times, power poles and transmission lines bore the brunt of the worst conditions. Over 1000 power poles were damaged and several hundred miles of transmission lines downed by the weight of the ice accumulation and stress of the high winds. Many roads were closed, including U.S. Highway 71, State Highway 86, and Interstate 90 due to power structures across roadways. Damage costs are rough estimates due to utility costs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow fell across southeast Minnesota from March 9th into the 10th with accumulations generally in the 2 to 4 inch range. The weight of this snow on top of the copious amounts that fell in February was too much for some buildings to withstand. In Winona (Winona County), the roof over a pool at a hotel collapsed along with part of the roof at a factory. Near Plainview (Wabasha County), a pole barn used to store collectible cars collapsed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow fell across Wabasha County from March 9th into the 10th with accumulations of 2 to 4 inches. The weight of this snow, along with the copious amounts that fell in February, caused a pole barn to collapse near Plainview. The barn was used to store collectible cars and an RV.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of rainfall and a melting snowpack, caused some flooding to occur across Wabasha County. There were numerous roads with debris on them that led to car accidents. One road was closed in the city of Wabasha and a mudslide occurred along County Highway 4 near the intersection with State Highway 60 north of Theilman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred across parts of Wabasha County from the combination of rainfall and melting snow. A mudslide occurred on County Road 4 near the intersection with State Highway 60 north of Theilman. A road was closed because of flooding in the city of Wabasha and several accidents occurred as a result of debris on roadways.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from rain and melting snow pushed Dobbins Creek out of its banks near Austin. The creek crested over a foot above the flood stage at 12.75 feet on March 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two storm systems, one over the northern Rockies, and another moving out into the southern Plains, merged over the central United States the weekend of March 9-10th. ||Initially, warmer air surged northward across the Upper Midwest and allowed for mostly rain and sleet at the beginning of the event across southern Minnesota, and into portions of west central Wisconsin. The warm layer held through most of Saturday, March 9th, before changing over to snow that evening. Farther to the west, and north, or across west central, and central Minnesota, enough cold air kept the precipitation in the form of snow. This is where the heaviest snow fell, with locally a foot in west central Minnesota before it tapered off Sunday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather information, including the general public and trained spotters, had indicated light rain and snow developing late Saturday afternoon March 9th. There were several hours of a mixture of snow and rain, before changing over to snow. Some heavy snow was reported. Snowfall totals of around 5 to 7 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were many factors that led to a severe Spring flood melt along the Minnesota and Mississippi Rivers and several of their tributaries during the last two weeks of March. The first factor was above average snowpack, especially over the Minnesota River Valley for mid March. Additionally, the added effect of a cold late Winter kept this snowpack deeper into the early Spring. Once temperatures warmed, and snow began to melt, a few storms, especially around the 12-13th of March, led to additional water on the already heavy snowpack. As area rivers, streams and creeks began to thaw, ice jams developed, which led to more areal flooding beyond the flooded river beds. ||Area streams, creeks, and mainstem rivers started to break up their Winter ice the second week of March, with a sharp rise in these basins during the three week sof March as the storm system around the 12th and 13th of March led to the ripening of the snowpack. Although most of the mainstem rivers along the Minnesota River crested the last week of March, flood waters continued into April. The Mississippi River crested after the last week of March, from St. Paul and points downstream.||During the third week of March, the Minnesota Department of Transportation started to close roads along mainstem rivers. County roads were already impacted as flood waters started to spread out across counties from melted snow and recent rainfall from storms.||There were numerous counties in southern and western Minnesota which were declared disaster areas due to the flooding.  They included Chippewa, Lac Qui Parle, Martin, and Yellow Medicine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed for several days due to snow melt, and the flood waters that develop from it. Rapidly runoff led to impassable roads, especially along streams and creeks along county roads.

This system spiraled several periods of precipitation through the region. The leading precipitation on April 10 was a mix of rain, freezing rain and sleet from far eastern South Dakota through the Interstate 90 areas of southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, changing to snow to the north and west. There were even a few elevated thunderstorms which occurred overnight on April 10-11 and produced some near severe hail, which actually occurred after some initial wintry precipitation. Significant icing was centered around 30 miles either side of the Interstate 90 corridor, with as much as a third to three-quarters inch ice accumulation. ||Heavy snowfall developed from the early morning of April 11 and continued into the early morning of April 12 across much of southeast South Dakota into northern portions of southwest Minnesota, which accumulated to 1 to 2 feet during the storm. The most persistent snowfall occurred for areas west to north of Sioux Falls, but even there, a brief period of freezing rain or sleet occurred at some point ahead of the main upper trough passage. ||The other aspect of this storm was the intense winds, which gusted at the peak from 40 to 60 mph, producing widespread blizzard conditions across southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota. Considerable power outages occurred as transmission lines succumbed to the ice accumulation and wind.  At the peak, electric companies and cooperatives estimated as many as 25,000 customers were without power.  Many areas were without power for a day or two due to the intensity of the storm. ||In fact, the strong winds lofted dust from the Texas/Oklahoma panhandles which caused some of the snowfall toward the latter portions of the event to fall with a brownish color. As winds increased during the night of April 10 into early April 11 with gusts reaching 45 to 50 mph at times, power poles and transmission lines bore the brunt of the worst conditions. Several hundred power poles were snapped by the weight of the ice accumulation and stress of the high winds, with more than 75 miles of power line down.  Many roads were closed, including U.S. Highway 59, due to power structures across roadways. Damage costs are rough estimates due to utility costs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter snow melted first across portions of Grant, Wilkin, and Clay counties. Visible satellite imagery confirmed this fact, showing a distinct snow free north to south band that extended roughly 20 or so miles east of the Red River in these three counties. In Fargo, the snow depth fell to zero on April 6th. However, a blizzard from April 10th to 12th brought more snow to the area. This snow finally melted by April 16th. As the snow melted, it flooded low lying areas and roads. Numerous sections of land pooled with water from the snow melt. Conditions were particularly bad from Campbell to Sabin, where drone footage showed many sections of land under water. Norman County was also hit hard. Highway 75 was closed due to high water south of Halstad. High resolution satellite imagery, drone footage, and vehicle reconnaissance showed numerous flooded sections of land over central and western Norman County. In Moorhead, the 12th avenue north and north Broadway bridges were closed due to high water in the Red River.

Storms intensified during the mid afternoon hours, as temperatures climbed into the mid to upper 80s with dew points in the 70s. These storms continued through the early evening hours, producing large hail, damaging wind gusts, and very heavy rain. Clusters of storms tracked through the Wahpeton-Breckenridge and the Fergus Falls areas. These storms moved fairly slowly, which resulted in the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large hail fell with strong winds.

By the early evening of July 3rd, a stationary boundary set up from near Oakes, North Dakota, to near Bemidji, Minnesota. The boundary was mainly a wind shift line, with south winds to the south of the boundary, and north winds to the north of the boundary. Thunderstorms broke out along the boundary, and also to the north of the boundary. The storms were slow movers, tracking slowly to the east-northeast. These storms mainly produced large hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was viewed and photographed from multiple locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong early spring storm system produced snow, sleet and freezing rain across southeast Minnesota from April 10th into the 11th. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 3 to 7 inches. Strong east winds brought down power poles and lines where enough freezing rain occurred to coat exposed surfaces. Around 100 power poles were downed across Mower County and the entire town of Rushford (Fillmore County) lost power. The highest reported snowfall total was 11 inches on the northeast side of Rochester (Olmsted County). The highest recorded wind gust was 56 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport. The Governor of Minnesota declared a state  of emergency and ordered the National Guard to assist as needed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 5 to 11 inches of snow fell across Olmsted County. In addition to the snow, a period of sleet and freezing rain occurred that coated exposed surfaces with a glaze of ice. With the coating of ice, power poles and lines were brought down by the strong winds across the southern and eastern sections of the county. The highest reported snowfall total was 11 inches in the northeast part of Rochester. The highest recorded wind gust was 56 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport. Lightning struck a tree on the northeast side of Rochester, ruptured a gas line that started the tree on fire with the remains of the tree falling on a house, breaking a window.
