EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding that began in late March along the Mississippi River continued into and in some cases, through the month of April. Flooding occurred the entire month at Wabasha (Wabasha County) and Winona (Winona County). At Lake City (Wabasha County) the flooding temporarily ended in the middle of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding that began in late March along the Mississippi River in Lake City, continued into the middle of April. The river crested about a foot and a half above the flood stage at 17.63 feet on April 1st.

The Governor of Minnesota declared a state  of emergency and ordered the National Guard to assist as needed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that around 5 inches of snow fell across Dodge County. In addition to the snow, a period of sleet and freezing rain occurred that coated exposed surfaces with a glaze of ice. With the glaze of ice, some power poles and lines were brought down across the southern part of the county by the strong winds causing around 4,000 people to lose power. At least three county roads were closed because of downed power lines. A countywide state of emergency was declared with shelters opened for those without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early afternoon of Friday, July 19th, a weak cold front pushed to a Hallock, Minnesota, to Carrington, North Dakota line. Afternoon temperatures peaked around 80 degrees along the front, with dew points in the mid 60s. However, further south, near Alexandria, Minnesota, temperatures were close to 90 degrees with dew points in the upper 70s. Thunderstorm activity broke out along the North Dakota, South Dakota, and Minnesota boundary region and expanded into west central Minnesota, closest to the best heat and humidity. These storms produced large hail.

A strong early April Colorado Low took shape over the Central Plains late Wednesday afternoon, April 10th. The surface low tracked nearly straight northward on Thursday, April 11th, reaching the Sioux Falls, South Dakota, area late in the afternoon. As it lifted northward, bands of heavy snow spread into southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota. Some of these bands of snow produced snow rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour. Generally most of this area received 6 to 12 inches of snow, but the Havana, North Dakota, and Bemidji, Minnesota, areas received 14 to 18 inches.

A strong early spring storm system produced snow, sleet and freezing rain across southeast Minnesota from April 10th into the 11th. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 3 to 7 inches. Strong east winds brought down power poles and lines where enough freezing rain occurred to coat exposed surfaces. Around 100 power poles were downed across Mower County and the entire town of Rushford (Fillmore County) lost power. The highest reported snowfall total was 11 inches on the northeast side of Rochester (Olmsted County). The highest recorded wind gust was 56 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport. The Governor of Minnesota declared a state  of emergency and ordered the National Guard to assist as needed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 5 and 6 inches of snow fell across Fillmore County. In addition to the snow, a period of sleet and freezing rain occurred that coated exposed surfaces with a glaze of ice. With the glaze of ice, some power poles and lines were brought down by the strong winds causing the entire town of Rushford to lose power.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large tree branches were snapped along Dicks Parkway, near Gates Corner, between 11 and 12 miles north of Fourtown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms broke out over the Lake of the Woods region during the mid to late afternoon of July 17th. These storms produced several tornadoes, damaging winds, and a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado snapped multiple poplar, spruce, and tamarac trees, leaving a 50 yard wide gouge in the forest and across Faunce road south. The tornado tracked intermittently for roughly 15 miles to the east-northeast. This area is deep in the Beltrami Island State Forest, which is largely inaccessible. However, similar tree snap areas were noted across Pilot Glade road, about 10 miles south of Pitt and across county road 1 about 5 miles north of Carp. Peak winds were estimated at 105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From Thursday night into Friday, April 12th, the low finally turned northeast and tracked toward Duluth. Wind speeds were not intensely strong during this blizzard event, as most sites only gusted into the 40 to 45 mph range. This event did occur during the spring snowmelt flood, when many rivers and ditches were filled with water. This brought an additional threat of vehicles sliding off slippery roads into water filled ditches. Interstate 29 was closed in eastern North Dakota and Interstate 94 was closed from Jamestown, North Dakota, to Osakis, Minnesota. Many other roads were either closed or had travel not recommended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the early evening of July 3rd, a stationary boundary set up from near Oakes, North Dakota, to near Bemidji, Minnesota. The boundary was mainly a wind shift line, with south winds to the south of the boundary, and north winds to the north of the boundary. Thunderstorms broke out along the boundary, and also to the north of the boundary. The storms were slow movers, tracking slowly to the east-northeast. These storms mainly produced large hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail fell along Division Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed along a cold front over northwest Minnesota during the afternoon of July 25th. One particularly strong cell produced quarter sized hail southeast of Roseau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was mostly pea to dime size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By 4 pm on May 15th, temperatures across eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota had risen to the 70s and low 80s. A weak area of surface low pressure was located near Minot, with a cold front extending south-southwest toward Hettinger. Surface dew points were quite dry ahead of the cold front, generally ranging from the mid 20s to mid 30s. However, along the cold front, they did rise into the 40s to low 50s. A broken line of thunderstorms formed along the front and tracked to the east-southeast (into northwest Minnesota) through the evening. These storms produced numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds. The last warning expired at midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two empty hopper bottomed seed bins were blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vertically stacked low pressure system pushed into eastern North Dakota during the afternoon of May 24th. The 4 pm surface chart showed the low near Bismarck, with a boundary extending to the east along the Interstate 94 corridor. Temperatures were on the cool side, with upper 40s near the Lake of the Woods to upper 60s along the North and South Dakota border in southeast North Dakota. Two areas of thunderstorms initially formed in the warm sector in North Dakota, one area over Stutsman County and the second mainly over Ransom and Sargent counties. Both areas moved to the northeast during the early evening and produced tornadoes, funnel clouds, and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent wall cloud with a brief funnel was noted by multiple spotters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado began in Norman County at 1822 CST. This was the first of twin tornadoes which were viewed and photographed from residents near Flom and Waubun. This tornado only tracked for a short distance in Norman County, before it continued into Mahnomen County. The tornado broke large tree branches in several shelterbelts along its path and appears to have caused a peak wind of 73 mph at both the Mahnomen airport and nearby MNDOT RWIS mesonet sensors as it dissipated. Peak winds were estimated at 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front, which had helped produce the severe weather during the evening of June 7th into the early morning hours of the 8th, finally pushed into the Red River Valley region during the afternoon of the 8th. Once again, thunderstorms broke out along and ahead of the boundary, producing damaging winds and large hail. After most of the storms had exited the area, another clump of storms developed and tracked through the central Red River Valley. One of these storms produced a tornado near Fertile, Minnesota.

Once again, thunderstorms broke out along and ahead of the boundary, producing damaging winds and large hail. After most of the storms had exited the area, another clump of storms developed and tracked through the central Red River Valley. One of these storms produced a tornado near Fertile, Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marble to quarter sized hail fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vertically stacked low pressure system pushed into eastern North Dakota during the afternoon of May 24th. The 4 pm surface chart showed the low near Bismarck, with a boundary extending to the east along the Interstate 94 corridor. Temperatures were on the cool side, with upper 40s near the Lake of the Woods to upper 60s along the North and South Dakota border in southeast North Dakota. Two areas of thunderstorms initially formed in the warm sector in North Dakota, one area over Stutsman County and the second mainly over Ransom and Sargent counties. Both areas moved to the northeast during the early evening and produced tornadoes, funnel clouds, and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the first of twin tornadoes which were viewed and photographed from residents near Flom and Waubun. This tornado only tracked for a short distance in Norman County, before it continued into Mahnomen County. The tornado broke large tree branches in several shelterbelts along its path and appears to have caused a peak wind of 73 mph at both the Mahnomen airport and nearby MNDOT RWIS mesonet sensors as it dissipated. Peak winds were estimated at 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado occurred in Freeborn County Minnesota on Memorial Day, May 27th, 2019.  The tornado touched down and dissipated north of Glenville, Minnesota which is located southeast of Albert Lea.  Approximately 100 trees were snapped or damaged, with trees falling on fences at two properties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey indicated that an EF-0 tornado touched down on the northern side of Glenville and tracked north-northwest.  It only lasted for about a minute, with a path of less than a mile and a max width of 50 yards. No structural damage was observed, but numerous trees were snapped or uprooted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen photographed a funnel cloud just west of Interstate 35 and County Road 85 and sent it to a broadcast meteorologist via social media. The photo was time stamped and had a specific location. It was not possible to determine whether the funnel cloud touched the ground, and there were no reports of any damage, so this was documented as a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bowing line of thunderstorms (bow echo) moved into the Devils Lake area from north central North Dakota just after sunrise. As the storms pushed east of Devils Lake, they tracked more southeast, staying mainly between highway 2 and Interstate 94 in eastern North Dakota through 1 pm. These storms held together as they passed into Clay County, Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were blown down at farmsteads across northern Humboldt Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms redeveloped over northwest Minnesota during the late evening of June 29th. Some back-building occurred, but generally the storms slowly built southward toward Bemidji through the very early morning hours of June 30th. There was a little break in the thunderstorm activity prior to sunrise, when more storms developed over west central Minnesota, mainly over Otter Tail, Wadena, and Grant counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter sized hail fell, along with strong winds and heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By 4 pm on May 15th, temperatures across eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota had risen to the 70s and low 80s. A weak area of surface low pressure was located near Minot, with a cold front extending south-southwest toward Hettinger. Surface dew points were quite dry ahead of the cold front, generally ranging from the mid 20s to mid 30s. However, along the cold front, they did rise into the 40s to low 50s. A broken line of thunderstorms formed along the front and tracked to the east-southeast (into northwest Minnesota) through the evening. These storms produced numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds. The last warning expired at midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail and strong winds swept across southern Grand Forks Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system was well south in the southern Mississippi River region the morning of Thursday February 7th. Over the Upper Midwest, there was an elongated, inverted trough that focused the precipitation across the Upper Midwest during the morning, and afternoon of Thursday February 7th. Once this system moved rapidly northeast over the Great Lakes region, and pressure rises occurred across the Upper Midwest, strong gusty winds developed and caused blizzard conditions in parts of Minnesota. ||The initial bands of precipitation developed over southern Minnesota during the predawn hours with a mixture of sleet, freezing rain and snow. There was already a coating of ice from a long duration of freezing drizzle the night before. The mixture of sleet, freezing rain, and snow quickly changed over to all snow after sunrise. ||Snowfall intensified in the morning, with amounts of 4 to 6 inches already reported in portions of central and east central Minnesota before noon. The snowfall continued through the afternoon, and tapered off in the evening. The heaviest snowfall occurred between 7 AM CST, to 2 PM CST. ||As winds began to increase during the afternoon, visibility dropped significantly in western Minnesota. By the mid to late afternoon, several roads closed as blowing snow kept snowplows off the road. In addition, the governor of Minnesota activated the National Guard for blizzard assistance in Renville County due to white-out conditions and stranded motorists.||Between 330 PM and 930 PM Thursday, February, 7th, the Minnesota State Patrol reported 149 crashes, 294 spin-outs and 19 jack knifed trucks in portions of west central, and northwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few reports from social media and the general public reported up to 7 inches of snow, that fell during the mid morning, through the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm system moved out of the Rockies, and into the Upper Midwest late Tuesday night, and into Wednesday, February 20th. Deep moisture was present during the onset of this storm, which allowed for much higher snowfall rates. There was also ample upper level jet energy that was coupled with mid-level forcing during the overnight and early morning hours of Wednesday February 20th. There was also presence of gulf moisture that interacted with the storm early in the development. The surface low was weak, and moved overhead across southern Minnesota during the morning of Wednesday, February 20th.||Snowfall amounts had already reached 6 inches in southwest Minnesota by 6 am Wednesday. During the morning, as snowfall was locally heavy at times, and snowfall rates were one to two inches per hour, amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches by noon from southwest Minnesota, northeast that including portions of the Twin Cities, and west central Wisconsin. Locally a foot, with isolated 15 inches, fell around New Ulm and Mankato before the snow tapered off in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trained weather observers, and the general public measured between 6 and 8 inches of snow that fell across the county. The highest measured report was 7.8 inches near Lake Lillian. The heaviest snowfall occurred during the morning with snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system that brought blizzard conditions to a wide area of southern and central Minnesota, as well as adjacent west central Wisconsin, developed early Saturday morning in the southern Rockies. The storm moved out into the Plains of Kansas Saturday morning, February 23th, and tracked northeast to northern Missouri by the afternoon. The storm kept steady during this time frame, but intensified rapidly during the evening and overnight period of Saturday and Sunday, February 23rd and 24th. The storm dropped 20 millibars from Saturday evening, through Sunday morning as it moved from northern Missouri, to the northern Great Lakes. ||Snow began in southern Minnesota Saturday afternoon February 23th, and ended Sunday morning. The heaviest snowfall occurred from south of Blue Earth (Faribault County), to Faribault, and Hastings in Minnesota, and to River Falls, and Rice Lake in west central Wisconsin. These areas received between 6 and 12 inches of snow, with localized areas of 12+ inches.||Roads began to close in south central Minnesota before 10 PM CST, Saturday, February 23rd. As winds increased during the night, and Sunday morning, several other roads, including I-35 & I-90 closed due to impassable conditions. The road closures extended northward across portions of southwest, west central and southeast Minnesota as winds increased and snow drifted over the road Sunday. Several social media pictures indicated that snow drifts were as high as 20 feet in some locations in southern Minnesota. Even military vehicles that are made for deep snow, became stuck.||Wind speeds started to increase late Saturday night, and peaked Sunday, and slowly subsided Sunday afternoon and evening as the storm system moved out of the region. However, some roads in south central Minnesota remained closed through Monday as snow plows reported drifts in excess of 20 feet.||During the height of the storm, the Minnesota Homeland Security and Emergency Management tallied approximately 600 stranded travelers who were rescued off of snow-filled ditches, drifted shut roads, and snowbanks. ||Troopers and the Minnesota Department of Transportation officials worked near 24 hours straight to get stranded motorists out of the Straight River rest stop along I-35 south of Owatonna. In Blue Earth County, 150 vehicles were reported off the roads, some were rescued. Seven vehicles were reported off the road in Red Wing. In Freeborn County, crews worked to rescue nine people from two trains stuck on tracks. As individuals were rescued, numerous shelters were opened across southern Minnesota. ||The following was the breakdown of the rescues across southern Minnesota;||Brown County: 5.|Chippewa County: 21.|Cottonwood County: 8.|Freeborn County: 91 (including from stranded trains).|Le Sueur County: 46.|Renville County: 33.|Rice County: 30. |Steele County: 200-plus. |Stevens County: 12. |Swift County: 20.|Watonwan County: 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather observations, including the Minnesota Department of Transportation, and local airport sensors, indicated blizzard conditions developing early Sunday morning, February 24th. The weather conditions deteriorated during the morning and afternoon, with a few roads closed, along with a few car accidents.  Winds were sustained over 30 mph, with gusts of 45 mph at times during the height of the storm. ||In addition, light to moderate snow fell Saturday evening, through early Sunday morning, led to storm totals of 3 to 5 inches across the county. There was a measured report of 5.2 inches near Miesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong clipper system dropped southeast across the region and was followed by an arctic front. Strong north winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph combined with new snowfall brought ground blizzard conditions to west central Minnesota. Travel was significantly disrupted or halted.

A strong clipper system dropped southeast across the region and was followed by an arctic front. Strong north winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph combined with new snowfall brought ground blizzard conditions to west central Minnesota. Travel was significantly disrupted or halted.

This snowfall event occurred in a small area due to a combination of elevated instability and mid-level forcing. ||At the surface, a large ridge of high pressure dominated the Upper Midwest during the morning of Tuesday, February, 5th. A dry and cold air mass was initially across the region, and most of the precipitation was not reaching the ground. As the forcing increased, regional radar noted banding in the reflectivity. The higher reflectivity promoted higher saturation rates and led to snow reaching the ground. By the late morning, and through the afternoon, surface observations noted low visibility and regional road conditions deteriorated rapidly as snowfall intensity rose to 1 to 1.5 inches per hour. ||The main band set up along and north of a line from Faribault, to Red Wing, to Eau Claire, Wisconsin. In this band, snowfall amounts  ranged from 7 to 10 inches. The heaviest occurred near Northfield, to Hastings in Minnesota, and from River Falls to Chippewa Falls in Wisconsin.||Additionally, a Beechcraft Super King Air 300 attempted to take off through falling snow but ice underneath caused the plane to slip off the side of the runway. There were no injuries to the four people on board. This happened at the Mankato airport in south central Minnesota around 200 PM CST, on February 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of 8 to 10 inches of snow fell across Rice County during the afternoon of Tuesday, February 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system was well south in the southern Mississippi River region the morning of Thursday February 7th. Over the Upper Midwest, there was an elongated, inverted trough that focused the precipitation across the Upper Midwest during the morning, and afternoon of Thursday February 7th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports from social media. trained spotters and the general public reported up to 8 inches of snow, that fell during the mid morning, through the afternoon. Some areas north of Morrison County received up to 8 inches of snow by 12 noon CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm system moved out of the Rockies, and into the Upper Midwest late Tuesday night, and into Wednesday, February 20th. Deep moisture was present during the onset of this storm, which allowed for much higher snowfall rates. There was also ample upper level jet energy that was coupled with mid-level forcing during the overnight and early morning hours of Wednesday February 20th. There was also presence of gulf moisture that interacted with the storm early in the development. The surface low was weak, and moved overhead across southern Minnesota during the morning of Wednesday, February 20th.||Snowfall amounts had already reached 6 inches in southwest Minnesota by 6 am Wednesday. During the morning, as snowfall was locally heavy at times, and snowfall rates were one to two inches per hour, amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches by noon from southwest Minnesota, northeast that including portions of the Twin Cities, and west central Wisconsin. Locally a foot, with isolated 15 inches, fell around New Ulm and Mankato before the snow tapered off in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trained weather observers, and the general public measured around 8 to 9 inches of snow that fell across the county. The highest amount measured was 8.5 inches in Darwin. The heaviest snowfall occurred during the morning with snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved across the Upper Midwest and brought a round of snow to southeast Minnesota from February 11th into the early morning hours of the 13th. The snow was heaviest along the Mississippi River where totals around a foot were common. Snowfall amounts ranged from around 5 inches up to 14 inches. The highest reported total was 14.5 inches near Goodview (Winona County) and in Caledonia (Houston County). As the storm exited the region, the winds increased and caused blowing and drifting snow to occur that resulted in hazardous travel conditions. Most schools were closed on February 12th including Winona State University and Minnesota State College-Southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 5 and 8 inches of snow fell across Dodge County. The highest reported total was 8.5 inches near Kasson. As the snow was ending, the winds increased and created blowing and drifting snow that resulted in hazardous travel conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed across Nicollet County during the afternoon as winds increased to 30 to 40 mph, and visibility dropped to one quarter of a mile or less for several hours. During the height of the blizzard, several roads were nearly impassable, or travel was not advised based on the Minnesota Department of Transportation web page.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm system moved out of the Rockies, and into the Upper Midwest late Tuesday night, and into Wednesday, February 20th. Deep moisture was present during the onset of this storm, which allowed for much higher snowfall rates. There was also ample upper level jet energy that was coupled with mid-level forcing during the overnight and early morning hours of Wednesday February 20th. There was also presence of gulf moisture that interacted with the storm early in the development. The surface low was weak, and moved overhead across southern Minnesota during the morning of Wednesday, February 20th.||Snowfall amounts had already reached 6 inches in southwest Minnesota by 6 am Wednesday. During the morning, as snowfall was locally heavy at times, and snowfall rates were one to two inches per hour, amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches by noon from southwest Minnesota, northeast that including portions of the Twin Cities, and west central Wisconsin. Locally a foot, with isolated 15 inches, fell around New Ulm and Mankato before the snow tapered off in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trained weather observers, and the general public measured around 8 to 12 inches of snow that fell across the county. The highest amount measured was 14.6 inches east of New Ulm. The heaviest snowfall occurred during the morning with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system that brought blizzard conditions to a wide area of southern and central Minnesota, as well as adjacent west central Wisconsin, developed early Saturday morning in the southern Rockies. The storm moved out into the Plains of Kansas Saturday morning, February 23th, and tracked northeast to northern Missouri by the afternoon. The storm kept steady during this time frame, but intensified rapidly during the evening and overnight period of Saturday and Sunday, February 23rd and 24th. The storm dropped 20 millibars from Saturday evening, through Sunday morning as it moved from northern Missouri, to the northern Great Lakes. ||Snow began in southern Minnesota Saturday afternoon February 23th, and ended Sunday morning. The heaviest snowfall occurred from south of Blue Earth (Faribault County), to Faribault, and Hastings in Minnesota, and to River Falls, and Rice Lake in west central Wisconsin. These areas received between 6 and 12 inches of snow, with localized areas of 12+ inches.||Roads began to close in south central Minnesota before 10 PM CST, Saturday, February 23rd. As winds increased during the night, and Sunday morning, several other roads, including I-35 & I-90 closed due to impassable conditions. The road closures extended northward across portions of southwest, west central and southeast Minnesota as winds increased and snow drifted over the road Sunday. Several social media pictures indicated that snow drifts were as high as 20 feet in some locations in southern Minnesota. Even military vehicles that are made for deep snow, became stuck.||Wind speeds started to increase late Saturday night, and peaked Sunday, and slowly subsided Sunday afternoon and evening as the storm system moved out of the region. However, some roads in south central Minnesota remained closed through Monday as snow plows reported drifts in excess of 20 feet.||During the height of the storm, the Minnesota Homeland Security and Emergency Management tallied approximately 600 stranded travelers who were rescued off of snow-filled ditches, drifted shut roads, and snowbanks. ||Troopers and the Minnesota Department of Transportation officials worked near 24 hours straight to get stranded motorists out of the Straight River rest stop along I-35 south of Owatonna. In Blue Earth County, 150 vehicles were reported off the roads, some were rescued. Seven vehicles were reported off the road in Red Wing. In Freeborn County, crews worked to rescue nine people from two trains stuck on tracks. As individuals were rescued, numerous shelters were opened across southern Minnesota. ||The following was the breakdown of the rescues across southern Minnesota;||Brown County: 5.|Chippewa County: 21.|Cottonwood County: 8.|Freeborn County: 91 (including from stranded trains).|Le Sueur County: 46.|Renville County: 33.|Rice County: 30. |Steele County: 200-plus. |Stevens County: 12. |Swift County: 20.|Watonwan County: 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather observations, including the Minnesota Department of Transportation, and local airport sensors, indicated blizzard conditions developing late Sunday morning, February 24th. The weather conditions deteriorated during the afternoon with a few car accidents. Winds were sustained over 30 mph, with gusts of 40 mph at times during the height of the storm. ||In addition, light snow fell Saturday evening, through early Sunday morning, led to storm totals of 1 to 3 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved across the Upper Midwest and brought a round of snow to southeast Minnesota from February 11th into the early morning hours of the 13th. The snow was heaviest along the Mississippi River where totals around a foot were common. Snowfall amounts ranged from around 5 inches up to 14 inches. The highest reported total was 14.5 inches near Goodview (Winona County) and in Caledonia (Houston County). As the storm exited the region, the winds increased and caused blowing and drifting snow to occur that resulted in hazardous travel conditions. Most schools were closed on February 12th including Winona State University and Minnesota State College-Southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 9 and 14 inches of snow fell across Houston County. The highest reported total was 14.5 inches in Caledonia. As the snow was ending, the winds increased and created blowing and drifting snow that resulted in hazardous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures warmed into the 40s over the first few days of February which was enough to allow the ice to break up on some rivers. The ice break up created an ice jam on the Root River in and around Houston (Houston County) that lead to some minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Root River caused flooding to occur in and around Houston. The ice jam formed after temperatures warmed into the 40s during the early part of February. The river crested almost a foot above the flood stage at 15.74 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather observations, including the Minnesota Department of Transportation, and local airport sensors, indicated blizzard conditions developing late Sunday morning, February 24th. Some roads became impassable during the afternoon as significant drifts developed. Winds were sustained over 30 mph, with occasional gusts of 40 mph at times during the height of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system that brought blizzard conditions to a wide area of southern and central Minnesota, as well as adjacent west central Wisconsin, developed early Saturday morning in the southern Rockies. The storm moved out into the Plains of Kansas Saturday morning, February 23th, and tracked northeast to northern Missouri by the afternoon. The storm kept steady during this time frame, but intensified rapidly during the evening and overnight period of Saturday and Sunday, February 23rd and 24th. The storm dropped 20 millibars from Saturday evening, through Sunday morning as it moved from northern Missouri, to the northern Great Lakes. ||Snow began in southern Minnesota Saturday afternoon February 23th, and ended Sunday morning. The heaviest snowfall occurred from south of Blue Earth (Faribault County), to Faribault, and Hastings in Minnesota, and to River Falls, and Rice Lake in west central Wisconsin. These areas received between 6 and 12 inches of snow, with localized areas of 12+ inches.||Roads began to close in south central Minnesota before 10 PM CST, Saturday, February 23rd. As winds increased during the night, and Sunday morning, several other roads, including I-35 & I-90 closed due to impassable conditions. The road closures extended northward across portions of southwest, west central and southeast Minnesota as winds increased and snow drifted over the road Sunday. Several social media pictures indicated that snow drifts were as high as 20 feet in some locations in southern Minnesota. Even military vehicles that are made for deep snow, became stuck.||Wind speeds started to increase late Saturday night, and peaked Sunday, and slowly subsided Sunday afternoon and evening as the storm system moved out of the region. However, some roads in south central Minnesota remained closed through Monday as snow plows reported drifts in excess of 20 feet.||During the height of the storm, the Minnesota Homeland Security and Emergency Management tallied approximately 600 stranded travelers who were rescued off of snow-filled ditches, drifted shut roads, and snowbanks. ||Troopers and the Minnesota Department of Transportation officials worked near 24 hours straight to get stranded motorists out of the Straight River rest stop along I-35 south of Owatonna. In Blue Earth County, 150 vehicles were reported off the roads, some were rescued. Seven vehicles were reported off the road in Red Wing. In Freeborn County, crews worked to rescue nine people from two trains stuck on tracks. As individuals were rescued, numerous shelters were opened across southern Minnesota. ||The following was the breakdown of the rescues across southern Minnesota;||Brown County: 5.|Chippewa County: 21.|Cottonwood County: 8.|Freeborn County: 91 (including from stranded trains).|Le Sueur County: 46.|Renville County: 33.|Rice County: 30. |Steele County: 200-plus. |Stevens County: 12. |Swift County: 20.|Watonwan County: 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved across the Upper Midwest and brought a round of snow to southeast Minnesota from February 11th into the early morning hours of the 13th. The snow was heaviest along the Mississippi River where totals around a foot were common. Snowfall amounts ranged from around 5 inches up to 14 inches. The highest reported total was 14.5 inches near Goodview (Winona County) and in Caledonia (Houston County). As the storm exited the region, the winds increased and caused blowing and drifting snow to occur that resulted in hazardous travel conditions. Most schools were closed on February 12th including Winona State University and Minnesota State College-Southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 8 and 10 inches of snow fell across Wabasha County. The highest reported total was 10.5 inches near Wabasha. As the snow was ending, the winds increased and created blowing and drifting snow that resulted in hazardous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a winter storm moved across the Upper Midwest on February 6th and 7th, it brought multiple different types of precipitation to southeast Minnesota. The precipitation started out as a mixture of sleet and snow on the 6th before switching to freezing drizzle. Only a light glazing occurred from the freezing drizzle before snow developed again during the morning of the 7th. This snow was then heavy at times with portions of southeast Minnesota receiving 5 to 9 inches. The highest reported total was 9 inches near Zumbro Falls (Wabasha County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 5 and 9 inches of snow fell across Wabasha County. Preceding the snow was a period of sleet and snow during the evening of the 6th followed by light freezing drizzle into the morning of the 7th. The highest reported snowfall amount was 9 inches near Zumbro Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system was well south in the southern Mississippi River region the morning of Thursday February 7th. Over the Upper Midwest, there was an elongated, inverted trough that focused the precipitation across the Upper Midwest during the morning, and afternoon of Thursday February 7th. Once this system moved rapidly northeast over the Great Lakes region, and pressure rises occurred across the Upper Midwest, strong gusty winds developed and caused blizzard conditions in parts of Minnesota. ||The initial bands of precipitation developed over southern Minnesota during the predawn hours with a mixture of sleet, freezing rain and snow. There was already a coating of ice from a long duration of freezing drizzle the night before. The mixture of sleet, freezing rain, and snow quickly changed over to all snow after sunrise. ||Snowfall intensified in the morning, with amounts of 4 to 6 inches already reported in portions of central and east central Minnesota before noon. The snowfall continued through the afternoon, and tapered off in the evening. The heaviest snowfall occurred between 7 AM CST, to 2 PM CST. ||As winds began to increase during the afternoon, visibility dropped significantly in western Minnesota. By the mid to late afternoon, several roads closed as blowing snow kept snowplows off the road. In addition, the governor of Minnesota activated the National Guard for blizzard assistance in Renville County due to white-out conditions and stranded motorists.||Between 330 PM and 930 PM Thursday, February, 7th, the Minnesota State Patrol reported 149 crashes, 294 spin-outs and 19 jack knifed trucks in portions of west central, and northwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports from social media, trained spotters, and the general public, reported up to 9 inches of snow, that fell during the mid morning, through the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm system moved out of the Rockies, and into the Upper Midwest late Tuesday night, and into Wednesday, February 20th. Deep moisture was present during the onset of this storm, which allowed for much higher snowfall rates. There was also ample upper level jet energy that was coupled with mid-level forcing during the overnight and early morning hours of Wednesday February 20th. There was also presence of gulf moisture that interacted with the storm early in the development. The surface low was weak, and moved overhead across southern Minnesota during the morning of Wednesday, February 20th.||Snowfall amounts had already reached 6 inches in southwest Minnesota by 6 am Wednesday. During the morning, as snowfall was locally heavy at times, and snowfall rates were one to two inches per hour, amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches by noon from southwest Minnesota, northeast that including portions of the Twin Cities, and west central Wisconsin. Locally a foot, with isolated 15 inches, fell around New Ulm and Mankato before the snow tapered off in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trained weather observers, and the general public measured around 8 to 11 inches of snow that fell across the county. The highest amount measured was 11.0 inches in the city of Webster. The heaviest snowfall occurred during the morning with snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted into the Northland on the 2nd with an area of low pressure then moving along the front into the next day. This led to a long duration event across the area. Snow fell across area with the highest amounts being reported in the northern reaches of the forecast area. Unfortunately, snowfall reports were hard to come by where the heaviest snow was seen on radar due to it being in a very low population area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall report of 8 was received from a trained spotter in Crane Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from southern Minnesota into northern Wisconsin the 6th into the 7th. This track set up an inverted trough and deformation zone across much of the forecast area with widespread heavy snowfall reported. Multiple waves of heavy snowfall occurred along with areas of freezing drizzle in between waves. Most locations saw between 8 and 12 of snow, although the tip of the Minnesota Arrowhead saw less than 6 of snow as they were further away from the low.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported snowfall of 11.0 7 miles WSW of Pine River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of high pressure set up to the north of the CWA in southern Canada. This set up easterly flow across western Lake Superior. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from eastern Kansas into southwestern Wisconsin May 8 into May 9. An area of snow along an inverted trough from the low spread across northeast Minnesota the night of the 8th. With this system arriving overnight, the warm May sun did not play a role in keeping snowfall accumulations down and snow was able to rapidly accumulate. However, most of the snow then melted during the day on the 9th with only an inch remaining on the ground by the next morning. Snowfall totals of 8 to 12 inches were seen in the Twin Ports area with the Gary New Duluth area seeing 12 inches, Wales receiving 11.5 inches and the NWS office seeing 10.6 inches of accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm dumped 4 to 14 inches of snow across southeast Minnesota from February 23rd into the 24th. As the snow was winding down, the winds increased with sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph common along with gusts of 45 to 55 mph. This created blizzard conditions for much of the 24th with major drifting, white-outs and numerous road closures, including Interstate 90 across Winona, Olmsted and Mower Counties. The weight of the accumulated snow caused several roofs to collapse. Three families were displaced from their mobile homes in Winona County where at least 3 barns also collapsed killing around 14 cows. Businesses in Winona (Winona County) and Dodge Center (Dodge County) lost sections of building roofs. A large pole shed collapse at a business in Oronoco (Olmsted County). In southeast Olmsted County, a barn collapsed killing 10 cows and prompting the owners to sell the rest of their herd and leave the dairy business. Some dairy farms had to dump milk when trucks were unable to make scheduled pick ups and one farm near Lanesboro (Fillmore County) dumped between 6 and 7 thousand gallons. Between 200 and 250 stranded motorists were rescued across southeast Minnesota. Several snowmobile clubs were called upon to utilize trail groomers to help rescue people. The Governor of Minnesota declared a state of emergency allowing the National Guard to be mobilized to help in the rescue efforts. In Wabasha County, a military tank also became stuck in the snow attempting to rescue stranded motorists. In Dodge County, around 100 stranded motorists were rescued and 30 to 40 people were housed in emergency shelters. Olmsted County declared a state of emergency during the storm. The effects of the blizzard continued into the 25th when most schools were closed. The highest reported snowfall amount was 14.3 inches near Rochester (Olmsted County) and the highest recorded wind gust was 58 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 11 to 13 inches of snow fell across Dodge County. As the snow was ending, winds increased to 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts that created major drifting, white-outs and road closures, including State Highway 56. Around 100 stranded motorists were rescued and 65 to 70 people were housed in emergency shelters. The weight of the accumulated snow caused part of a roof to collapse at a business in Dodge Center. Fire trucks responding to a fire became stuck in the snow and had to be extricated with a front end loader. The impacts from the blizzard conditions caused schools to be closed on February 25th. The highest reported snowfall total was 13 inches near Kasson. The highest recorded wind gust was 51 mph by a mesonet station near Kasson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm system moved out of the Rockies, and into the Upper Midwest late Tuesday night, and into Wednesday, February 20th. Deep moisture was present during the onset of this storm, which allowed for much higher snowfall rates. There was also ample upper level jet energy that was coupled with mid-level forcing during the overnight and early morning hours of Wednesday February 20th. There was also presence of gulf moisture that interacted with the storm early in the development. Locally a foot, with isolated 15 inches, fell around New Ulm and Mankato before the snow tapered off in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few reports from Faribault County had around 6 to 7 inches of snow by the mid morning. Most of the heavy snow tapered off by the late morning, with mainly flurries in the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system that brought blizzard conditions to a wide area of southern and central Minnesota, as well as adjacent west central Wisconsin, developed early Saturday morning in the southern Rockies. The storm moved out into the Plains of Kansas Saturday morning, February 23th, and tracked northeast to northern Missouri by the afternoon. The storm kept steady during this time frame, but intensified rapidly during the evening and overnight period of Saturday and Sunday, February 23rd and 24th. The storm dropped 20 millibars from Saturday evening, through Sunday morning as it moved from northern Missouri, to the northern Great Lakes. ||Snow began in southern Minnesota Saturday afternoon February 23th, and ended Sunday morning. The heaviest snowfall occurred from south of Blue Earth (Faribault County), to Faribault, and Hastings in Minnesota, and to River Falls, and Rice Lake in west central Wisconsin. These areas received between 6 and 12 inches of snow, with localized areas of 12+ inches.||Roads began to close in south central Minnesota before 10 PM CST, Saturday, February 23rd. As winds increased during the night, and Sunday morning, several other roads, including I-35 & I-90 closed due to impassable conditions. The road closures extended northward across portions of southwest, west central and southeast Minnesota as winds increased and snow drifted over the road Sunday. Several social media pictures indicated that snow drifts were as high as 20 feet in some locations in southern Minnesota. Even military vehicles that are made for deep snow, became stuck.||Wind speeds started to increase late Saturday night, and peaked Sunday, and slowly subsided Sunday afternoon and evening as the storm system moved out of the region. However, some roads in south central Minnesota remained closed through Monday as snow plows reported drifts in excess of 20 feet.||During the height of the storm, the Minnesota Homeland Security and Emergency Management tallied approximately 600 stranded travelers who were rescued off of snow-filled ditches, drifted shut roads, and snowbanks. ||Troopers and the Minnesota Department of Transportation officials worked near 24 hours straight to get stranded motorists out of the Straight River rest stop along I-35 south of Owatonna. In Blue Earth County, 150 vehicles were reported off the roads, some were rescued. Seven vehicles were reported off the road in Red Wing. In Freeborn County, crews worked to rescue nine people from two trains stuck on tracks. As individuals were rescued, numerous shelters were opened across southern Minnesota. ||The following was the breakdown of the rescues across southern Minnesota;||Brown County: 5.|Chippewa County: 21.|Cottonwood County: 8.|Freeborn County: 91 (including from stranded trains).|Le Sueur County: 46.|Renville County: 33.|Rice County: 30. |Steele County: 200-plus. |Stevens County: 12. |Swift County: 20.|Watonwan County: 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather observations, including the Minnesota Department of Transportation, and local airport sensors, indicated blizzard conditions developing early Sunday morning, February 24th. The weather conditions deteriorated during the morning, with numerous roads closed, including I-35 in the county. During the height of the storm, people who ventured outdoors, became stuck and disoriented, and had to be rescued.  Winds were sustained over 30 mph, with gusts of 50 mph at times during the height of the storm. ||In addition, heavy snowfall Saturday afternoon, through early Sunday morning, led to storm totals of 8 to 12 inches across the county. There was a measured report of 11.5 inches near Albert Lea.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across northern Minnesota led to a few thunderstorms, most of which were sub-severe. One storm was able to produce marginally severe hail along with heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to quarter size hail was falling along with heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms redeveloped over northwest Minnesota during the late evening of June 29th. Some back-building occurred, but generally the storms slowly built southward toward Bemidji through the very early morning hours of June 30th. There was a little break in the thunderstorm activity prior to sunrise, when more storms developed over west central Minnesota, mainly over Otter Tail, Wadena, and Grant counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a weak mid-level wave moved across the area, a few storms fired along a frontal boundary. While the environment featured 1500-2000 J/kg MLCAPE, the deep layer shear was 20 knots or less, and this resulted in most storms weakening fairly rapidly after development. Isolated storms ended up with strong enough updrafts to produce hail.

Thunderstorms that developed across west central Minnesota during the morning of June 30th, became severe near Mille Lacs Lake where reports of downed trees occurred near Vineland. Also, a few wind gusts downed trees near Rush City. There were also a few wind gusts of 50 to 55 mph that occurred south of St. Cloud from near South Haven, to Annandale, and Maple Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mille Lacs Band of Ojibwe Tribal Emergency Management showed a few trees that were blown down in the city of Onamia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening of Sunday, June 30th, several storms merged in west central Minnesota and developed a bow echo segment in Redwood, and Renville Counties. As the storms merged, the bow echo surged eastward causing wind damage and a few severe wind gusts as it moved from near Redwood Falls, and Olivia, eastward toward the southern part of the Twin Cities metro area before decaying in west central Wisconsin. There was a measured wind gust of 53 mph at the Olivia airport in Renville County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down on houses and vehicles, along with power lines in Glencoe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the late morning across west central Minnesota. These storms were from activity that produced severe weather in central North Dakota the previous day. ||Ahead of the thunderstorms, temperatures rose into the 70s and 80s. A few storms produced large hail in Grant County prior to moving into Douglas County in west central Minnesota during the late morning of Tuesday, June 4th. As the storms moved east into Douglas and Pope Counties, a few hail stones, up to penny size, fell, along with wind gusts of 45 to 50 mph. Slowly these storms intensified as they moved southeast across southern Minnesota. ||As two severe storms merged in southern Scott, and northern Le Sueur Counties, damaging straight-line winds and downburst occurred. The worst storm damage occured southeast of Belle Plaine, and along the border of Scott and Le Sueur Counties from Union Hill to New Prague. This is where two storm spotters measured a wind gust of 85 mph. This caused structural damage to a barn, and numerous trees were blown down from Union Hill to New Prague. ||There were other areas of southern Minnesota that had wind damage and large hail. This included areas around Hutchinson, Gaylord and Buffalo Lake. ||Large hail also occurred, with numerous reports of quarter size hail, with a few as large as golf balls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of June 30th. These storms primarily produced damaging winds across portions of Mower and Fillmore Counties. An outbuilding suffered damaged to the siding and framing near Lyle (Mower County) while trees and power lines were blown down in Mabel (Fillmore County). Around 1,100 customers were without power in Lyle following the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective complex moved out of central South Dakota and across northeast South Dakota late night, spurred by a strong low-level jet and elevated instability up to 3000 J/kg. Cells developing along the southern flank of this system quickly became severe with scattered reports of large hail and damaging wind.

An area of low pressure developed in the lee of the Rockies late on the 10th and then slowly moved northeast on the 11th before passing over the Northland on the 12th. This lead to a long duration snowfall event over most of the area with periods of freezing rain mixing in at times. Widespread strong northeast winds of 40 mph plus were reported with upwards of 50 to 60 mph gusts observed at the head of Lake Superior. The strong winds lead to building waves on Lake Superior, but the ice that was still present at the western end of the lake acted to dampen the wave action, preventing the large waves from crashing onto the lakeshore. The winds also led to numerous reports of downed trees and power lines with multiple vehicle crashes due to the snow and freezing rain. Snowfall amounts were generally in the 3 to 9 inch range with the highest totals being 10.9 inches in Chisholm, 11.0 inches just north of Knife River and 13.5 inches near Pine River.

Oppressive temperatures in the mid 90's to around 100 degrees along with dew point readings in the mid to upper 70's produced very dangerous heat conditions with heat index readings in excess of 105 degrees for much of the afternoon and early evening.||Some of the more extreme heat index readings experienced during the afternoon included 113 degrees at Wagner, 112 degrees at Le Mars, Canton and Mitchell, 111 degrees at Cherokee, Sioux City, Huron and Yankton, 110 degrees at Sioux Falls, Colton and White Lake, and 109 degrees near Beaver Creek.

Oppressive temperatures in the mid 90's to around 100 degrees along with dew point readings in the mid to upper 70's produced very dangerous heat conditions with heat index readings in excess of 105 degrees for much of the afternoon and early evening.||Some of the more extreme heat index readings experienced during the afternoon included 113 degrees at Wagner, 112 degrees at Le Mars, Canton and Mitchell, 111 degrees at Cherokee, Sioux City, Huron and Yankton, 110 degrees at Sioux Falls, Colton and White Lake, and 109 degrees near Beaver Creek.
A severe storm developed between Waseca and Albert Lea during the afternoon of June 30th. This storm produced quarter size hail northwest of Albert Lea.
A brief thunderstorm developed in an area of weak shear, humid weather, and a boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Steele County Sheriff filmed a brief landspout tornado over an open field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed in the lee of the Rockies late on the 10th and then slowly moved northeast on the 11th before passing over the Northland on the 12th. This lead to a long duration snowfall event over most of the area with periods of freezing rain mixing in at times. Widespread strong northeast winds of 40 mph plus were reported with upwards of 50 to 60 mph gusts observed at the head of Lake Superior. The strong winds lead to building waves on Lake Superior, but the ice that was still present at the western end of the lake acted to dampen the wave action, preventing the large waves from crashing onto the lakeshore. The winds also led to numerous reports of downed trees and power lines with multiple vehicle crashes due to the snow and freezing rain. Snowfall amounts were generally in the 3 to 9 inch range with the highest totals being 10.9 inches in Chisholm, 11.0 inches just north of Knife River and 13.5 inches near Pine River.

Oppressive temperatures in the mid 90's to around 100 degrees along with dew point readings in the mid to upper 70's produced very dangerous heat conditions with heat index readings in excess of 105 degrees for much of the afternoon and early evening.||Some of the more extreme heat index readings experienced during the afternoon included 113 degrees at Wagner, 112 degrees at Le Mars, Canton and Mitchell, 111 degrees at Cherokee, Sioux City, Huron and Yankton, 110 degrees at Sioux Falls, Colton and White Lake, and 109 degrees near Beaver Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent vort max moving over the Minnesota Arrowhead led to popcorn-type showers and storms during the afternoon hours. One of these storms interacted with an inland-moving lake breeze and resulted in a brief, weak tornado that was observed by a fishing party.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fishing party on Elbow Lake observed a funnel cloud and took a photo of it. They reported there was a brief period of debris being lofted into the air. The party contacted a local pilot who performed a flyover and confirmed tree damage in the woods north of Elbow Lake. This tornado occurred in an area with no roads, making it difficult to survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of one to three inches on frozen ground and into a snow pack with between 3 and 6 inches of liquid water equivalent resulted in considerable overland flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding resulted in damage to public infrastructure including county and township roads and culverts. The property damage costs include estimates for public property losses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With temperatures peaking in the mid to upper 90's and dew points in the lower to mid 70's, heat index values ranged from 100 to 108 during the afternoon and early evening hours.

A cluster of thunderstorms that developed in west central Minnesota during the afternoon of Thursday, June 20th, became severe with several reports of large hail, damaging winds, and tornado damage. Damage occurred from a tornado in Redwood County, between Wabasso, and Redwood Falls, and was determined to have EF1 damage. More extensive damage was on the east and southeast side of St. James where vehicles were blown off of MN-60 and a turkey barn was destroyed. Other severe storms produced an EF0 tornado in Brown County.

Southeast Minnesota sweltered for one day with excessive heat in the middle of July. Heat indices topped out between 105 and 115 on July 19th. Air temperatures during this heat wave generally topped out around 90 while dew points climbed into the lower 80s. The highest calculated heat index was 117 from a mesonet station in Houston (Houston County), near Kellogg (Wabasha County) and the automated weather observing equipment at the Winona airport (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices of 105 to 115 were common across Fillmore County on July 19th. The highest calculated heat index was 114 from a mesonet station near Canton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: St. James regional airport measured a wind gust of 78 mph.  A storm survey team from the NWS determined the damage from surrounding areas was due to a downburst, with a classic fanning out pattern. Part of a roof from a turkey barn was blown off, several vehicles were blown off Highway 60, and hundreds of trees were broken or uprooted southwest to southeast of the airport. There was also significant damage to the airport's hanger.

An area of low pressure developed in the lee of the Rockies late on the 10th and then slowly moved northeast on the 11th before passing over the Northland on the 12th. This lead to a long duration snowfall event over most of the area with periods of freezing rain mixing in at times. Widespread strong northeast winds of 40 mph plus were reported with upwards of 50 to 60 mph gusts observed at the head of Lake Superior. The strong winds lead to building waves on Lake Superior, but the ice that was still present at the western end of the lake acted to dampen the wave action, preventing the large waves from crashing onto the lakeshore. The winds also led to numerous reports of downed trees and power lines with multiple vehicle crashes due to the snow and freezing rain. Snowfall amounts were generally in the 3 to 9 inch range with the highest totals being 10.9 inches in Chisholm, 11.0 inches just north of Knife River and 13.5 inches near Pine River.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain produced some flash flooding across the western portions of Fillmore County. Water covered roads west of Spring Valley and several small creeks went out of their banks including Spring Valley, Little Kingsley, Deer and Bear. In the city of Spring Valley, water from Spring Valley creek flooded the downtown area for the first time in more than a decade. At least three people tried to drive their vehicles through the flood waters with all three vehicles becoming stranded. Two of the people needed assistance from the Spring Valley fire department to be safely removed from the flood waters. Near Ostrander, water went over County Road 14 and deposited debris from a farm field that made travel difficult after the water receded.

Isolated damage occurred near Delano where a large tree was blown down. Otherwise, most of the reports were of a few small branches blown down, or power outages due to small limbs landing on power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A social media picture posted a large tree that was blown down in Delano.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure stalled out over northern Minnesota during the 11th into the 12th and brought light snowfall to the region. The placement of the low resulted in onshore winds off of Lake Superior along the North Shore which led to lake enhancement of the snow. Additionally, a terrain ridge in this same location helped contribute to snowfall of 4 to 9 inches plus along the Gunflint Trail in Cook County Minnesota. While most of the snow with this event melted due to warm ground temperatures, this lake enhanced area was so heavy it overcame the warm ground temperatures and piled up quickly.

During the early morning of June 30th, a complex of thunderstorms that developed over northern Minnesota, dived southeast across west central Wisconsin. There were several reports of non-severe wind gusts as these storms moved southward across western Wisconsin. However, in the wake of the storms, a wake low developed and caused outflow from the decaying storms across east central Minnesota. Due to the dense population in the Twin Cities, a few reports of 40 to 50 mph winds occurred. There were sporadic reports of tree limbs blown down, but a couple of larger branches and smaller trees did manage to be blown down during this event near Crystal and Edina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were a few reports of downed trees and power lines near Crystal Airport, and Edina. Measured wind gusts from the decaying thunderstorm complex in west central Wisconsin were between 40 and 50 mph around the Twin Cities metro area. There was a measured wind gust at the MSP airport of 52 mph during the morning event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that developed across west central Minnesota during the morning of June 30th, became severe near Mille Lacs Lake where reports of downed trees occurred near Vineland. Also, a few wind gusts downed trees near Rush City. There were also a few wind gusts of 50 to 55 mph that occurred south of St. Cloud from near South Haven, to Annandale, and Maple Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down near Vineland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain moved very slowly across southeast Minnesota during the early morning hours of June 28th. These storms dumped between 3 and 7 inches of rain that resulted in flash flooding occurring in Dodge, Olmsted and Fillmore Counties. The Rochester International Airport (Olmsted County) was closed when water covered the runways. Numerous roads were underwater and between 40 and 60 cows were swept into the South Branch of the Middle Fork of the Zumbro River north of Byron (Olmsted County). A state of emergency was declared in Fillmore County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Kasson, 7.5 inches of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over northeastern Minnesota on the afternoon of the 4th. A warm front was draped along the International Border with a secondary warm front pushing northward from the south. Daytime heating, advection of a more favorable airmass for severe weather and a shortwave trough moved into the western part of the area and lead to increased shear and steep lapse rates that led to the development of storms. Storms become stronger through the afternoon as they moved southeast, but limited reports of severe weather were received. Aside from a few hail and wind damage reports, one instance of flash flooding was noted along a Minnesota State Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon and early evening of June 4th. The first round of storms produced large hail across Dodge County, with hail ranging from 1 inch up to 2.75 inches. These storms merged with other storms that moved in from south-central Minnesota and went on to produce a tornado in Rochester (Olmsted County) with wind damage across portions of Wabasha, Olmsted, Fillmore and Houston Counties. A 70 mph wind gust was estimated by spotters near Chatfield (Fillmore County) and trees were blown down in Kellogg (Wabasha County) and near Mound Prairie (Houston County). Around 2,000 customers lost power in Rochester following the passage of the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain moved very slowly across southeast Minnesota during the early morning hours of June 28th. These storms dumped between 3 and 7 inches of rain that resulted in flash flooding occurring in Dodge, Olmsted and Fillmore Counties. The Rochester International Airport (Olmsted County) was closed when water covered the runways. Numerous roads were underwater and between 40 and 60 cows were swept into the South Branch of the Middle Fork of the Zumbro River north of Byron (Olmsted County). A state of emergency was declared in Fillmore County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Zumbro River out of its banks in Zumbro Falls. The river crested just over nine feet above the flood stage at 27.03 feet on June 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon and early evening of June 4th. The first round of storms produced large hail across Dodge County, with hail ranging from 1 inch up to 2.75 inches. These storms merged with other storms that moved in from south-central Minnesota and went on to produce a tornado in Rochester (Olmsted County) with wind damage across portions of Wabasha, Olmsted, Fillmore and Houston Counties. A 70 mph wind gust was estimated by spotters near Chatfield (Fillmore County) and trees were blown down in Kellogg (Wabasha County) and near Mound Prairie (Houston County). Around 2,000 customers lost power in Rochester following the passage of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to quarter sized hail was reported just south of Kasson.

Southeast Minnesota sweltered for one day with excessive heat in the middle of July. Heat indices topped out between 105 and 115 on July 19th. Air temperatures during this heat wave generally topped out around 90 while dew points climbed into the lower 80s. The highest calculated heat index was 117 from a mesonet station in Houston (Houston County), near Kellogg (Wabasha County) and the automated weather observing equipment at the Winona airport (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices of 105 to 115 were common across Houston County on July 19th. The highest calculated heat index was 117 from a mesonet station in Houston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed in the lee of the Rockies late on the 10th and then slowly moved northeast on the 11th before passing over the Northland on the 12th. This lead to a long duration snowfall event over most of the area with periods of freezing rain mixing in at times. Widespread strong northeast winds of 40 mph plus were reported with upwards of 50 to 60 mph gusts observed at the head of Lake Superior. The strong winds lead to building waves on Lake Superior, but the ice that was still present at the western end of the lake acted to dampen the wave action, preventing the large waves from crashing onto the lakeshore. The winds also led to numerous reports of downed trees and power lines with multiple vehicle crashes due to the snow and freezing rain. Snowfall amounts were generally in the 3 to 9 inch range with the highest totals being 10.9 inches in Chisholm, 11.0 inches just north of Knife River and 13.5 inches near Pine River.

Rainfall of one to three inches on frozen ground and into a snow pack with between 3 and 6 inches of liquid water equivalent resulted in considerable overland flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding resulted in extensive damage to public infrastructure including numerous county and township roads, culverts, and some bridges. The property damage costs include estimates for public property losses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds of 30 to 40 mph, with gusts to around 60 mph, developed across the area as surface low pressure intensified rapidly on October 21 moving northward across Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were frequently above 40 mph over several hours, with a peak wind gust of 53 mph at 2256CST at Marshall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were multiple waves of precipitation that occurred with this major winter storm that lasted for more than two days. ||The first wave of precipitation developed during the morning of Wednesday, April 10th where the initial band moved from southern Minnesota, northward into central Minnesota, and west central Wisconsin by the afternoon and evening. This band of precipitation had thunder, sleet and heavy snow that produced several inches of snow across portions of southern and central Minnesota. Most of the heavier precipitation tapered off in the southern quarter of Minnesota, with light snow continuing across central Minnesota, during the afternoon and evening of Thursday, April 11th.  ||The second wave of precipitation developed in far southern Minnesota after midnight, Thursday morning, April 11th. This band of precipitation had warmer air aloft which led to more sleet, rain, freezing rain, and thunder. This band was similar to the first with the intensity, but had more mixed precipitation.||By the mid morning, the wave of precipitation had moved out of the region, with much warmer air surging northward ahead of a surface low that moved into southwest Minnesota during the afternoon. There were even some severe thunderstorms that developed near Redwood Falls where one inch hail was reported.||In addition to the precipitation Thursday morning, the next weather element developed. This weather element was a significant increase in the wind speeds, with sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph, with gusts of 50 mph or more across a wide areas of central, and southern Minnesota. This created blizzard conditions in portions of southwest, and west central Minnesota. It would have been worse if the precipitation was in the form of all snow, but warmer air aloft led to more sleet and rain, vs snow. ||The high winds also created structural damage to power poles and trees. Some freezing rain led to fallen tree limbs and power outages from ice build up. The local emergency manager in Freeborn County had more than 300 downed power poles, which affected 6500 residents. The Waseca County emergency manager also reported numerous power poles blown down across the county. The National Guard was requested for Freeborn and Steele Counties of south central Minnesota due to stranded motorists. The Governor of Minnesota declared a state of emergency due to the widespread power outages, and stranded motorists. ||By Thursday afternoon, most of the heavier precipitation and thunderstorms had moved north and east of the area, with mainly light precipitation in the form of drizzle, light snow or sleet in small areas rotating northward as the main storm started to weaken and move overhead.  In addition, wind speeds lessened which led to much better visibility in the west. However, the Minnesota Department of Transportation had already closed or not advised travel for a wide area of west central to southwest Minnesota. ||The third wave of precipitation was associated with the main upper level system and changed the precipitation over to all snow by early Friday morning, April 12th. By the afternoon, most of the precipitation had ended, but residual light snow or flurries continued as the main system moved off to the east.||Snowfall totals as the storm ended ranged from over 18 inches in portions of west central Minnesota, to 6 to 12 inches across a wide area of central and southern Minnesota. Areas along the Iowa border, which had more sleet and rain, received 1 to locally 4 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several waves of precipitation occurred across the county with heavy snow at the start, with a mixture of sleet, and snow during the overnight hours of Wednesday/Thursday, before mostly light snow and sleet occurred Thursday, and Thursday night. Wind speeds increased early Thursday morning, with significant blowing and drifting snow, causing blizzard conditions for several hours. Wind speeds lessened Thursday afternoon but light snow redeveloped. Snowfall totals averaged 10 to 18 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread rainfall of 1 to 3 inches between May 21 and May 27 led to significant rises on most rivers. This worsened flooding across existing flooded areas and expanded flooding to additional areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flandreau Creek near Flandreau (gage site FCRS2) reached a crest of 0.20 feet above flood stage on May 28. The main impact was inundation of agricultural land and additional delays to spring planting in the flood plain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Sioux River near Montgomery (gage site MLSI4) reached a crest of 1.07 feet above flood stage on May 30. The main impact was inundation of agricultural land, which further delayed spring planting. 170th Avenue was flooded north of State Highway 9. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed mid to late afternoon along a surface boundary near the Minnesota-Iowa border which extended back into southeast South Dakota. Deep layer shear of 30-35 knots and up to 1500 J/kg MLCAPE led to rapidly developing storms which quickly became multi-cellular and organized in clusters. One stronger updraft produced sub-severe hail.

A few large hail stones fell across southwest Minnesota during the morning of Thursday, April 11th. A large storm system produced a large swath of snow initially across the Upper Midwest during the previous 12-14 hours. As this system began to tap very mild air from the south, enough instability led to elevated convection. A few thunderstorms developed Thursday morning, and some produced up to quarter size hail near Redwood Falls and Olivia.

Isolated thunderstorms developed within strong mid-level warm advection and elevated instability during the early morning hours.  A couple updrafts were persistent enough to produce some sub-severe size hail, but storms generally remained only weakly organized.

During the morning of Wednesday, April 17th, a large area of showers developed across southern Minnesota. Several embedded thunderstorms developed after sunrise and moved across south central, and into southeast and east central Minnesota. A very anomalous moist atmosphere was in place, and rainfall was very efficient. Several of the storms moved across the same areas from Mankato to St. Peter, and New Prague, while another moved from Faribault to Cannon Falls, and Red Wing Minnesota. Rainfall rates were high, and some observers and weather sensors reported up to 1 and 2 inches per hour. By the afternoon as the precipitation slowly ended, a band of 3 to 5 inches of rain fell from near Faribault, to Northfield, and Hastings in Minnesota. Another heavy band of rainfall with amounts of 2 to 4 inches fell from St. Peter, to La Sueur, and New Prague.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall triggered multiple rock and mudslides on Highway 169, between St. Peter and Mankato. The Minnesota Department of Transportation had to close parts of Highway 169 due to debris on the southbound lanes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall with amounts of 2 to 4 inches fell in a short period of time, and caused roads to be covered by water. The Minnesota Department of Transportation indicated that water was running over State Highway 112 at 311 Avenue, east of Ottawa. The road was closed temporarily during this event.
