The city of Northfield was hit the hardest with several roads having running water up to 2 feet deep. ||The local emergency manager reported the following;||The City of Northfield had the most problems relating to flooding. It was mainly the northern portion of the city. City streets were flooded in and along Greenvale Ave west of Highway 3. The Cannon Valley Drive had a huge intersection flooded, and the Cannon River in Northfield rose 3 feet in a very short amount of time. ||In Rice County, along County Road 46 & County Road 9, which parallels I-35, there were reports of a measured 6 of water flowing over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of one to three inches on frozen ground and into a snow pack with between 3 and 6 inches of liquid water equivalent resulted in considerable overland flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flowed over multiple areas of U.S. Highway 71, mainly in areas east of Steen and near Hardwick.  Numerous other county and township roads were covered by water at times. The property damage costs include estimates for public property losses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of one to three inches on frozen ground and into a snow pack with between 3 and 6 inches of liquid water equivalent resulted in considerable overland flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding resulted in extensive damage to public infrastructure including numerous county and township roads, culverts, and some bridges. The property damage costs include estimates for public property losses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of August 4th was very hot and humid. Most areas topped out in the 80s, but several locations in the northern Red River Valley made it to around 90 degrees. This, along with dew point values around 70 degrees in some areas, made for a very muggy afternoon. By evening, a weak stationary boundary set up from the Lake of the Woods region over into central North Dakota. Thunderstorms finally broke out over northwest and north central North Dakota around midnight. These storms tracked to the east-southeast into the early morning hours of August 5th, mainly affecting the Devils Lake region down into southeast North Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon highs on August 6th again topped out in the 80s, although dew points had fallen somewhat from August 5th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage was reported in Tabor Township from hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occasional light snow and freezing drizzle developed during the evening of Thursday, November 28 resulting in a light accumulation of icing before tapering off. Another surge of mixed-type precipitation developed the following afternoon before changing back to snow during the evening of November 30. Snowfall totaled 2.2 inches near Edgerton and 1.2 inches at Pipestone.

A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.70 inches at Pipestone) resulted in sporadic and minor flooding of agricultural lands. Pipestone Creek below Pipestone (gage site PSTM5) reached the highest stage on record cresting 0.42 feet above flood stage on March 24.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.11 inches near Garretson and 1.70 inches at  Pipestone) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands. 250th Street along the Minnesota/South Dakota border was flooded for numerous days. Split Rock Creek below Jasper (gage site JSPM5) reached the fourth highest stage on record cresting 4.53 feet above flood stage on March 18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were multiple waves of precipitation that occurred with this major winter storm that lasted for more than two days. ||The first wave of precipitation developed during the morning of Wednesday, April 10th where the initial band moved from southern Minnesota, northward into central Minnesota, and west central Wisconsin by the afternoon and evening. This band of precipitation had thunder, sleet and heavy snow that produced several inches of snow across portions of southern and central Minnesota. Most of the heavier precipitation tapered off in the southern quarter of Minnesota, with light snow continuing across central Minnesota, during the afternoon and evening of Thursday, April 11th.  ||The second wave of precipitation developed in far southern Minnesota after midnight, Thursday morning, April 11th. This band of precipitation had warmer air aloft which led to more sleet, rain, freezing rain, and thunder. This band was similar to the first with the intensity, but had more mixed precipitation.||By the mid morning, the wave of precipitation had moved out of the region, with much warmer air surging northward ahead of a surface low that moved into southwest Minnesota during the afternoon. There were even some severe thunderstorms that developed near Redwood Falls where one inch hail was reported.||In addition to the precipitation Thursday morning, the next weather element developed. This weather element was a significant increase in the wind speeds, with sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph, with gusts of 50 mph or more across a wide areas of central, and southern Minnesota. This created blizzard conditions in portions of southwest, and west central Minnesota. It would have been worse if the precipitation was in the form of all snow, but warmer air aloft led to more sleet and rain, vs snow. ||The high winds also created structural damage to power poles and trees. Some freezing rain led to fallen tree limbs and power outages from ice build up. The local emergency manager in Freeborn County had more than 300 downed power poles, which affected 6500 residents. The Waseca County emergency manager also reported numerous power poles blown down across the county. The National Guard was requested for Freeborn and Steele Counties of south central Minnesota due to stranded motorists. The Governor of Minnesota declared a state of emergency due to the widespread power outages, and stranded motorists. ||By Thursday afternoon, most of the heavier precipitation and thunderstorms had moved north and east of the area, with mainly light precipitation in the form of drizzle, light snow or sleet in small areas rotating northward as the main storm started to weaken and move overhead.  In addition, wind speeds lessened which led to much better visibility in the west. However, the Minnesota Department of Transportation had already closed or not advised travel for a wide area of west central to southwest Minnesota. ||The third wave of precipitation was associated with the main upper level system and changed the precipitation over to all snow by early Friday morning, April 12th. By the afternoon, most of the precipitation had ended, but residual light snow or flurries continued as the main system moved off to the east.||Snowfall totals as the storm ended ranged from over 18 inches in portions of west central Minnesota, to 6 to 12 inches across a wide area of central and southern Minnesota. Areas along the Iowa border, which had more sleet and rain, received 1 to locally 4 inches of snow.

Snowfall totals averaged 9 to 12 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the low level jet (strong wind speeds) and warm advection helped fuel several severe thunderstorms from the late evening of August 19th through the early morning hours of August 20th. The first severe thunderstorm occurred just north of Devils Lake, North Dakota. Later severe storms generally tracked from the Devils Lake region, through the Ada-Twin Valley area, and then into portions of west central Minnesota. The storms mainly produced large hail, but at least one storm produced a bow echo and damaging winds across west central Minnesota.

The combination of the low level jet (strong wind speeds) and warm advection helped fuel several severe thunderstorms from the late evening of August 19th through the early morning hours of August 20th. The first severe thunderstorm occurred just north of Devils Lake, North Dakota. Later severe storms generally tracked from the Devils Lake region, through the Ada-Twin Valley area, and then into portions of west central Minnesota. The storms mainly produced large hail, but at least one storm produced a bow echo and damaging winds across west central Minnesota.
A funnel cloud was reported during the early evening of August 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud formed and moved eastward for a few minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.77 inches near Edgerton) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands. The Rock River near Hardwick (gage site HRDM5) reached the fifth highest stage on record cresting 2.63 feet above flood stage on March 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occasional light snow developed during the evening of Thursday, November 28, and continued into December 1, briefly mixing with freezing drizzle. Snowfall totaled 5.0 inches at Tracy, 4.9 inches at Minneota and 4.6 inches at Marshall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the afternoon of August 17th, a cold front extended from the Lake of the Woods region back into central South Dakota. Thunderstorms broke out along the front and continued into the early evening, producing numerous funnel clouds, as well as large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over northeastern Minnesota on the afternoon of the 4th. A warm front was draped along the International Border with a secondary warm front pushing northward from the south. Daytime heating, advection of a more favorable airmass for severe weather and a shortwave trough moved into the western part of the area and lead to increased shear and steep lapse rates that led to the development of storms. Storms become stronger through the afternoon as they moved southeast, but limited reports of severe weather were received. Aside from a few hail and wind damage reports, one instance of flash flooding was noted along a Minnesota State Highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southbound lanes of Minnesota Highway 33 1.5 miles north of Cloquet were beginning to flood and traffic was having to slow to navigate the water on the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread rainfall of 1 to 3 inches between May 21 and May 27 led to significant rises on most rivers. This worsened flooding across existing flooded areas and expanded flooding to additional areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Windom (gage site WDOM5) reached a peak of 0.63 feet above flood stage at the end of the month. The main impact was inundation of agricultural land and additional delays to spring planting in the flood plain. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.77 inches near Edgerton) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands. The Rock River near Hardwick (gage site HRDM5) reached the fifth highest stage on record cresting 2.63 feet above flood stage on March 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few large hail stones fell across southwest Minnesota during the morning of Thursday, April 11th. A large storm system produced a large swath of snow initially across the Upper Midwest during the previous 12-14 hours. As this system began to tap very mild air from the south, enough instability led to elevated convection. A few thunderstorms developed Thursday morning, and some produced up to quarter size hail near Redwood Falls and Olivia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Minnesota during the day on the 19th. Ahead of the front was a very moist and extremely unstable air mass along with a warm front. Strong wind shear was also in place, especially in the vicinity of the warm front and with a low-level jet pushing into the area as well. These ingredients led to storm development across central Minnesota by mid-afternoon with one storm becoming a dominant supercell that produced very large hail over a wide area, with some of that large hail being driven by winds in excess of 60 mph. As the storm crossed I-35, upwards of 20 cars experienced broken windshields with many of the cars being trapped on the highway as the most intense part of the storm crossed the highway in the middle of a construction zone where traffic was at a standstill due to the construction and heavy weekend vacation traffic from the Minneapolis-St. Paul area. This storm then crossed into Wisconsin and continued to produce large hail and sporadic wind damage, along with a few funnel clouds. Heading into the evening hours, this storm evolved upscale into a large MCS that caused large amounts of damage across parts of Wisconsin. Additional storms were seen ahead of the large supercell that led to additional wind and hail damage as well.

By the early afternoon of Thursday, October 10th, surface low pressure was located near Wadena, Minnesota. A stationary boundary extended northward from the low, up toward Baudette, Minnesota. As a result, there were wide temperature differences across the area, with low 50s along the stationary boundary to the lower 30s from Devils Lake to Jamestown. Steady snow fell west of the Red River Valley, while rain or mixed precipitation fell along and east of the Red River Valley. As the low slowly moved toward north central Minnesota by Friday morning, winds turned to the northwest along and west of the Red River Valley, and temperatures dropped to around or below 32 degrees. East of the Red River Valley, winds stayed southwest with temperatures holding in the low to mid 30s. Therefore, snow became the predominant precipitation type for all areas except around the Lake of the Woods region, where it remained rain or mixed precipitation. For the rest of Friday into early Saturday, the low actually spun back westward, or back into northwest Minnesota. This kept steady snow over most of the area, but the least from the Lake of the Woods region to Bemidji to Wadena. As far as storm totals (covering from the 10th to the 12th), areas just west of the Red River Valley into the Devils Lake region had the most snow, up to 30 inches in spots. The Forman to Fargo to Grand Forks to Hallock corridor picked up 1 to 2 inches of rainfall before it turned to snow. Because of this initial rain, snow totals were lower, but this corridor still managed to get 4 to 24 inches of snow, with some mixed precipitation as well. This had huge impacts on the area, including tree damage, power outages, river flooding, and basement flooding.

A warm front lifted across central and northeastern Minnesota during the day on the 14th. Thunderstorms developed along the front with a few of the storms producing marginally severe hail and damaging wind gusts in the Brainerd Lakes area during the late afternoon and early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the hail was pea-sized but there were several to the size of quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Minnesota during the day on the 19th. Ahead of the front was a very moist and extremely unstable air mass along with a warm front. Strong wind shear was also in place, especially in the vicinity of the warm front and with a low-level jet pushing into the area as well. These ingredients led to storm development across central Minnesota by mid-afternoon with one storm becoming a dominant supercell that produced very large hail over a wide area, with some of that large hail being driven by winds in excess of 60 mph. As the storm crossed I-35, upwards of 20 cars experienced broken windshields with many of the cars being trapped on the highway as the most intense part of the storm crossed the highway in the middle of a construction zone where traffic was at a standstill due to the construction and heavy weekend vacation traffic from the Minneapolis-St. Paul area. This storm then crossed into Wisconsin and continued to produce large hail and sporadic wind damage, along with a few funnel clouds. Heading into the evening hours, this storm evolved upscale into a large MCS that caused large amounts of damage across parts of Wisconsin. Additional storms were seen ahead of the large supercell that led to additional wind and hail damage as well.

By the early afternoon of Thursday, October 10th, surface low pressure was located near Wadena, Minnesota. A stationary boundary extended northward from the low, up toward Baudette, Minnesota. As a result, there were wide temperature differences across the area, with low 50s along the stationary boundary to the lower 30s from Devils Lake to Jamestown. Steady snow fell west of the Red River Valley, while rain or mixed precipitation fell along and east of the Red River Valley. As the low slowly moved toward north central Minnesota by Friday morning, winds turned to the northwest along and west of the Red River Valley, and temperatures dropped to around or below 32 degrees. East of the Red River Valley, winds stayed southwest with temperatures holding in the low to mid 30s. Therefore, snow became the predominant precipitation type for all areas except around the Lake of the Woods region, where it remained rain or mixed precipitation. For the rest of Friday into early Saturday, the low actually spun back westward, or back into northwest Minnesota. This kept steady snow over most of the area, but the least from the Lake of the Woods region to Bemidji to Wadena. As far as storm totals (covering from the 10th to the 12th), areas just west of the Red River Valley into the Devils Lake region had the most snow, up to 30 inches in spots. The Forman to Fargo to Grand Forks to Hallock corridor picked up 1 to 2 inches of rainfall before it turned to snow. Because of this initial rain, snow totals were lower, but this corridor still managed to get 4 to 24 inches of snow, with some mixed precipitation as well. This had huge impacts on the area, including tree damage, power outages, river flooding, and basement flooding.

A strong Fall storm system developed during the morning of Sunday, October 20th in the northern Rockies, and slowly moved eastward, intensified, and drifted across Minnesota through the early morning of Tuesday, October 22nd. Initially, strong southeast winds developed, but as the system moved across Minnesota, colder air filtering southward, and an increased pressure gradient, allowed for winds to strengthen. Numerous airport, mesonet and private weather wind sensors measured wind speeds of 40 to 55 mph by Monday afternoon. The Alexandria airport measured a wind speed of 58 mph late Monday afternoon.

A cold front moved into eastern North Dakota during the late afternoon of Saturday, September 21st, then pushed into the northwest quarter of Minnesota in the early evening. Thunderstorms broke out along the front and tracked east-northeast, producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The large hail shredded cornfields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the late afternoon of the 20th, a weak area of surface low pressure set up near Devils Lake, with the warm front extending out to the east-southeast. Temperatures had risen into the mid 70s to low 80s in the warm sector, with dew points in the upper 60s to lower 70s. To the southwest of the low, a cold front extended back toward Bismarck. Just before 5 pm CDT, a fairly solid line of thunderstorms developed along the cold front, while a smaller cluster formed around Cavalier. By around 6 pm CDT, another cluster of storms developed west of Fargo. Most of this thunderstorm activity had shifted north of the highway 200 corridor by 10 pm, although the most intense rainfall occurred along the southern edge of this area. The New Rockford to Grand Forks/East Grand Forks to Roseau areas picked up 2 to 5 inches of rain in a short duration, prompting flash flood warnings in those areas. Off and on steady rainfall continued into Saturday morning the 21st, before the rain started to end from southwest to northeast. Many of the flash flood warnings were transitioned to areal flood warnings, as the heavy rain ran off into the local drainage and river systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The swath of large hail fell from Erhard to Pelican Rapids.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over southern Manitoba the morning of the 15th moved to near southern Hudson Bay by the morning of the 16th. A cold front associated with this low moved across the Northland during the afternoon and evening hours of the 15th. A warm front had moved through on the previous day brought a warm and unstable airmass in its wake. The cold front then worked with this airmass to create severe storms from northeast Minnesota into northwest Wisconsin and western Lake Superior. Damaging winds accounted for most of the reports with these storms, although there was some marginally severe hail as well. Most of the damage was to trees, but some property damage to pontoon boats and a home also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo was received via social media of a large tree down on a power line near the Island View Lodge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With already saturated soils from a wet September, runoff from more rainfall events caused flooding to occur along the Mississippi River. The river went out of its banks in Wabasha (Wabasha County) during the middle part of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from rainfall events pushed the Mississippi River out of its banks in Wabasha. The river crested around a half foot above the flood stage at 12.37 feet on the 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A reinforcing jet streak dropped into a deep trough across the Northern Plains. Increasing warm advection in advance of the wave led to the development of a narrow frontal band, which was reinforced for a time during the late afternoon and evening of November 5 as the main jet streak dug into the region. The snow band dropped from 2 to 4 inch accumulations from the mid-James River Valley into southern Minnesota.|In a narrow zone on the south side of the band of snowfall, a brief period of freezing rain produced a light glaze of ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of light wintry mix, a period of light snowfall resulted in accumulations of 3.0 inches at Brookings and 2.9 inches at Minneota.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of light wintry mix, a period of light snowfall resulted in accumulations of 3.0 inches at Tracy and 2.9 inches at Minneota. Around 0730CST on November 6, a vehicle at the intersection of U.S. Highway 14 and 280th Avenue lost control on a snow- and ice-covered roadway then rolled several times resulting in an injury (indirect) to the driver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were multiple waves of precipitation that occurred with this major winter storm that lasted for more than two days. ||The first wave of precipitation developed during the morning of Wednesday, April 10th where the initial band moved from southern Minnesota, northward into central Minnesota, and west central Wisconsin by the afternoon and evening. This band of precipitation had thunder, sleet and heavy snow that produced several inches of snow across portions of southern and central Minnesota. Most of the heavier precipitation tapered off in the southern quarter of Minnesota, with light snow continuing across central Minnesota, during the afternoon and evening of Thursday, April 11th.  ||The second wave of precipitation developed in far southern Minnesota after midnight, Thursday morning, April 11th. This band of precipitation had warmer air aloft which led to more sleet, rain, freezing rain, and thunder. This band was similar to the first with the intensity, but had more mixed precipitation.||By the mid morning, the wave of precipitation had moved out of the region, with much warmer air surging northward ahead of a surface low that moved into southwest Minnesota during the afternoon. There were even some severe thunderstorms that developed near Redwood Falls where one inch hail was reported.||In addition to the precipitation Thursday morning, the next weather element developed. This weather element was a significant increase in the wind speeds, with sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph, with gusts of 50 mph or more across a wide areas of central, and southern Minnesota. This created blizzard conditions in portions of southwest, and west central Minnesota. It would have been worse if the precipitation was in the form of all snow, but warmer air aloft led to more sleet and rain, vs snow. ||The high winds also created structural damage to power poles and trees. Some freezing rain led to fallen tree limbs and power outages from ice build up. The local emergency manager in Freeborn County had more than 300 downed power poles, which affected 6500 residents. The Waseca County emergency manager also reported numerous power poles blown down across the county. The National Guard was requested for Freeborn and Steele Counties of south central Minnesota due to stranded motorists. The Governor of Minnesota declared a state of emergency due to the widespread power outages, and stranded motorists. ||By Thursday afternoon, most of the heavier precipitation and thunderstorms had moved north and east of the area, with mainly light precipitation in the form of drizzle, light snow or sleet in small areas rotating northward as the main storm started to weaken and move overhead.  In addition, wind speeds lessened which led to much better visibility in the west. However, the Minnesota Department of Transportation had already closed or not advised travel for a wide area of west central to southwest Minnesota. ||The third wave of precipitation was associated with the main upper level system and changed the precipitation over to all snow by early Friday morning, April 12th. By the afternoon, most of the precipitation had ended, but residual light snow or flurries continued as the main system moved off to the east.||Snowfall totals as the storm ended ranged from over 18 inches in portions of west central Minnesota, to 6 to 12 inches across a wide area of central and southern Minnesota. Areas along the Iowa border, which had more sleet and rain, received 1 to locally 4 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several waves of precipitation occurred across the county with moderate to heavy snow at the start, with a mixture of sleet, and snow during the overnight hours of Wednesday/Thursday, before mostly light snow and sleet occurred Thursday, and Thursday night. Wind speeds increased Thursday morning, with significant blowing and drifting snow, causing blizzard conditions for several hours. Wind speeds lessened late Thursday afternoon but light snow redeveloped. Snowfall totals averaged 10 to 14 inches across the county.

By the early afternoon of the 20th, the surface warm front had lifted to a Grafton, North Dakota, to Bemidji, Minnesota line. There had been a short lull in the thunderstorm activity, but by early afternoon, clusters of thunderstorms had increased in areal coverage along the Canadian border, from St. Vincent to Baudette. Elements within these thunderstorm clusters continued to strengthen to severe levels, mainly producing large hail.

A reinforcing jet streak dropped into a deep trough across the Northern Plains. Increasing warm advection in advance of the wave led to the development of a narrow frontal band, which was reinforced for a time during the late afternoon and evening of November 5 as the main jet streak dug into the region. The snow band dropped from 2 to 4 inch accumulations from the mid-James River Valley into southern Minnesota.|In a narrow zone on the south side of the band of snowfall, a brief period of freezing rain produced a light glaze of ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of light wintry mix, a period of light snowfall resulted in an accumulation of 3.5 inches at Worthington. Around 0240CST on November 6, a vehicle traveling westbound on Interstate 90 near Adrian lost control on the snow- and ice-covered road and entered the ditch, resulting in injury (indirect) to the passenger.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the late afternoon of the 20th, a weak area of surface low pressure set up near Devils Lake, with the warm front extending out to the east-southeast. Temperatures had risen into the mid 70s to low 80s in the warm sector, with dew points in the upper 60s to lower 70s. To the southwest of the low, a cold front extended back toward Bismarck. Just before 5 pm CDT, a fairly solid line of thunderstorms developed along the cold front, while a smaller cluster formed around Cavalier. By around 6 pm CDT, another cluster of storms developed west of Fargo. Most of this thunderstorm activity had shifted north of the highway 200 corridor by 10 pm, although the most intense rainfall occurred along the southern edge of this area. The New Rockford to Grand Forks/East Grand Forks to Roseau areas picked up 2 to 5 inches of rain in a short duration, prompting flash flood warnings in those areas. Off and on steady rainfall continued into Saturday morning the 21st, before the rain started to end from southwest to northeast. Many of the flash flood warnings were transitioned to areal flood warnings, as the heavy rain ran off into the local drainage and river systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over southern Manitoba the morning of the 15th moved to near southern Hudson Bay by the morning of the 16th. A cold front associated with this low moved across the Northland during the afternoon and evening hours of the 15th. A warm front had moved through on the previous day brought a warm and unstable airmass in its wake. The cold front then worked with this airmass to create severe storms from northeast Minnesota into northwest Wisconsin and western Lake Superior. Damaging winds accounted for most of the reports with these storms, although there was some marginally severe hail as well. Most of the damage was to trees, but some property damage to pontoon boats and a home also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 14-inch tree fell due to the winds and damaged a garage, vehicle and a light pole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet dug into the southwestern U.S. on November 25 and ejected into the plains by early November 26, where a surface low pressure area began to organize in the southern High Plains. The mid-level trough and surface cyclone intensified rapidly lifting toward the western Great Lakes by the morning of November 27. With sharp baroclinicity in place, a band of moderate to heavy snowfall from 4 to 9 inches blanketed far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, and much of southwest Minnesota. The snow was accompanied by northwest winds with gusts as high as 35 to 50 mph near and east of Interstate 29, which resulted in periodic visibility from one-quarter to one-half mile. Some heavier snowfall amounts across southwest Minnesota included 7.2 inches 2 miles northeast of Lakefield, 6.8 inches at Windom, 6.2 inches at Minneota, 5.5 inches at Slayton, and 4.5 inches at Pipestone.

An unusually strong and cold area of high pressure surged into the northern Plains in early March. Winds were mainly a gusty 20 to 35 mph, and when coupled with temperatures from 10 below to 20 below zero, produced a long duration of wind chills colder than 20 below zero. Coldest wind chills were recorded between 0400-0900 on March 3, reaching -51 near Oak Lake, -48 at Volga, -47 at Flandreau and White Lake, -46 at Colton and 5 miles south of Hendricks, -45 at Windom, Worthington and near Sioux Falls, and -44 at Huron, Brookings and Madison. March 3 was one of the coldest March days on record for the area, with the coldest or second coldest March high temperatures on record at Huron, Sioux Falls, Mitchell and Sioux City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the early afternoon of Thursday, October 10th, surface low pressure was located near Wadena, Minnesota. A stationary boundary extended northward from the low, up toward Baudette, Minnesota. As a result, there were wide temperature differences across the area, with low 50s along the stationary boundary to the lower 30s from Devils Lake to Jamestown. Steady snow fell west of the Red River Valley, while rain or mixed precipitation fell along and east of the Red River Valley. As the low slowly moved toward north central Minnesota by Friday morning, winds turned to the northwest along and west of the Red River Valley, and temperatures dropped to around or below 32 degrees. East of the Red River Valley, winds stayed southwest with temperatures holding in the low to mid 30s. Therefore, snow became the predominant precipitation type for all areas except around the Lake of the Woods region, where it remained rain or mixed precipitation. For the rest of Friday into early Saturday, the low actually spun back westward, or back into northwest Minnesota. This kept steady snow over most of the area, but the least from the Lake of the Woods region to Bemidji to Wadena. As far as storm totals (covering from the 10th to the 12th), areas just west of the Red River Valley into the Devils Lake region had the most snow, up to 30 inches in spots. The Forman to Fargo to Grand Forks to Hallock corridor picked up 1 to 2 inches of rainfall before it turned to snow. Because of this initial rain, snow totals were lower, but this corridor still managed to get 4 to 24 inches of snow, with some mixed precipitation as well. This had huge impacts on the area, including tree damage, power outages, river flooding, and basement flooding.

Wind speeds cranked up on the morning of the 11th and continued through the morning of the 12th. Winds gusted up to 60 mph just west of the Red River Valley, from the Devils Lake region down to Valley City. In the immediate Red River Valley, the wind gusts were a little lower, but they were still around the 50 mph range. On top of the heavy snow that just fell, these winds produced whiteout conditions and huge impacts. Interstate 29 was closed from the Canadian border to Fargo, Interstate 94 was closed from Fargo to Bismarck, and U. S. Highway 2 was closed west of Grand Forks. Across the Devils Lake region, some snow drifts were as tall as one story roof tops. Some motorists became stranded, and had to be rescued. The agricultural impacts were also devastating. Combined with the recent wet conditions (including the 1 to 2 inches of rain from this winter storm), the snow and cold temperatures made it difficult to impossible for farmers to finish their sugar beet and potato harvest.

A line of thunderstorms swept across southeast Minnesota during the late evening of September 2nd into the early morning hours of the 3rd. These storms dropped some large hail and also produced damaging winds. The largest hail size reported was nickel sized in Kasson (Dodge County). The automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County) measured a 59 mph wind gust and a tree limb was blown down onto a house in Dodge Center (Dodge County) causing a power outage.

Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occasional light snow developed during the evening of Thursday, November 28, and continued into December 1, briefly mixing with freezing drizzle. Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches at Windom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the early morning of September 11th. A tornado was spawned out of the storms in Winona (Winona County) and moved through a cemetery causing extensive damage to trees and gravestones. Trees and power lines were also blown down in other parts of the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down in Winona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain moved across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of September 12th. The runoff from the heavy rain prompted officials to close Interstate 90 in Austin (Mower County) when standing water covered all the lanes of traffic, stranding some motorists. Some street flooding also occurred in other parts of Austin. Radar estimates indicate 2 to 4 inches of rain fell across much of Mower County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Lyle, 2.5 inches of rain fell in less than two hours.

A narrow band of moderate to heavy snowfall enhanced over the area during the afternoon and evening of November 30, producing 7.5 inches near Round Lake and 5.5 inches at Worthington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of one to three inches on frozen ground and into a snow pack with between 3 and 6 inches of liquid water equivalent resulted in considerable overland flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding resulted in damage to public infrastructure including county and township roads and culverts. State Highway 30 flooded between 110th and 100th Avenue. The property damage costs include estimates for public property losses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of one to three inches on frozen ground and into a snow pack with between 3 and 6 inches of liquid water equivalent resulted in considerable overland flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All county and township roads were closed to through traffic on March 14. Extensive flood damage was done in many areas. The property damage costs include estimates for public property losses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain moved across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of September 12th. The runoff from the heavy rain prompted officials to close Interstate 90 in Austin (Mower County) when standing water covered all the lanes of traffic, stranding some motorists. Some street flooding also occurred in other parts of Austin. Radar estimates indicate 2 to 4 inches of rain fell across much of Mower County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet equipment at Dobbins Creek measured 4.18 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were multiple waves of precipitation that occurred with this major winter storm that lasted for more than two days. ||The first wave of precipitation developed during the morning of Wednesday, April 10th where the initial band moved from southern Minnesota, northward into central Minnesota, and west central Wisconsin by the afternoon and evening. This band of precipitation had thunder, sleet and heavy snow that produced several inches of snow across portions of southern and central Minnesota. Most of the heavier precipitation tapered off in the southern quarter of Minnesota, with light snow continuing across central Minnesota, during the afternoon and evening of Thursday, April 11th.  ||The second wave of precipitation developed in far southern Minnesota after midnight, Thursday morning, April 11th. This band of precipitation had warmer air aloft which led to more sleet, rain, freezing rain, and thunder. This band was similar to the first with the intensity, but had more mixed precipitation.||By the mid morning, the wave of precipitation had moved out of the region, with much warmer air surging northward ahead of a surface low that moved into southwest Minnesota during the afternoon. There were even some severe thunderstorms that developed near Redwood Falls where one inch hail was reported.||In addition to the precipitation Thursday morning, the next weather element developed. This weather element was a significant increase in the wind speeds, with sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph, with gusts of 50 mph or more across a wide areas of central, and southern Minnesota. This created blizzard conditions in portions of southwest, and west central Minnesota. It would have been worse if the precipitation was in the form of all snow, but warmer air aloft led to more sleet and rain, vs snow. ||The high winds also created structural damage to power poles and trees. Some freezing rain led to fallen tree limbs and power outages from ice build up. The local emergency manager in Freeborn County had more than 300 downed power poles, which affected 6500 residents. The Waseca County emergency manager also reported numerous power poles blown down across the county. The National Guard was requested for Freeborn and Steele Counties of south central Minnesota due to stranded motorists. The Governor of Minnesota declared a state of emergency due to the widespread power outages, and stranded motorists. ||By Thursday afternoon, most of the heavier precipitation and thunderstorms had moved north and east of the area, with mainly light precipitation in the form of drizzle, light snow or sleet in small areas rotating northward as the main storm started to weaken and move overhead.  In addition, wind speeds lessened which led to much better visibility in the west. However, the Minnesota Department of Transportation had already closed or not advised travel for a wide area of west central to southwest Minnesota. ||The third wave of precipitation was associated with the main upper level system and changed the precipitation over to all snow by early Friday morning, April 12th. By the afternoon, most of the precipitation had ended, but residual light snow or flurries continued as the main system moved off to the east.||Snowfall totals as the storm ended ranged from over 18 inches in portions of west central Minnesota, to 6 to 12 inches across a wide area of central and southern Minnesota. Areas along the Iowa border, which had more sleet and rain, received 1 to locally 4 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several waves of precipitation occurred across the county with light to moderate snow at the start, with a mixture of sleet, and snow during the overnight hours of Wednesday/Thursday, before mostly light snow and sleet occurred Thursday, and Thursday night. Wind speeds increased Thursday morning, with significant blowing and drifting snow, causing blizzard conditions for several hours. Wind speeds lessened late Thursday afternoon but light snow redeveloped. Snowfall totals averaged 8 to 12 inches across the county.

Snowfall totals averaged 2 to 4 inches across the county. The most significant weather factor was the combination of high winds and ice coated trees and power lines. This led to widespread downed tree limbs, and power poles. The local emergency manager indicated that up to 300 power poles were blown down during the height of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late March snowmelt flooding continued into early April on the Minnesota River. The river rose above the flood stage of 10 feet to 10.6 feet on April 1st and then fell below flood stage on April 4th.

An area of low pressure over southern Manitoba the morning of the 15th moved to near southern Hudson Bay by the morning of the 16th. A cold front associated with this low moved across the Northland during the afternoon and evening hours of the 15th. A warm front had moved through on the previous day brought a warm and unstable airmass in its wake. The cold front then worked with this airmass to create severe storms from northeast Minnesota into northwest Wisconsin and western Lake Superior. Damaging winds accounted for most of the reports with these storms, although there was some marginally severe hail as well. Most of the damage was to trees, but some property damage to pontoon boats and a home also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was knocked down approximately 5 miles north of Grand Marais along the Gunflint Trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the early afternoon of Thursday, October 10th, surface low pressure was located near Wadena, Minnesota. A stationary boundary extended northward from the low, up toward Baudette, Minnesota. As a result, there were wide temperature differences across the area, with low 50s along the stationary boundary to the lower 30s from Devils Lake to Jamestown. Steady snow fell west of the Red River Valley, while rain or mixed precipitation fell along and east of the Red River Valley. As the low slowly moved toward north central Minnesota by Friday morning, winds turned to the northwest along and west of the Red River Valley, and temperatures dropped to around or below 32 degrees. East of the Red River Valley, winds stayed southwest with temperatures holding in the low to mid 30s. Therefore, snow became the predominant precipitation type for all areas except around the Lake of the Woods region, where it remained rain or mixed precipitation. For the rest of Friday into early Saturday, the low actually spun back westward, or back into northwest Minnesota. This kept steady snow over most of the area, but the least from the Lake of the Woods region to Bemidji to Wadena. As far as storm totals (covering from the 10th to the 12th), areas just west of the Red River Valley into the Devils Lake region had the most snow, up to 30 inches in spots. The Forman to Fargo to Grand Forks to Hallock corridor picked up 1 to 2 inches of rainfall before it turned to snow. Because of this initial rain, snow totals were lower, but this corridor still managed to get 4 to 24 inches of snow, with some mixed precipitation as well. This had huge impacts on the area, including tree damage, power outages, river flooding, and basement flooding.
Rainfall of one to three inches on frozen ground and into a snow pack with between 3 and 6 inches of liquid water equivalent resulted in considerable overland flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Highway 14 between 300th Avenue and County Road 54 was covered by water. Other flooding resulted in damage to public infrastructure including county and township roads and culverts. The property damage costs include estimates for public property losses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of one to three inches on frozen ground and into a snow pack with between 3 and 6 inches of liquid water equivalent resulted in considerable overland flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of numerous county and township roads resulted in damage to road beds and culverts.  Several towns reported street flooding and water impacting lower and poor drainage areas. The property damage costs include estimates for public property losses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt runoff brought flooding on Twelve Mile Creek above Wheaton for a short period of time from April 18th through the 20th. The creek went above the flood stage of 18 feet to 19.7 feet on April 19th.

Late March snowmelt flooding on the Mustinka River near Wheaton slowly fell to below flood stage by April 3rd.  It reached 2.6 feet above the flood stage of 92 feet on March 30th. The river again went temporarily just above flood stage from April 19th through the 21st.

A cold front moved across Minnesota during the day on the 19th. Ahead of the front was a very moist and extremely unstable air mass along with a warm front. Strong wind shear was also in place, especially in the vicinity of the warm front and with a low-level jet pushing into the area as well. These ingredients led to storm development across central Minnesota by mid-afternoon with one storm becoming a dominant supercell that produced very large hail over a wide area, with some of that large hail being driven by winds in excess of 60 mph. As the storm crossed I-35, upwards of 20 cars experienced broken windshields with many of the cars being trapped on the highway as the most intense part of the storm crossed the highway in the middle of a construction zone where traffic was at a standstill due to the construction and heavy weekend vacation traffic from the Minneapolis-St. Paul area. This storm then crossed into Wisconsin and continued to produce large hail and sporadic wind damage, along with a few funnel clouds. Heading into the evening hours, this storm evolved upscale into a large MCS that caused large amounts of damage across parts of Wisconsin. Additional storms were seen ahead of the large supercell that led to additional wind and hail damage as well.

Despite a cool and relatively dry surface layer, conditions were much more favorable for elevated showers and thunderstorms. Good 850mb warm advection, a decent low level jet, and strong shear were expected during the overnight hours. Thunderstorms crossed the border north of Langdon just after midnight on the 5th and tracked to the east-southeast through the pre-dawn hours, staying mainly north of a line from Langdon to Park Rapids.

By mid afternoon of July 8th, temperatures across eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota had risen into the mid to upper 80s with dew points in the mid 60s to low 70s. A stationary boundary set up from west of Bismarck to near Rolla, North Dakota. Thunderstorms initially fired along this boundary, but as the evening wore on, the storms merged into lines, which tracked to the east-southeast, eventually into northwest and west central Minnesota. These storms mainly produced strong winds, but heavy rain was also reported in many areas. The Fargo-Moorhead area picked up quite a bit of rain in a short period of time, which resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One nine inch diameter tree and several large branches were blown down in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: September 2nd was a cool day across eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. Late afternoon temperatures ranged from the upper 50s around the Lake of the Woods region to the lower 70s across portions of southeast North Dakota. Thunderstorms fired between Bismarck and Minot by mid afternoon, then tracked east-southeast during the late afternoon into early evening. A bow echo appeared in the radar imagery between Devils Lake and Jamestown by 430 pm CDT, tracked through the Fargo-Moorhead area around 6 pm, then shifted into west central Minnesota after 7 pm. Winds up to 90 mph and large hail were common along the track of the bow echo. After the bow echo had tracked through, a wake low formed behind it, resulting if a few more damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station measured a 75 mph wind gust before it broke. The station was near Interstate 94 and 34th street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe storms developed in a highly sheared environment. Instability was adequate enough to cause a few of the storms to become tornadic near Silver Lake, and around Forest Lake, and Scandia during the afternoon of Sunday, July 28th. ||A storm survey found a total of six tornadoes. These surveys indicated an EF1 tornado formed near Silver Lake, and another between Forest Lake, north of Scandia, and into southern Chicago County. ||The worst damage occurred along Bone Lake near Scandia, where very large trees were uprooted or blown down. Some of the trees caused significant damage to lake homes on Bone Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado spun up southeast of Silver Lake and quickly became 1/4 mile wide (440 yards), as it tracked northeast across Highway 7 before dissipating 3 miles east-northeast of Silver Lake. Corn was flattened, a few outbuildings were destroyed, and many trees were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic blizzard affected west central Minnesota from April 11th to the 12th. The storm came in two waves. The first wave brought a band of moderate to heavy snow as it lifted from south to north across the region during the morning hours of the 10th. The second wave of heavy snow and strong north winds were with the main surface low pressure area moving across the central plains. The heavy snow in combination with winds gusting to over 40 mph brought blizzard conditions along with heavy drifting. At the storm's end, locations reported very heavy snowfall amounts of 13 to 18 inches. ||The blizzard impacted cattle producers, roads, schools, businesses, and more. This storm also exacerbated the ongoing flooding with the addition of more snowmelt water. Some snowfall amounts include 13 inches at Wheaton and Browns Valley and 18 inches at Artichoke Lake and Ortonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the late evening of July 18th into the early morning of July 19th. As the initial round of storms moved through, a brief tornado touched down east of Witoka (Winona County). The tornado damaged a house and numerous trees. These storms dropped between 3 and 5 inches of rain across portions of Winona and Houston Counties. Runoff from this heavy rain caused a mudslide to occur near Pickwick (Winona County) with a rock slide occurring near Winona (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Ridgeway, between 4.5 and 5 inches of rain fell.

Thunderstorms initially fired along this boundary, but as the evening wore on, the storms merged into lines, which tracked to the east-southeast, eventually into northwest and west central Minnesota. These storms mainly produced strong winds, but heavy rain was also reported in many areas. The Fargo-Moorhead area picked up quite a bit of rain in a short period of time, which resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large tree branches were blown down around town. One tree branch pierced the siding and inside wall of a house and another home had a window blown in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: September 2nd was a cool day across eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. Late afternoon temperatures ranged from the upper 50s around the Lake of the Woods region to the lower 70s across portions of southeast North Dakota. Thunderstorms fired between Bismarck and Minot by mid afternoon, then tracked east-southeast during the late afternoon into early evening. A bow echo appeared in the radar imagery between Devils Lake and Jamestown by 430 pm CDT, tracked through the Fargo-Moorhead area around 6 pm, then shifted into west central Minnesota after 7 pm. Winds up to 90 mph and large hail were common along the track of the bow echo. After the bow echo had tracked through, a wake low formed behind it, resulting if a few more damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The report was relayed through Spotter Network.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the morning of July 20th. The first round produced at 74 mph wind gust at the Rochester International Airport (Olmsted County). The second round of storms blew down trees from near Austin (Mower County) to the northwest side of Rochester. Around 5,000 customers were without power after the storms passed through Rochester. Another 1,000 customers lost power in Mower County. Several streets were blocked in Rochester by downed trees. A fire started by a lightning strike destroyed a house and garage north of Genoa (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester International Airport measured a wind gust of 74 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite a cool and relatively dry surface layer, conditions were much more favorable for elevated showers and thunderstorms. Good 850mb warm advection, a decent low level jet, and strong shear were expected during the overnight hours. Thunderstorms crossed the border north of Langdon just after midnight on the 5th and tracked to the east-southeast through the pre-dawn hours, staying mainly north of a line from Langdon to Park Rapids.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few storms developed ahead of a cold front in a moderately, but weakly sheared environment in northern Minnesota the afternoon and evening of the 25th. These storms pulsed in intensity at times with one storm causing some minor tree damage south of Ely during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down near the intersection of Minnesota Highway 1 and Esterberg Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed ahead of a cold front moving across northern Minnesota on the afternoon of the 26th. These storms contained large hail and damaging wind gusts as they moved across the Minnesota Arrowhead with hail reports up to ping pong ball-sized and trees and power lines downed due to the winds. The storms remained severe as they moved over western Lake Superior and across the Bayfield Peninsula of northwestern Wisconsin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms moved from eastern South Dakota and southwestern Minnesota the morning of the 17th to the Minnesota Arrowhead by the mid-afternoon hours. These elevated storms were able to tap into some elevated instability and briefly become stronger over northeastern St. Louis County. Marginally severe hail was reported along with a few downed trees in the same area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.11 inches near Garretson and 1.70 inches at  Pipestone) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands. 250th Street along the Minnesota/South Dakota border was flooded for numerous days. Split Rock Creek below Jasper (gage site JSPM5) reached the fourth highest stage on record cresting 4.53 feet above flood stage on March 18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of Monday, July 1st in far southern Minnesota. These storms congealed in a small bow echo west of Mankato and moved rapidly eastward. A measured wind gust of 55 mph was recorded northeast of Madelia with these storms. Out ahead of these storms, an isolated storm produced a brief tornado east of Hollandale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser observed a brief touchdown in an open field east of Hollandale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.77 inches near Edgerton and 1.54 inches at Luverne) resulted in flooding of agricultural lands. The Rock River at Luverne (gage site LUVM5) reached a crest 0.47 feet above flood stage on March 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall runoff caused the west branch of Twelve Mile Creek near Dumont to go above the flood stage of 6 feet on July 5th rising to 7.33 feet on July 6th. The creek then fell back below flood stage on July 13th.

A cold front moved across Minnesota during the day on the 19th. Ahead of the front was a very moist and extremely unstable air mass along with a warm front. Strong wind shear was also in place, especially in the vicinity of the warm front and with a low-level jet pushing into the area as well. These ingredients led to storm development across central Minnesota by mid-afternoon with one storm becoming a dominant supercell that produced very large hail over a wide area, with some of that large hail being driven by winds in excess of 60 mph. As the storm crossed I-35, upwards of 20 cars experienced broken windshields with many of the cars being trapped on the highway as the most intense part of the storm crossed the highway in the middle of a construction zone where traffic was at a standstill due to the construction and heavy weekend vacation traffic from the Minneapolis-St. Paul area. This storm then crossed into Wisconsin and continued to produce large hail and sporadic wind damage, along with a few funnel clouds. Heading into the evening hours, this storm evolved upscale into a large MCS that caused large amounts of damage across parts of Wisconsin. Additional storms were seen ahead of the large supercell that led to additional wind and hail damage as well.

Thunderstorms formed over southeast North Dakota and tracked east-northeast into adjacent areas of west central Minnesota. These storms produced numerous tornado and funnel cloud reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent wall cloud produced a brief funnel.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twin funnels moved across the Red River north of Breckenridge and produced at least one damaging touchdown path. This tornado was the second (2B) which formed in this cyclic series. It touched down near highway 75 and tracked northeast, breaking numerous large tree branches in shelterbelts along its path. One farmstead had a few ash and cottonwood trees snapped. Peak winds were estimated at 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain moved across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of September 12th. The runoff from the heavy rain prompted officials to close Interstate 90 in Austin (Mower County) when standing water covered all the lanes of traffic, stranding some motorists. Some street flooding also occurred in other parts of Austin. Radar estimates indicate 2 to 4 inches of rain fell across much of Mower County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused street flooding to occur in Austin. Officials had to close Interstate 90 for about 4 hours because water covered all the lanes of traffic. Some motorists became stranded because of the flooding.

These storms dropped between 2 and 4 inches of rain with the resulting runoff producing some flooding. A campground northwest of Spring Grove (Houston County) had to be evacuated due to flooding along Riceford Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A campground northwest of Spring Grove had to be evacuated because of flooding along Riceford Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of rain fell in the upper basins of the Redwood River from early on July 4 to the morning of July 5.  Runoff resulted in a brief period of minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rainfall of 2 to 3 inches in the Redwood River basin from July 4 into July 5 (2.70 inches at Marshall) produced brief flooding of agricultural land. The Redwood River near Marshall (gage site MSHM5) crested at 0.60 feet above flood stage on July 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of showers and thunderstorms moved slowly across parts of southeast Minnesota during the evening of July 5th. These storms dropped between 2 and 6 inches of rain across the central portions of Dodge County into western Olmsted County. The resulting runoff caused flash flooding to occur in Dodge Center (Dodge County) and near Byron (Olmsted County).  The main impact was water covered roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water covered roads in and near Byron after thunderstorms dropped between 2 and 6 inches of rain. U.S. Highway 14 was closed in Byron after water covered all the lanes of traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of showers and thunderstorms moved slowly across parts of southeast Minnesota during the evening of July 5th. These storms dropped between 2 and 6 inches of rain across the central portions of Dodge County into western Olmsted County. The resulting runoff caused flash flooding to occur in Dodge Center (Dodge County) and near Byron (Olmsted County).  The main impact was water covered roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Kasson, 4.8 inches of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite a cool and relatively dry surface layer, conditions were much more favorable for elevated showers and thunderstorms. Good 850mb warm advection, a decent low level jet, and strong shear were expected during the overnight hours. Thunderstorms crossed the border north of Langdon just after midnight on the 5th and tracked to the east-southeast through the pre-dawn hours, staying mainly north of a line from Langdon to Park Rapids.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Minnesota during the day on the 19th. Ahead of the front was a very moist and extremely unstable air mass along with a warm front. Strong wind shear was also in place, especially in the vicinity of the warm front and with a low-level jet pushing into the area as well. These ingredients led to storm development across central Minnesota by mid-afternoon with one storm becoming a dominant supercell that produced very large hail over a wide area, with some of that large hail being driven by winds in excess of 60 mph. As the storm crossed I-35, upwards of 20 cars experienced broken windshields with many of the cars being trapped on the highway as the most intense part of the storm crossed the highway in the middle of a construction zone where traffic was at a standstill due to the construction and heavy weekend vacation traffic from the Minneapolis-St. Paul area. This storm then crossed into Wisconsin and continued to produce large hail and sporadic wind damage, along with a few funnel clouds. Heading into the evening hours, this storm evolved upscale into a large MCS that caused large amounts of damage across parts of Wisconsin. Additional storms were seen ahead of the large supercell that led to additional wind and hail damage as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail stones punched through a steel garage roof and sheetrock ceiling and damaged a vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By mid afternoon of July 8th, temperatures across eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota had risen into the mid to upper 80s with dew points in the mid 60s to low 70s. A stationary boundary set up from west of Bismarck to near Rolla, North Dakota. Thunderstorms initially fired along this boundary, but as the evening wore on, the storms merged into lines, which tracked to the east-southeast, eventually into northwest and west central Minnesota. These storms mainly produced strong winds, but heavy rain was also reported in many areas. The Fargo-Moorhead area picked up quite a bit of rain in a short period of time, which resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on the northeast shore of Lake Bemidji.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: September 2nd was a cool day across eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. Late afternoon temperatures ranged from the upper 50s around the Lake of the Woods region to the lower 70s across portions of southeast North Dakota. Thunderstorms fired between Bismarck and Minot by mid afternoon, then tracked east-southeast during the late afternoon into early evening. A bow echo appeared in the radar imagery between Devils Lake and Jamestown by 430 pm CDT, tracked through the Fargo-Moorhead area around 6 pm, then shifted into west central Minnesota after 7 pm. Winds up to 90 mph and large hail were common along the track of the bow echo. After the bow echo had tracked through, a wake low formed behind it, resulting if a few more damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large branches were blown down between Cormorant Lake and Lake Maude. Shingles were torn off a roof and a boat launch was flipped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the late evening of July 18th into the early morning of July 19th. As the initial round of storms moved through, a brief tornado touched down east of Witoka (Winona County). The tornado damaged a house and numerous trees. These storms dropped between 3 and 5 inches of rain across portions of Winona and Houston Counties. Runoff from this heavy rain caused a mudslide to occur near Pickwick (Winona County) with a rock slide occurring near Winona (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused mudslides to occur south of Pickwick. The mudslide took down trees and covered County Road 7 between Pickwick and County Road 12. County Road 7 was also covered by floodwaters from Big Trout Creek. A rockslide occurred on Garvin Heights Road in the city of Winona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occasional light snow and freezing drizzle developed during the evening of Thursday, November 28 resulting in a light accumulation of icing before tapering off. Another surge of mixed-type precipitation developed the following afternoon before changing back to snow during the evening of November 30. Snowfall totaled 2.2 inches near Edgerton and 1.0 inches at Rock Rapids.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeast Minnesota sweltered for one day with excessive heat in the middle of July. Heat indices topped out between 105 and 115 on July 19th. Air temperatures during this heat wave generally topped out around 90 while dew points climbed into the lower 80s. The highest calculated heat index was 117 from a mesonet station in Houston (Houston County), near Kellogg (Wabasha County) and the automated weather observing equipment at the Winona airport (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices of 105 to 115 were common across Dodge County on July 19th. The highest calculated heat index was 113 from a mesonet station near Kasson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic blizzard affected west central Minnesota from April 11th to the 12th. The storm came in two waves. The first wave brought a band of moderate to heavy snow as it lifted from south to north across the region during the morning hours of the 10th. The second wave of heavy snow and strong north winds were with the main surface low pressure area moving across the central plains. The heavy snow in combination with winds gusting to over 40 mph brought blizzard conditions along with heavy drifting. At the storm's end, locations reported very heavy snowfall amounts of 13 to 18 inches. ||The blizzard impacted cattle producers, roads, schools, businesses, and more. This storm also exacerbated the ongoing flooding with the addition of more snowmelt water. Some snowfall amounts include 13 inches at Wheaton and Browns Valley and 18 inches at Artichoke Lake and Ortonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal zone sagging slowly southward across the region interacted with a zone of slightly unstable air on its southern flank to produce a band of snowfall, which became heavy at times during several hours between 0800-1300CST. Snowfall was at such a rate (frequent visibility of less than a half mile) that numerous non-injury accidents occurred for many locations near and north of Interstate 90. Snowfall was generally from 3 to 5 inches over a 6 to 9 hour period. Greatest snowfall amounts were reported at Windom and Worthington (4.0 inches), 2 miles northeast of Lakefield (3.9 inches), Marshall (3.7 inches) and Edgerton (3.5 inches).

A couple rounds of storms with locally heavy rains moved across southeast Minnesota at the beginning of July. The runoff from the rain caused the Whitewater River in the Whitewater State Park (Winona County) to go out of its banks twice on July 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed the Whitewater River out of its banks in the Whitewater State Park. The river was above the flood stage for less than hour with a crest almost a foot above the flood stage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from a second heavy rain event pushed the Whitewater River out of its banks in the Whitewater state Park. The river crested around a half foot above the flood stage at 13.85 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the morning of July 20th. The first round produced at 74 mph wind gust at the Rochester International Airport (Olmsted County). The second round of storms blew down trees from near Austin (Mower County) to the northwest side of Rochester. Around 5,000 customers were without power after the storms passed through Rochester. Another 1,000 customers lost power in Mower County. Several streets were blocked in Rochester by downed trees. A fire started by a lightning strike destroyed a house and garage north of Genoa (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 60 mph wind gust occurred on the northwest side of Rochester.

The second round of storms blew down trees from near Austin (Mower County) to the northwest side of Rochester. Around 5,000 customers were without power after the storms passed through Rochester. Another 1,000 customers lost power in Mower County. Several streets were blocked in Rochester by downed trees. A fire started by a lightning strike destroyed a house and garage north of Genoa (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down in Plainview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By mid afternoon of July 8th, temperatures across eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota had risen into the mid to upper 80s with dew points in the mid 60s to low 70s. A stationary boundary set up from west of Bismarck to near Rolla, North Dakota. Thunderstorms initially fired along this boundary, but as the evening wore on, the storms merged into lines, which tracked to the east-southeast, eventually into northwest and west central Minnesota. These storms mainly produced strong winds, but heavy rain was also reported in many areas. The Fargo-Moorhead area picked up quite a bit of rain in a short period of time, which resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large branches were blown down in a tree claim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal zone remained locked in place under southwest flow aloft as a series of mid-level waves moved across the region over a three-day period. Widespread heavy rainfall resulted and amounts reached a two-day record at Pipestone (6.75 inches) as well as totals of nearly 11 inches at Avoca, 9.24 inches at Edgerton, and 6.02 inches at Lake Wilson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage to several county and township roads resulted from significant ponding of water, mainly for areas north of Interstate 90.  Hardest hit were areas near County Roads 65 and 10 in far northern Rock County. Property damage costs are rough estimates. Flooding also resulted in crop losses across the area. Crop loss estimates provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.
