EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occasional light snow developed during the evening of Thursday, November 28, and continued into December 1, briefly mixing with freezing drizzle. Snowfall totaled 1.0 inch at Lake Wilson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked through west central Minnesota during the afternoon of August 13th. Thunderstorms rotated around the low, and one produced a weak tornado near Henning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near the Henning airport and tracked southeast for roughly a quarter of a mile across a cut hay field. The tornado threw hay onto power lines and across a portion of county road 134. Peak winds were estimated at 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms affected the Northland on the 5th with a complex during the morning hours producing wind damage followed by additional development during the afternoon hours as the cold front moved through. Morning storms produced areas of tree damage and downed power lines from the Leech Lake area eastward to the I-35 corridor with recorded gusts of 61 and 62 mph at area airports. After the first complex of storms moved through, conditions again became unstable ahead of the advancing cold front. Additional storms developed ahead of the front, but did not mature and become severe until they crossed into Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and a few power lines were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface boundary, along with weak flow through the near surface layer, contributed to a quick spin up as a storm intensified near Windom, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado spun up and touched down on the west side of Windom, MN near the corner of 13th Street and River Road where it flipped a small pop-up camper.  The tornado tracked northeastward toward the fairgrounds for approximately four city blocks, where it damaged the roof of a barn.  The tornado then made a slightly more eastward turn, causing damage to trees moving along 15th and 16th Streets.  A small metal shed was displaced and small corn crib along Highway 60 holding aluminum cans for recycling was tipped over.  The tornado uprooted a tree near the western shore of Cottonwood Lake, and the tornado was seen travelling over the lake by Cottonwood County Deputies before dissipation.  The average path width was 30 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong and cold area of high pressure surged into the northern Plains in early March. Winds were mainly a gusty 20 to 35 mph, and when coupled with temperatures from 10 below to 20 below zero, produced a long duration of wind chills colder than 20 below zero. Coldest wind chills were recorded between 0400-0900 on March 3, reaching -51 near Oak Lake, -48 at Volga, -47 at Flandreau and White Lake, -46 at Colton and 5 miles south of Hendricks, -45 at Windom, Worthington and near Sioux Falls, and -44 at Huron, Brookings and Madison. March 3 was one of the coldest March days on record for the area, with the coldest or second coldest March high temperatures on record at Huron, Sioux Falls, Mitchell and Sioux City.

A few weak thunderstorms developed near Lake Mille Lacs the evening of the 26th ahead of a cold front. While the overall threat of severe storms was low, one of the storms did produce a waterspout over Farm Island Lake that did produce damage along the shore. This storm produced additional funnel clouds, but no other waterspouts or tornadoes were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The waterspout developed over the northwestern arm of Farm Island Lake east of Johnson's Point and moved to the southeast, passing just offshore of the southwest tip of the point. It then turned a little more easterly and passed near or over the northern tip of First Island and over open water before moving over the southern part of Delicarlia Peninsula. Docks were damaged at this location along with a few boats flipped and tree damage. The waterspout then turned more easterly and moved across Church Camp Bay before reaching shore again, damaging another dock, flipping multiple boats and causing sporadic tree damage. It then turned a but more southeasterly and traveled through a grove of trees and dissipated before reaching US Highway 169.

A few storms developed along a cold front in central Minnesota. While most of the storms remained below severe limits, one storm did reach severe limits in the Brainerd Lakes area and caused some tree damage as it moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house was struck by a positive bolt of lightning which caused a fire that began in the basement. Fire crews were on the scene for about 3 hours and reported a lot of smoke and water damage, all electronics destroyed, holes through the roof and floors, complete loss of the mechanical room and part of the kitchen, making the house most likely a total loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that formed across southern South Dakota during the evening of Saturday, August 17th, quickly moved eastward over far southern Minnesota. These thunderstorms developed a bow echo over southwest Minnesota, and accelerated along the Iowa, Minnesota border during the late evening, before weakening during the early morning hours of Sunday, August 18th. There were numerous reports of high wind along the apex of the bow echo and damage along the path. After midnight, the line of storms weakened and produced only minor tree damage and occasional wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell on a house in Janesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Tuesday, August 13th, severe storms developed along a line of instability across central, and southern Minnesota. Spotters and storm chasers video taped numerous funnel clouds across southern Minnesota>  Three of these funnel clouds briefly touched the ground, but no damage was reported. Some corn was flattened on one of the touchdowns near Ellendale. Some of the storms in the afternoon did produce large hail in Dakota County where ping pong size hail was reported near New Trier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed south of Sunburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface trough moved across eastern Minnesota during the late afternoon, and early evening of Monday, August 26th. Storms developed along the weak surface trough and some produced two tornadoes south of Lake Mille Lacs. Surface instability was around 1000 J/kg.  Wind shear parameters were initially not conducive of tornadic storms, but there was an increase in bulk wind shear values during the afternoon when the tornadoes formed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey indicated that an EF-1 tornado touched down in a field just east of Mille Lacs County Road 4, and tracked to the southeast. EF-1 damage occurred along 70th Avenue where a large pole barn was completely destroyed. A nearby home also saw about half of its roof decking lifted off. Dozens of trees were broken or uprooted. The maximum winds were estimated at 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of Monday, August, 5th, thunderstorms were moving across central Minnesota ahead of a cold front moving southeast. By the late morning, and early afternoon, thunderstorms developed west of the Twin Cities metro area and quickly became severe. Very large hail occurred with these storms, with numerous reports of golf ball size hail. The largest hail stone was near Delano with a measurement of 4 inches in diameter. Damage was extensive along the swath of hail. A special midday upper air sounding was launched and indicated a very high chance of large hail stones. Bulk wind shear parameters of 45 knots and MLCAPES of 3000 J/Kg were adequate to produce these types of large hail stones. ||A secondary line of storms developed during the afternoon northwest of the Twin Cities. These storms produced up to 3 inch diameter hail in Anoka County. As the storms moved farther to the southeast, isolated severe hail stones occurred and wind damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong upper level wave pushed into the western Dakotas during the evening hours, isolated supercellular storms developed across central South Dakota and moved through areas west of the James River. With 2000-2500 J/kg of MLCAPE and deep layer shear of 35 to 45 knots, the isolated storms produced hail up to golf ball size. Soon, an increasing amount of DCAPE of 1000-1250 J/kg and increase in 0-3 km shear with development of a strong low-level jet aided in the transition to enhanced linear structures, which then developed a series of mesovortices as the line moved into far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa. Several brief tornadoes developed with mesovortices in southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, along with areas of enhanced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado formed around 2 miles west-southwest of Hadley, moving northeast through a farmstead shortly after touchdown and damaging trees and partially removing a roof from a metal outbuilding. After crossing Minnesota Highway 30, the tornado struck another farm, causing destruction of a barn, and resulting in damage to the house including blowing in a door and driving a 2x4 through the siding. The tornado reached Hadley three minutes after touchdown. Two garages were destroyed in town, along with damage to numerous trees. One final structure was grazed by the tornado just northeast of Hadley, blowing in the door and damaging the roof of a metal outbuilding. The tornado lifted around 2 miles northeast of Hadley after crossing 121st Street and damaging a power transmission pole. The average path width was 75 yards.

A slow moving and powerful winter storm spread snow across the Northland starting the evening of the 29th and brought a few waves of snow into the afternoon of the 30th. The low then deepened and passed to the south of the region, bringing heavy snowfall and high winds the evening of the 30th into the morning of the 1st. A sustained 10 hour period of blizzard conditions was observed in the Twin Ports with widespread snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches. Heavier amounts to around 2 feet fell downwind of the head of Lake Superior and along the South Shore. Additionally, lakeshore flooding occurred in the Duluth area with a prolonged period of northeast winds pushing water up onto shore and waves causing beach erosion along Park Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The morning of Tuesday, August, 20th, a line of storms moved from central Minnesota, southward into southern Minnesota. One storm developed ahead of this line near Buffalo and quickly became severe with damage to a roof, and a few large trees blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local TV station reported damage to a building, and several large trees uprooted near Buffalo. These reports were relayed to the NWS through social media after the damage occurred.

A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall with a late winter storm drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With 1 to 3 inches of snow water equivalent still in the snow pack south of Interstate 90 and 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90, gradually warming temperatures, and the frozen impervious ground, area rivers crested a second time due to additional runoff about a week after the first rapid crests.  Overall, fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.15 inches at Windom) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands and Island Park in Windom. The Des Moines River at Windom (gage site WDOM5) reached the third highest stage on record cresting 4.01 feet above flood stage on March 23.  The river remained in minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall with a late winter storm drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With 1 to 3 inches of snow water equivalent still in the snow pack south of Interstate 90 and 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90, gradually warming temperatures, and the frozen impervious ground, area rivers crested a second time due to additional runoff about a week after the first rapid crests.  Overall, fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.22 inches at Flandreau and 1.70 inches at Pipestone) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural land and inundation of the city golf course in Flandreau. Flandreau Creek above Flandreau (gage site FCRS2) crested 1.88 feet above flood stage on March 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed during the evening along an outflow boundary from earlier day convection across southwest Minnesota southwest of Marshall.  The initial storm development produced a weak funnel cloud, but the main impact was from a couple hours of torrential rainfall which produced small stream flooding.  Later in the night, a well organized linear structure swept eastward into southwest Minnesota, producing an isolated severe wind event just across the border from South Dakota along Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Creeks flooding with water standing in some fields along U.S. Highway 14.

Snowmelt flooding that began in late March continued throughout April across west central Minnesota. A mid-April blizzard only added to the flooding problems, due to additional large amounts of snowmelt water. Many roads along with cropland remained or became flooded. This flooding and delayed planting continued into May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted northeast from the Texas Panhandle early on March 9, reaching the western Great Lakes as an intense closed low. This system included a leading surge of warm air, allowing precipitation to become rain or light freezing rain over a large area, with many locations receiving a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch ice accumulation before colder air wrapped in behind and transitioned precipitation to snowfall. Brief periods of heavier snowfall due to marginally unstable air off the surface led to accumulations of 2 to 6 inches north of Interstate 90 along with travel disruptions, with generally a trace to 2 inches south; the exception being parts of northwest Iowa which received 2 to 4 inches. As the surface low pressure moved across Iowa by later in the afternoon and evening of March 9, northwest winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of freezing rain from 0500-0900CST brought up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation.  Snowfall followed with visibility briefly reduced to 1/2 mile between 1200-1600, with total accumulation of 2.5 inches 2 miles northeast of Lakefield.  Northwest winds increased by evening and gusted as high as 41 mph at Worthington and 36 mph 3 miles south southeast of Lake Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon of Saturday, August 10th, isolated thunderstorms developed in southwest Minnesota. These storms moved into an area of a highly sheared environment, but weak instability. However, due to boundary layer winds backing ahead of these storms, a brief tornado developed southeast of Fairmont. No damage occurred as it touched down in an open field.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser was 5 miles southeast of Fairmont, and reported a brief touchdown 1 mile northwest of his location in an open field. No damage was reported, but it appeared visually to have been weak.

As a strong upper level wave pushed into the western Dakotas during the evening hours, isolated supercellular storms developed across central South Dakota and moved through areas west of the James River. With 2000-2500 J/kg of MLCAPE and deep layer shear of 35 to 45 knots, the isolated storms produced hail up to golf ball size. Soon, an increasing amount of DCAPE of 1000-1250 J/kg and increase in 0-3 km shear with development of a strong low-level jet aided in the transition to enhanced linear structures, which then developed a series of mesovortices as the line moved into far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa. Several brief tornadoes developed with mesovortices in southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, along with areas of enhanced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado spun up a mile and a half northwest of Reading, uprooting a tree and taking down several other branches. While most of the damage along the path toward the east-northeast was to trees and crops, one house had some minor damage with removal of a section of roofing material.  The average path width was 40 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense area of low pressure approached the region through the day on Monday, October 21st, producing increasingly strong east to northeast winds. The winds increased through the day, and were especially strong coming off Lake Superior into the Twin Ports area. Peak wind speeds topped 70 MPH at some of the bridges and waves caused damage along many of the lake shore areas. In addition, the strong winds knocked down trees and power lines down leading to power outages. The Duluth Water Level Observation Station located in the Duluth harbor operated by NOAA/National Ocean Service broke an all-time record at 604.75 feet topping the previous record of 604.42 feet recorded in 1985. Winds gusted to 61 mph at the Duluth International Airport (KDLH) with a peak gust of 74 mph recorded on the Blatnik Bridge in the Twin Ports. Fairly widespread rains of 1 to over 3 inches of rain fell leading to some ponding, localized flooding, and rises on area rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds at the sensor on the Blatnik Bridge gusted to 58 mph or greater starting at 12PM and lasted through 6PM with a peak gust of 74 mph recorded just after 4PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted northeast from the Texas Panhandle early on March 9, reaching the western Great Lakes as an intense closed low. This system included a leading surge of warm air, allowing precipitation to become rain or light freezing rain over a large area, with many locations receiving a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch ice accumulation before colder air wrapped in behind and transitioned precipitation to snowfall. Brief periods of heavier snowfall due to marginally unstable air off the surface led to accumulations of 2 to 6 inches north of Interstate 90 along with travel disruptions, with generally a trace to 2 inches south; the exception being parts of northwest Iowa which received 2 to 4 inches. As the surface low pressure moved across Iowa by later in the afternoon and evening of March 9, northwest winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of freezing rain from 0500-0900CST brought up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation.  Snowfall followed with visibility occasionally reduced to 1/4 to 1/2 mile between 1100-1800, with total accumulations of 9.0 inches at Minneota, 6.0 inches at Tracy and 5.0 inches at Marshall. Northwest winds increased by later evening, with a peak gust of 40 mph at Marshall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Tuesday, August 13th, severe storms developed along a line of instability across central, and southern Minnesota. Spotters and storm chasers video taped numerous funnel clouds across southern Minnesota>  Three of these funnel clouds briefly touched the ground, but no damage was reported. Some corn was flattened on one of the touchdowns near Ellendale. Some of the storms in the afternoon did produce large hail in Dakota County where ping pong size hail was reported near New Trier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the large hail stone, there was a power pole blown down in Waseca.

A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall with a late winter storm drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With 1 to 3 inches of snow water equivalent still in the snow pack south of Interstate 90 and 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90, gradually warming temperatures, and the frozen impervious ground, area rivers crested a second time due to additional runoff about a week after the first rapid crests.  Overall, fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.15 inches at Windom) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands and Island Park in Windom. The Des Moines River at Windom (gage site WDOM5) reached the third highest stage on record cresting 4.01 feet above flood stage on March 23. The river remained in minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that formed across southern South Dakota during the evening of Saturday, August 17th, quickly moved eastward over far southern Minnesota. These thunderstorms developed a bow echo over southwest Minnesota, and accelerated along the Iowa, Minnesota border during the late evening, before weakening during the early morning hours of Sunday, August 18th. There were numerous reports of high wind along the apex of the bow echo and damage along the path. After midnight, the line of storms weakened and produced only minor tree damage and occasional wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree blew down near Butterfield and caused a gas leak. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continuation of a severe weather episode at the end of June, thunderstorms propagated along a nearly stationary frontal boundary from southeast South Dakota into southwest Minnesota.  One storm briefly strengthened and produced an area of tree and crop damage west of Marshall, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree uprooted with areas of corn blown over.

Peak wind speeds topped 70 MPH at some of the bridges and waves caused damage along many of the lake shore areas. In addition, the strong winds knocked down trees and power lines down leading to power outages. The Duluth Water Level Observation Station located in the Duluth harbor operated by NOAA/National Ocean Service broke an all-time record at 604.75 feet topping the previous record of 604.42 feet recorded in 1985. Winds gusted to 61 mph at the Duluth International Airport (KDLH) with a peak gust of 74 mph recorded on the Blatnik Bridge in the Twin Ports. Fairly widespread rains of 1 to over 3 inches of rain fell leading to some ponding, localized flooding, and rises on area rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large waves in excess of 12 feet and potentially record high water levels led to water pushing ashore into the Canal Park and Park Point areas. Large logs and rocks were being pushed up onto the beach along these locations as well. Access to Park Point over the Duluth Aerial Lift Bridge was suspended for a time due to water over the roadway on the bridge approach. Damage occurred to homes and property along Park Point as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.70 inches at Pipestone) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural lands. Initial flooding was sporadic and minor, but warming later in the month increased the runoff and impact of flooding. The North Branch of Pipestone Creek near Pipestone (gage site PSNM5) reached the highest stage on record cresting 1.24 feet above flood stage on March 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of Monday, August, 5th, thunderstorms were moving across central Minnesota ahead of a cold front moving southeast. By the late morning, and early afternoon, thunderstorms developed west of the Twin Cities metro area and quickly became severe. Very large hail occurred with these storms, with numerous reports of golf ball size hail. The largest hail stone was near Delano with a measurement of 4 inches in diameter. Damage was extensive along the swath of hail. A special midday upper air sounding was launched and indicated a very high chance of large hail stones. Bulk wind shear parameters of 45 knots and MLCAPES of 3000 J/Kg were adequate to produce these types of large hail stones. ||A secondary line of storms developed during the afternoon northwest of the Twin Cities. These storms produced up to 3 inch diameter hail in Anoka County. As the storms moved farther to the southeast, isolated severe hail stones occurred and wind damage was reported.

As a strong upper level wave pushed into the western Dakotas during the evening hours, isolated supercellular storms developed across central South Dakota and moved through areas west of the James River. With 2000-2500 J/kg of MLCAPE and deep layer shear of 35 to 45 knots, the isolated storms produced hail up to golf ball size. Soon, an increasing amount of DCAPE of 1000-1250 J/kg and increase in 0-3 km shear with development of a strong low-level jet aided in the transition to enhanced linear structures, which then developed a series of mesovortices as the line moved into far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa. Several brief tornadoes developed with mesovortices in southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, along with areas of enhanced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The track of this tornado followed generally eastward along 181st Street between 140th and 150th Avenues. The tornado started with a slight south of east track, flattening corn and damaging trees in a shelter belt.  The tornado then began to move more eastward, striking and destroying an old barn around a quarter mile from the touchdown point.  The remainder of the track was across cornfields, crossing north of 181st Street before lifting.  The average path width was 40 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall with a late winter storm drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With 1 to 3 inches of snow water equivalent still in the snow pack south of Interstate 90 and 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90, gradually warming temperatures, and the frozen impervious ground, area rivers crested a second time due to additional runoff about a week after the first rapid crests.  Overall, fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.42 inches 2 miles northeast of Lakefield and 1.15 inches at Windom) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands and park areas in Jackson. The Des Moines River at Jackson (gage site JCKM5) crested 2.08 feet above flood stage on March 24. The river remained in minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that formed across southern South Dakota during the evening of Saturday, August 17th, quickly moved eastward over far southern Minnesota. These thunderstorms developed a bow echo over southwest Minnesota, and accelerated along the Iowa, Minnesota border during the late evening, before weakening during the early morning hours of Sunday, August 18th. There were numerous reports of high wind along the apex of the bow echo and damage along the path. After midnight, the line of storms weakened and produced only minor tree damage and occasional wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large tree limbs were blown down in Winnebago.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving and powerful winter storm spread snow across the Northland starting the evening of the 29th and brought a few waves of snow into the afternoon of the 30th. The low then deepened and passed to the south of the region, bringing heavy snowfall and high winds the evening of the 30th into the morning of the 1st. A sustained 10 hour period of blizzard conditions was observed in the Twin Ports with widespread snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches. Heavier amounts to around 2 feet fell downwind of the head of Lake Superior and along the South Shore. Additionally, lakeshore flooding occurred in the Duluth area with a prolonged period of northeast winds pushing water up onto shore and waves causing beach erosion along Park Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late March snowmelt flooding on Twelve Mile Creek near Dumont continued off and on into April, going above flood stage several times through April 19th. The largest degree of flooding during this time was 1.8 feet above the flood stage of 10 feet on April 18th.

Snowmelt flooding that began in late March continued throughout April across west central Minnesota. A mid-April blizzard only added to the flooding problems, due to additional large amounts of snowmelt water. Many roads along with cropland remained or became flooded. This flooding and delayed planting continued into May.

Snowmelt flooding caused the Bois De Sioux River to go above the flood stage of 80 feet on April 5th to 80.5 feet on April 7th, before falling back to below flood stage on April 9th. The river rose slightly above flood stage again from April 23rd to April 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted northeast from the Texas Panhandle early on March 9, reaching the western Great Lakes as an intense closed low. This system included a leading surge of warm air, allowing precipitation to become rain or light freezing rain over a large area, with many locations receiving a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch ice accumulation before colder air wrapped in behind and transitioned precipitation to snowfall. Brief periods of heavier snowfall due to marginally unstable air off the surface led to accumulations of 2 to 6 inches north of Interstate 90 along with travel disruptions, with generally a trace to 2 inches south; the exception being parts of northwest Iowa which received 2 to 4 inches. As the surface low pressure moved across Iowa by later in the afternoon and evening of March 9, northwest winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of freezing rain from 0500-0900CST brought up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation.  Snowfall followed with visibility briefly reduced to 1/4 to 1/2 mile between 1100-1500, with total accumulations of 5.1 inches at Edgerton and 2.8 inches near Garretson.  Northwest winds increased by evening and gusted as high as 42 mph near Beaver Creek and 37 mph at Luverne.

During the afternoon of Tuesday, August 13th, severe storms developed along a line of instability across central, and southern Minnesota. Spotters and storm chasers video taped numerous funnel clouds across southern Minnesota>  Three of these funnel clouds briefly touched the ground, but no damage was reported. Some corn was flattened on one of the touchdowns near Ellendale. Some of the storms in the afternoon did produce large hail in Dakota County where ping pong size hail was reported near New Trier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were two separate reports of a funnel cloud near White Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe hail fell in an area of storms that developed across southwest Minnesota during the evening of Thursday, August 15th. One of the storms produced half dollar size hail southeast of Redwood Falls, near Morgan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a social media image of half dollar size hail that fell near Morgan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted northeast from the Texas Panhandle early on March 9, reaching the western Great Lakes as an intense closed low. This system included a leading surge of warm air, allowing precipitation to become rain or light freezing rain over a large area, with many locations receiving a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch ice accumulation before colder air wrapped in behind and transitioned precipitation to snowfall. Brief periods of heavier snowfall due to marginally unstable air off the surface led to accumulations of 2 to 6 inches north of Interstate 90 along with travel disruptions, with generally a trace to 2 inches south; the exception being parts of northwest Iowa which received 2 to 4 inches. As the surface low pressure moved across Iowa by later in the afternoon and evening of March 9, northwest winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of freezing rain from 0500-0900CST brought up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation.  Snowfall followed with visibility occasionally reduced to 1/4 to 1/2 mile between 1100-1800, with total accumulations of 7.0 inches at Lake Benton. Northwest winds increased by evening, with a peak gust of 37 mph near Lake Benton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two storm chasers filmed a tornado in east central Minnesota, in Kanabec County. Earlier, a funnel cloud was documented northeast of St Cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser documented a funnel cloud. It was not possible from the video to determine whether it touched down, and there were no reports of damage, so it was deemed to have been a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of severe storms rolled across southeast Minnesota during the evening of September 24th. These storms produced a tornado that moved through Lake City (Wabasha County). Trees and building roofs were damaged along with dry docked boats at the marina. Wind gusts were estimated to be in the 70 to 80 mph range in Zumbro Falls (Wabasha County). Trees were blown down in and near Rochester (Olmsted County), in Lewiston (Winona County) and in Winona (Winona County) where the roof of a restaurant was also damaged. Damage from the storms caused numerous power outages with 1,200 losing power in Winona, 1,400 across western Wabasha County, between 800 and 900 in Rochester and around 300 across Dodge County. A local disaster declaration was made in Lake City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down in Goodview. The downed trees blocked some roads. Some trees were started on fire by the downed power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High water levels from April continued at the start of May 2019 for many locations along the Big Sioux, James, and Missouri Rivers. For many locations, this started the third month of constant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of flooding from April. The Des Moines River near Avoca (gage site AVOM5) had an early month crest at 1.04 feet above flood stage on May 1, before falling below flood stage on May 13. Significant agricultural flooding persisted, with no opportunity for spring planting throughout the flood plain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front set up over the region during the early morning hours of the 30th. Torrential rainfall was reported overnight leading to saturated soils in the area. Additional showers and thunderstorms moved over these same areas during the late morning and early afternoon hours and led to isolated areas of flash flooding where water over the roadway led to closed roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Highway 18 was closed at the Snake River due to water over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated instability from 1000-2000 J/kg along with 40-50 kt deep-layer shear sustained organized convection pushing though southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa. Winds averaged from 60 to 80 mph along the path of the large scale bow echo, producing significant damage to trees and power lines, and minor damage to buildings along the path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over Wyoming and Montana on the 8th and moved eastward across the Dakotas and into northeastern Iowa on the morning of the 9th, setting up an area of snow to the north of the low that passed across the Northland. Snow began the evening of the 8th and ended for most the afternoon of the 9th, although lake effect snows of Lake Superior lasted into the 10th before ending. Snowfall totals of 6 to 8 inches were common across much of northeastern Minnesota with a high of 10 in the Minnesota Arrowhead.

During the afternoon of Tuesday, September, 24th, scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front moving across southern Minnesota. Storms quickly formed over northern Iowa, and moved northeast across south central and east central Minnesota during the early evening. There were a couple measured wind gusts of over 60 mph as the thunderstorms moved across the area. However, a lot of the winds were sub-severe with gusts of 40 to 50 mph which occasionally produced minor tree damage. ||As the storms continued to move across southern Minnesota, they congealed into a broken line and caused large hail and straight-line wind damage in east central and southeast Minnesota.

A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed across North Dakota during the afternoon of Monday, September 2nd. This line of storms continued to move southeast across west central, central, and southern Minnesota during the evening. Some parts of the line had bow echo segments and produced a couple of tornadoes. One tornado occurred near Howard Lake, and another east of Watertown, on the western edge of the Twin Cities metro area. There were also numerous reports of straight-line winds up to 70 mph along this bow echo segment, plus several large hail reports. These storms continued to move southeast into portions of west central Wisconsin before weakening.

A subtle wave in northwest flow aloft moved across southwest Minnesota toward early evening as a warm frontal zone crawled northward toward the Minnesota-Iowa border.  An isolated storm developed across southwest Minnesota and intensified in an environment with near 1000 J/kg MLCAPE.  With deep shear at only around 30 knots, the storm did not maintain intensity for long, dissipating as it drifted southeast into northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds also estimated around 50 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey determined an EF0 tornado developed east of Watertown near County Road 20, and moved southeast into Hennepin County, north of Timber Creek Golf Course. Most of the damage was consistent with EF0 damage with numerous trees blown down or uprooted along its path.  The path length in Carver County was 1.3 miles, with a maximum width of 90 yards. The tornado was also confirmed by the MPX radar in real time given the Tornado Debris Signature sampled by the radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of low pressure moved across the Upper Midwest, thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of Memorial Day (May 27th) across portions of southeast Minnesota. These storms quickly became severe and produced four tornadoes across Fillmore County. The first tornado moved into the southern part of the county from Iowa and damaged some farm buildings and trees along its path as it moved toward Greenleafton. The other three tornadoes were just brief touchdowns over the western portions of the county south and east of Spring Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed the Whitewater River out of its banks in Whitewater River State Park. The river crested a foot above the flood stage at 14.54 feet.

A line of thunderstorms swept across southeast Minnesota during the late evening of September 2nd into the early morning hours of the 3rd. These storms dropped some large hail and also produced damaging winds. The largest hail size reported was nickel sized in Kasson (Dodge County). The automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County) measured a 59 mph wind gust and a tree limb was blown down onto a house in Dodge Center (Dodge County) causing a power outage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Dodge Center, a tree limb was blown down onto a house causing a power outage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the early morning of September 11th. A tornado was spawned out of the storms in Winona (Winona County) and moved through a cemetery causing extensive damage to trees and gravestones. Trees and power lines were also blown down in other parts of the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A short-lived tornado moved through Woodlawn Cemetery in Winona. The tornado caused extensive damage to trees and gravestones. The roof of a shed was also damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving and powerful winter storm spread snow across the Northland starting the evening of the 29th and brought a few waves of snow into the afternoon of the 30th. The low then deepened and passed to the south of the region, bringing heavy snowfall and high winds the evening of the 30th into the morning of the 1st. A sustained 10 hour period of blizzard conditions was observed in the Twin Ports with widespread snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches. Heavier amounts to around 2 feet fell downwind of the head of Lake Superior and along the South Shore. Additionally, lakeshore flooding occurred in the Duluth area with a prolonged period of northeast winds pushing water up onto shore and waves causing beach erosion along Park Point.

Snow began the evening of the 8th and ended for most the afternoon of the 9th, although lake effect snows of Lake Superior lasted into the 10th before ending. Snowfall totals of 6 to 8 inches were common across much of northeastern Minnesota with a high of 10 in the Minnesota Arrowhead.

As an area of low pressure moved across the Upper Midwest, thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of Memorial Day (May 27th) across portions of southeast Minnesota. These storms quickly became severe and produced four tornadoes across Fillmore County. The first tornado moved into the southern part of the county from Iowa and damaged some farm buildings and trees along its path as it moved toward Greenleafton. The other three tornadoes were just brief touchdowns over the western portions of the county south and east of Spring Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down near Etna and uprooted some trees at a cemetery.

Another two farms also sustained some minor damage to buildings and a few homes sustained minor wind and tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across northern Minnesota the night of the 13th into the 14th. An area of convergence set up along the North Shore of Lake Superior leading to a band of heavy lake effect snow that slowly moved up the shore. A narrow swath of 6 inches was seen along the shore with higher amounts to around 9 inches just up the terrain ridge from the shoreline.

Cold Canadian high pressure settled over the Northland the morning of the 18th with clear skies that allowed for excellent radiational cooling conditions. As the sun came up and the atmosphere began to mix, there was a brief period where winds combined with the very cold temperatures to produce dangerous wind chills.

During the afternoon of Tuesday, September, 24th, scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front moving across southern Minnesota. Storms quickly formed over northern Iowa, and moved northeast across south central and east central Minnesota during the early evening. There were a couple measured wind gusts of over 60 mph as the thunderstorms moved across the area. However, a lot of the winds were sub-severe with gusts of 40 to 50 mph which occasionally produced minor tree damage. ||As the storms continued to move across southern Minnesota, they congealed into a broken line and caused large hail and straight-line wind damage in east central and southeast Minnesota.

A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed across North Dakota during the afternoon of Monday, September 2nd. This line of storms continued to move southeast across west central, central, and southern Minnesota during the evening. Some parts of the line had bow echo segments and produced a couple of tornadoes. One tornado occurred near Howard Lake, and another east of Watertown, on the western edge of the Twin Cities metro area. There were also numerous reports of straight-line winds up to 70 mph along this bow echo segment, plus several large hail reports. These storms continued to move southeast into portions of west central Wisconsin before weakening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind report from a private weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With water levels still high from the spring snowmelt flooding, another round of flooding occurred on the Mississippi River in late May from runoff after a couple of rainfall events. The flooding continued into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Sunday, Sept. 15th, the local police department reported that an individual had disappeared in the Redwood River near the 100 block of West Bridge Street. Several juveniles had been swimming in the river when they reported one in the group, had been caught in the current and swept away. This river was above flood stage and had a river flood warning for the Redwood River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local police department reported that an individual had disappeared in the Redwood River near the 100 block of West Bridge Street and was presumed dead from drowning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the late afternoon of the 20th, a weak area of surface low pressure set up near Devils Lake, with the warm front extending out to the east-southeast. Temperatures had risen into the mid 70s to low 80s in the warm sector, with dew points in the upper 60s to lower 70s. To the southwest of the low, a cold front extended back toward Bismarck. Just before 5 pm CDT, a fairly solid line of thunderstorms developed along the cold front, while a smaller cluster formed around Cavalier. By around 6 pm CDT, another cluster of storms developed west of Fargo. Most of this thunderstorm activity had shifted north of the highway 200 corridor by 10 pm, although the most intense rainfall occurred along the southern edge of this area. The New Rockford to Grand Forks/East Grand Forks to Roseau areas picked up 2 to 5 inches of rain in a short duration, prompting flash flood warnings in those areas. Off and on steady rainfall continued into Saturday morning the 21st, before the rain started to end from southwest to northeast. Many of the flash flood warnings were transitioned to areal flood warnings, as the heavy rain ran off into the local drainage and river systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streets, low spots, rivers, and basements were flooded by 4 to 9 inches of rain over a short duration of time. Cars that got stuck in the flood waters were abandoned where they stalled. Farm fields and access roads were also flooded.

Winds averaged from 60 to 80 mph along the path of the large scale bow echo, producing significant damage to trees and power lines, and minor damage to buildings along the path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain moved across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of September 12th. The runoff from the heavy rain prompted officials to close Interstate 90 in Austin (Mower County) when standing water covered all the lanes of traffic, stranding some motorists. Some street flooding also occurred in other parts of Austin. Radar estimates indicate 2 to 4 inches of rain fell across much of Mower County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house fire near Rollingstone was started by lightning. The house sustained minimal damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the continuation of the Carver County tornado that moved into Hennepin County northeast of Timber Creek Golf Course.  The storm survey determined an EF1 tornado occurred in Hennepin County where trees, crops, and a few machine sheds or outbuildings were damaged or blown down. ||The path length in Hennepin County was 2.5 miles, making the tornado total path length of 3.87 miles. The maximum width in Hennepin County was 210 yards, with a maximum wind speed of 90 mph. ||This tornado was also confirmed by the MPX radar in real time given the Tornado Debris Signature sampled by the radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter weather commenced late evening on Thursday, November 28 with a mix of light snow and freezing rain, which produced up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation before surface temperatures warmed above freezing on the morning of November 30. A narrow band of moderate to heavy snowfall enhanced over the area during the afternoon and evening of November 30, producing 8.0 inches 8 miles east of Round Lake and 3.7 inches 2 miles northeast of Lakefield.  Well after the event ended, at 1437CST December 1, a vehicle travelling westbound on Interstate 90 at mile marker 79 slid into the median and rolled twice, injuring (indirect) the driver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two storm chasers filmed a tornado in east central Minnesota, in Kanabec County. Earlier, a funnel cloud was documented northeast of St Cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two storm chasers documented a tornado that mostly crossed open fields. A NWS storm survey revealed damage to some bean fields as well as about two dozen trees uprooted or broken. No structural damage was noted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The city of Wanamingo was hit hard with severe wind gusts. There were numerous full size trees blown down, branches everywhere, and other assorted damage.  There was damage to one mobile home, plus damage to a few cars (broken windshields and dents) from falling trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused the West Branch of 12-Mile Creek near Dumont to flood for about a week in mid-September. The creek rose above the flood stage of 6 feet on the 11th to 8.7 feet on the 14th and then back below flood stage on the 18th.

A couple rounds of severe storms rolled across southeast Minnesota during the evening of September 24th. These storms produced a tornado that moved through Lake City (Wabasha County). Trees and building roofs were damaged along with dry docked boats at the marina. Wind gusts were estimated to be in the 70 to 80 mph range in Zumbro Falls (Wabasha County). Trees were blown down in and near Rochester (Olmsted County), in Lewiston (Winona County) and in Winona (Winona County) where the roof of a restaurant was also damaged. Damage from the storms caused numerous power outages with 1,200 losing power in Winona, 1,400 across western Wabasha County, between 800 and 900 in Rochester and around 300 across Dodge County. A local disaster declaration was made in Lake City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather observing equipment at the Austin airport measured a wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front set up over the region during the early morning hours of the 30th. Torrential rainfall was reported overnight leading to saturated soils in the area. Additional showers and thunderstorms moved over these same areas during the late morning and early afternoon hours and led to isolated areas of flash flooding where water over the roadway led to closed roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood water from the Namadji River led to a lane closure on Minnesota Highway 23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High water levels from April continued at the start of May 2019 for many locations along the Big Sioux, James, and Missouri Rivers. For many locations, this started the third month of constant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of flooding from April. Split Rock Creek below Jasper (gage site JSPM5) saw flooding slowly end at the start of the month.  Starting the month at 0.51 feet above flood stage, the river fell below flood stage on May 2.  Low water crossings on 250th and 251st Streets on the Minnesota/South Dakota border were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed across North Dakota during the afternoon of Monday, September 2nd. This line of storms continued to move southeast across west central, central, and southern Minnesota during the evening. Some parts of the line had bow echo segments and produced a couple of tornadoes. One tornado occurred near Howard Lake, and another east of Watertown, on the western edge of the Twin Cities metro area. There were also numerous reports of straight-line winds up to 70 mph along this bow echo segment, plus several large hail reports. These storms continued to move southeast into portions of west central Wisconsin before weakening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow spread into eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota during the late afternoon and early evening of December 8th and continued into the morning of December 9th. Late in the afternoon of the 8th, temperatures ranged from zero at Devils Lake, to 12 degrees at Grand Forks and Fargo, to 22 degrees at Park Rapids. By mid morning of December 9th, temperatures had dropped to -13 degrees at Devils Lake, -5 degrees at Fargo, and 4 degrees at Park Rapids. East central North Dakota into adjacent areas of northwest Minnesota picked up 6 to 10 inches of snow. A little further south, or along the Interstate 94 corridor in North Dakota and the highway 10 corridor in northwest Minnesota, picked up a little mixed precipitation, 4 to 6 inches of snow, and had wind gusts to 35 mph.

A Colorado Low took shape during the early morning hours of Saturday, December 28th, with an inverted trough extending up toward the Red River Valley. During the day, the surface low tracked into eastern Nebraska, but the inverted trough remained over the valley. This allowed snow to spread slowly from the South Dakota border up to the highway 2 corridor. It was a very mild day, with high temperatures in the low 30s over southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota, to the lower 20s in the Devils Lake region. Temperatures did not change much Saturday night, as the surface low tracked from eastern Nebraska up into north central Iowa. Meanwhile, the snow finally reached the Canadian border, and north to northeast winds continued to increase. From Sunday into Sunday night, the surface low lingered over southwest Minnesota, before it finally began to fill. This kept a prolonged period of gusty north winds and steady snow over eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. From 5 to 17 inches of snow fell across the area, with the highest totals generally over the central Red River Valley into portions of southeast North Dakota. Winds along and west of the Red River Valley also gusted from 40 to 50 mph, causing blizzard conditions from Sunday morning through Monday morning. Interstate 29 was closed in eastern North Dakota and Interstate 94 was closed from Fargo to Bismarck.

During the early morning of Saturday, December 28th, a large storm system began to develop across the southern plains. A surge of warm air initially produced a band of sleet, freezing rain and snow across southern Minnesota. The far western part of Minnesota was cold enough to keep the precipitation in the form of snow with a quick one to two inches of snow by 9 am Saturday along the South Dakota, and Minnesota border. The next band of precipitation developed Saturday evening, and moved northward during the night. By Sunday morning, a band of 6 to 9 inches of snow fell in Yellow Medicine, Lac Qui Parle and the western portions of Swift, Stevens and Chippewa Counties by noon Sunday. Some of the higher snowfall totals include 10 inches near Boyd in far southern Lac Qui Parle County, and 9 inches at both Canby, and Madison.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather observer reported snowfall amounts of 5 to 6 inches near Milan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from the Southern Plains to northern Iowa the 28th into the 29th and spread snowfall across northern Minnesota starting the morning of the 28th. Snow was moderate to heavy at times before warmer air aloft worked in and changed snow to rain, freezing rain and sleet. Snowfall totals of 6 to 8 inches were seen around the region before the changeover with higher amounts to around 9 inches in far northern areas where snow persisted longer. The heavy, wet snow caused a roof to partially collapse at a soft drink bottling plant. Additionally, icing was seen after the snow leading to a layer of ice on top of snow covered roads leading to dangerous driving conditions. Strong northeast winds and high waves along the North Shore of Lake Superior also led to flooding in downtown Grand Marais.

A strong winter storm created numerous impacts across west central Minnesota from December 28th into the 30th. The heaviest precipitation came in two waves, with the first wave arriving during the early morning of the 28th ending by noon.  Behind this first wave of heavy snowfall, there was a period of light snow, drizzle, and freezing drizzle.  The second wave of precipitation was all snow and arrived on the evening of December 28th ending early on December 30th. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 11 inches across west central Minnesota. Some snowfall amounts include, 6 inches at Wheaton; 8 inches at Artichoke Lake; 10 inches at Browns Valley; and 11 inches at Ortonville.

Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST. There were a total of 6 injuries (indirect) and one fatality (indirect) during the event. See November 2019 Storm Data episode for more information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 5.0 inches at Tracy, 4.9 inches at Minneota and 4.6 inches at Marshall. At 1500CST December 1, a vehicle on Highway 23 near County Road 22 lost control on the icy roadway and slid into another vehicle which was stopped on the shoulder, injuring (indirect) the driver of the moving vehicle. See the 11/28 Winter Weather event at 1800CST for more details.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough across the southwestern U.S. in the early morning of Saturday, December 28 pushed eastward through the day, allowing low pressure to organize near the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles. The storm intensified as it moved into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa by 0600CST December 29. Strong warming aloft ahead of the system led to periods of light rain or light mixed precipitation, while heavy snow organized in the trowal over central and northeast South Dakota.||Impacts from the storm became significant as the storm closed off in the mid-levels and slowed movement, only reaching southern Minnesota by 0600CST December 30. Precipitation changed over to snow as colder air collapsed southward, and winds increased to a gusty 40 to 55 mph across southeast South Dakota and adjacent areas. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storms congealed in central Minnesota during the late afternoon, and early evening across Morrison County. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Colorado Low took shape during the early morning hours of Saturday, December 28th, with an inverted trough extending up toward the Red River Valley. During the day, the surface low tracked into eastern Nebraska, but the inverted trough remained over the valley. This allowed snow to spread slowly from the South Dakota border up to the highway 2 corridor. It was a very mild day, with high temperatures in the low 30s over southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota, to the lower 20s in the Devils Lake region. Temperatures did not change much Saturday night, as the surface low tracked from eastern Nebraska up into north central Iowa. Meanwhile, the snow finally reached the Canadian border, and north to northeast winds continued to increase. From Sunday into Sunday night, the surface low lingered over southwest Minnesota, before it finally began to fill. This kept a prolonged period of gusty north winds and steady snow over eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. From 5 to 17 inches of snow fell across the area, with the highest totals generally over the central Red River Valley into portions of southeast North Dakota. Winds along and west of the Red River Valley also gusted from 40 to 50 mph, causing blizzard conditions from Sunday morning through Monday morning. Interstate 29 was closed in eastern North Dakota and Interstate 94 was closed from Fargo to Bismarck.

Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST. There were a total of 6 injuries (indirect) and one fatality (indirect) during the event. See November 2019 Storm Data episode for more information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 1.0 inch at Lake Wilson. See the 11/28 Winter Weather event at 2100CST for more details.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two severe storms developed during the early morning of Thursday, July 18th in central, and southern Minnesota. One of the storms that developed in Scott County quickly became severe near Prior Lake and dropped up to baseball size hail from the south side of Prior Lake, northeast through Credit River, Savage, and Burnsville. Another severe storm produced up to golf ball size hail along the Swift, and Pope County line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the continued path of very large hail that moved from Scott County, into Dakota County. Up to ping pong size hail continued to fall into Dakota County near the I-35W, and I-35E interchange.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick snowfall developed along the leading edge of an Arctic cold front that moved across Minnesota Sunday, and remained nearly stationary over southern Minnesota through late Sunday night. A surface low developed across  the plains Sunday night, and quickly moved northeast over the Midwest, and across the Great Lakes by Monday afternoon. By early Monday morning, strong northwest winds developed on the back side of this system, with areas of blowing snow developing across west central/southwest and south central Minnesota Monday afternoon. ||An area of snow developed across western and northern Minnesota after midnight Sunday, and moved across the state, and into western Wisconsin late Monday morning.  Most areas of west central, central, and parts of east central Minnesota received between 2 and 5 inches of snow by Monday morning. The heaviest occurred in northern Todd County where 5 to 6 inches fell. There were some locally higher amounts near 5 inches around St. Cloud and Milaca before the snow tapered off to flurries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in Hewitt, Minnesota, in far northern Todd County, received 6.2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm began on November 30th. A fairly atypical hybrid surface low tracked across northern Nebraska into central Iowa from Saturday, November 30th into early Sunday, December 1st. For most of the event, temperatures held in the upper 20s to lower 30s, with steady east-northeast winds. This cross valley flow tends to limit stronger wind gusts. Initially, there was a little mixed precipitation, but it quickly turned over to snow. As winds turned more northerly during the early morning hours of December 1st, temperatures fell and winds finally became more gusty. However, by that time, the snow was tapering off. Most areas picked up 6 to 12 inches of snow. Because wind speeds never got that gusty during the event, roads were never closed. However, travel was not recommended in some areas.

A deep low pressure system tracked across the Northern Plains in the days following Thanksgiving, and produced heavy snow across west central Minnesota. Areas of freezing drizzle occurred on Thanksgiving Day and on Friday the 29th, bringing some spotty icy conditions. Heavy, wet snow then tracked across the area from late on the 29th persisting into Saturday the 30th. The snow then tapered off and ended during the morning hours of December 1st. See November 2019 storm data.||Northwest winds gusting to 30 to 50 mph caused areas of blowing snow reducing visibilities and creating difficult holiday travel. Some snowfall amounts include, 6 inches at Ortonville and 7 inches at Browns Valley.

This storm began on November 30th. A fairly atypical hybrid surface low tracked across northern Nebraska into central Iowa from Saturday, November 30th into early Sunday, December 1st. For most of the event, temperatures held in the upper 20s to lower 30s, with steady east-northeast winds. This cross valley flow tends to limit stronger wind gusts. Initially, there was a little mixed precipitation, but it quickly turned over to snow. As winds turned more northerly during the early morning hours of December 1st, temperatures fell and winds finally became more gusty. However, by that time, the snow was tapering off. Most areas picked up 6 to 12 inches of snow. Because wind speeds never got that gusty during the event, roads were never closed. However, travel was not recommended in some areas.

Thunderstorms with heavy rain moved across southeast Minnesota during the morning of September 19th. These storms dropped between 2 and 4 inches of rain with the resulting runoff producing some flooding. A campground northwest of Spring Grove (Houston County) had to be evacuated due to flooding along Riceford Creek.

An area of low pressure moved from the Southern Plains to northern Iowa the 28th into the 29th and spread snowfall across northern Minnesota starting the morning of the 28th. Snow was moderate to heavy at times before warmer air aloft worked in and changed snow to rain, freezing rain and sleet. Snowfall totals of 6 to 8 inches were seen around the region before the changeover with higher amounts to around 9 inches in far northern areas where snow persisted longer. The heavy, wet snow caused a roof to partially collapse at a soft drink bottling plant. Additionally, icing was seen after the snow leading to a layer of ice on top of snow covered roads leading to dangerous driving conditions. Strong northeast winds and high waves along the North Shore of Lake Superior also led to flooding in downtown Grand Marais.

Snow spread into eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota during the late afternoon and early evening of December 8th and continued into the morning of December 9th. Late in the afternoon of the 8th, temperatures ranged from zero at Devils Lake, to 12 degrees at Grand Forks and Fargo, to 22 degrees at Park Rapids. By mid morning of December 9th, temperatures had dropped to -13 degrees at Devils Lake, -5 degrees at Fargo, and 4 degrees at Park Rapids. East central North Dakota into adjacent areas of northwest Minnesota picked up 6 to 10 inches of snow. A little further south, or along the Interstate 94 corridor in North Dakota and the highway 10 corridor in northwest Minnesota, picked up a little mixed precipitation, 4 to 6 inches of snow, and had wind gusts to 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from the Southern Plains to northern Iowa the 28th into the 29th and spread snowfall across northern Minnesota starting the morning of the 28th. Snow was moderate to heavy at times before warmer air aloft worked in and changed snow to rain, freezing rain and sleet. Snowfall totals of 6 to 8 inches were seen around the region before the changeover with higher amounts to around 9 inches in far northern areas where snow persisted longer. The heavy, wet snow caused a roof to partially collapse at a soft drink bottling plant. Additionally, icing was seen after the snow leading to a layer of ice on top of snow covered roads leading to dangerous driving conditions. Strong northeast winds and high waves along the North Shore of Lake Superior also led to flooding in downtown Grand Marais.

Snow spread into eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota during the late afternoon and early evening of December 8th and continued into the morning of December 9th. Late in the afternoon of the 8th, temperatures ranged from zero at Devils Lake, to 12 degrees at Grand Forks and Fargo, to 22 degrees at Park Rapids. By mid morning of December 9th, temperatures had dropped to -13 degrees at Devils Lake, -5 degrees at Fargo, and 4 degrees at Park Rapids. East central North Dakota into adjacent areas of northwest Minnesota picked up 6 to 10 inches of snow. A little further south, or along the Interstate 94 corridor in North Dakota and the highway 10 corridor in northwest Minnesota, picked up a little mixed precipitation, 4 to 6 inches of snow, and had wind gusts to 35 mph.

A Colorado Low took shape during the early morning hours of Saturday, December 28th, with an inverted trough extending up toward the Red River Valley. During the day, the surface low tracked into eastern Nebraska, but the inverted trough remained over the valley. This allowed snow to spread slowly from the South Dakota border up to the highway 2 corridor. It was a very mild day, with high temperatures in the low 30s over southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota, to the lower 20s in the Devils Lake region. Temperatures did not change much Saturday night, as the surface low tracked from eastern Nebraska up into north central Iowa. Meanwhile, the snow finally reached the Canadian border, and north to northeast winds continued to increase. From Sunday into Sunday night, the surface low lingered over southwest Minnesota, before it finally began to fill. This kept a prolonged period of gusty north winds and steady snow over eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. From 5 to 17 inches of snow fell across the area, with the highest totals generally over the central Red River Valley into portions of southeast North Dakota. Winds along and west of the Red River Valley also gusted from 40 to 50 mph, causing blizzard conditions from Sunday morning through Monday morning. Interstate 29 was closed in eastern North Dakota and Interstate 94 was closed from Fargo to Bismarck.

A slow moving and powerful winter storm spread snow across the Northland starting the evening of the 29th and brought a few waves of snow into the afternoon of the 30th. The low then deepened and passed to the south of the region, bringing heavy snowfall and high winds the evening of the 30th into the morning of the 1st. A sustained 10 hour period of blizzard conditions was observed in the Twin Ports with widespread snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches. Heavier amounts to around 2 feet fell downwind of the head of Lake Superior and along the South Shore. Additionally, lakeshore flooding occurred in the Duluth area with a prolonged period of northeast winds pushing water up onto shore and waves causing beach erosion along Park Point.

A fairly atypical hybrid surface low tracked across northern Nebraska into central Iowa from Saturday, November 30th into early Sunday, December 1st. For most of the event, temperatures held in the upper 20s to lower 30s, with steady east-northeast winds. This cross valley flow tends to limit stronger wind gusts. Initially, there was a little mixed precipitation, but it quickly turned over to snow. As winds turned more northerly during the early morning hours of December 1st, temperatures fell and winds finally became more gusty. However, by that time, the snow was tapering off. Most areas picked up 6 to 12 inches of snow. Because wind speeds never got that gusty during the event, roads were never closed. However, travel was not recommended in some areas.

An anticipated winter storm brought heavy snow across the Upper Midwest. It started late Tuesday afternoon, and quickly exited the region by late Wednesday morning. ||The winter storm was a typical Colorado Low that developed in the central Rockies Monday night, moved out into the plains of Colorado, and tracked east, then northeast across Kansas, northern Missouri, southeast Iowa, and into southern Wisconsin by Wednesday morning. The main forcing with this system was at mid-levels and it was also quite fast. The strongest forcing that contributed  to this system was late Tuesday evening, and lasted around 6 hours. Therefore, snowfall amounts were limited to locally 6 to 9 inches where the best forcing and lift occurred as the surface low tracked across eastern Iowa, and into southern/eastern Wisconsin.  Wind speeds were generally 15-30 mph, with blowing and drifting snow in open areas.||In Minnesota, the highest totals were in the Twin Cities metro, which received 9-11 inches of snow based on public and trained observers.  Some of the higher totals observed include:|New Prague, Minnesota, 11.0 inches. |Savage, Minnesota, 9.5 inches. |Minneapolis/St. Paul Airport, 9.2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather observers measured around 6 inches of snow before the snow tapered off Wednesday morning. The heaviest occurred Tuesday evening, and into the early morning of Wednesday, November 27th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of strong northeast winds pushed water on shore in the Canal Park area leading to flooding there and along Harbor Drive, closing the street, on the harbor side of Canal Park. Access to Park Point was closed at the Aerial Lift Bridge for a period due to deep water at the foot of the approach. Eventually, limited access was provided to residents of Park Point. Additionally, beach erosion from the waves was widespread along Park Point.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm passing through the region led to heavy snow along with wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph plus for an almost 12 hour period. This led to persistent reduced visibilities of 1/4 mi or less for the duration of the strong winds leading to whiteout conditions. Snowfall of almost 2 feet accumulated during this time as well leading to impassable roads and stranded motorists. Conditions were so dangerous, plows were pulled off the roads until the next morning.

The first winter storm of the season moved across the region during the busy pre-Thanksgiving travel period. The storm dumped a swath of heavy snow over much of southeast Minnesota with accumulations generally in the 5 to 8 inch range. Winds also gusted between 40 to 50 mph which led to drifting snow and travel impacts across the region. The highest reported total was 8 inches near Wabasha (Wabasha County). The highest recorded wind gust was 51 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 5 to 7 inches of snow fell across Dodge County. The highest reported total was 7.3 inches in Kasson. Winds gusts of 40 to 45 mph created drifting snow as the storm was departing the region.
