The storm dumped a swath of heavy snow over much of southeast Minnesota with accumulations generally in the 5 to 8 inch range. Winds also gusted between 40 to 50 mph which led to drifting snow and travel impacts across the region. The highest reported total was 8 inches near Wabasha (Wabasha County). The highest recorded wind gust was 51 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 5 to 8 inches of snow fell across Wabasha County. The highest reported total was 8 inches in Wabasha. Wind gusts of 35 to 40 mph created drifting snow as the storm was departing the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anticipated winter storm brought heavy snow across the Upper Midwest. It started late Tuesday afternoon, and quickly exited the region by late Wednesday morning. ||The winter storm was a typical Colorado Low that developed in the central Rockies Monday night, moved out into the plains of Colorado, and tracked east, then northeast across Kansas, northern Missouri, southeast Iowa, and into southern Wisconsin by Wednesday morning. The main forcing with this system was at mid-levels and it was also quite fast. The strongest forcing that contributed  to this system was late Tuesday evening, and lasted around 6 hours. Therefore, snowfall amounts were limited to locally 6 to 9 inches where the best forcing and lift occurred as the surface low tracked across eastern Iowa, and into southern/eastern Wisconsin.  Wind speeds were generally 15-30 mph, with blowing and drifting snow in open areas.||In Minnesota, the highest totals were in the Twin Cities metro, which received 9-11 inches of snow based on public and trained observers.  Some of the higher totals observed include:|New Prague, Minnesota, 11.0 inches. |Savage, Minnesota, 9.5 inches. |Minneapolis/St. Paul Airport, 9.2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather observers measured 5 to 7 inches of snow before the snow tapered off Wednesday morning. The heaviest occurred late Tuesday evening, and into the early morning of Wednesday, November 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system tracked across the Northern Plains in the days following Thanksgiving, and produced heavy snow across west central Minnesota. Areas of freezing drizzle occurred on Thanksgiving Day and on Friday the 29th, bringing some spotty icy conditions. Heavy, wet snow then tracked across the area from late on the 29th persisting into Saturday the 30th. The snow then tapered off and ended during the morning hours of December 1st. See December 2019 storm data.||Northwest winds gusting to 30 to 50 mph caused areas of blowing snow reducing visibilities and creating difficult holiday travel. Some snowfall amounts include, 6 inches at Ortonville and 7 inches at Browns Valley.

A complex of thunderstorms formed overnight in northern Minnesota then tracked southeast through the early morning hours along an instability gradient. Large hail and wind damage were observed with this system, but due to it occurring during the early morning hours, most of the damage reports were not received until the next morning. Heavy rainfall also led to some isolated flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was snapped in half with a large limb broken off another tree and landing on the roof of a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly atypical hybrid surface low tracked across northern Nebraska into central Iowa from Saturday, November 30th into early Sunday, December 1st. For most of the event, temperatures held in the upper 20s to lower 30s, with steady east-northeast winds. This cross valley flow tends to limit stronger wind gusts. Initially, there was a little mixed precipitation, but it quickly turned over to snow. As winds turned more northerly during the early morning hours of December 1st, temperatures fell and winds finally became more gusty. However, by that time, the snow was tapering off. Most areas picked up 6 to 12 inches of snow. Because wind speeds never got that gusty during the event, roads were never closed. However, travel was not recommended in some areas.

Lake enhancement along the North Shore of Lake Superior led to heavier snowfall amounts in the Minnesota Arrowhead, with reports of 6 to 10 inches from northern St. Louis County eastward to Lake Superior. A spotter 12 miles north Hovland received 11 inches of snowfall due to lake enhancement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, and fairly slow moving storm system first developed Friday afternoon November 29th with an area of light snow from the initial surge of warmer air across southern Minnesota. This area of light snow moved across eastern Minnesota, and adjacent western Wisconsin Friday night, with generally one to three inches of snow falling across the region. By Saturday morning, this initial wave of light snow moved north into northeastern Minnesota. A secondary band of precipitation, with some sleet reported with this second band, moved from northern Iowa, across southern Minnesota during the morning. This band produced mainly one inch of snow, with some sleet at times. ||During the afternoon, the main storm system moved out into the plains of Nebraska, with an enhanced area of precipitation developing across eastern Minnesota, and western Wisconsin Saturday afternoon, November 30th.  Some areas of east central, and central Minnesota had up to 6 inches of snow by late Saturday afternoon, and into the early morning hours of Sunday, December 1st.  ||The strongest part of the storm system moved across Iowa Saturday evening, and into southern Wisconsin Sunday morning December 1st. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over northeastern Minnesota on the afternoon of the 4th. A warm front was draped along the International Border with a secondary warm front pushing northward from the south. Daytime heating, advection of a more favorable airmass for severe weather and a shortwave trough moved into the western part of the area and lead to increased shear and steep lapse rates that led to the development of storms. Storms become stronger through the afternoon as they moved southeast, but limited reports of severe weather were received. Aside from a few hail and wind damage reports, one instance of flash flooding was noted along a Minnesota State Highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were downed in Cloquet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few marginally severe storms developed across north-central Minnesota during the afternoon hours as an upper level system approached from the north and a warm front from the south. These features combined to produce widespread storms that were heavy rainfall producers for the most part. One storm did produce some low end severe hail that caused tree damage, but for the most part, the storms remained below severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail lasting between 5 and 10 minutes stripped the leaves off of trees along Nature Center Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms formed overnight in northern Minnesota then tracked southeast through the early morning hours along an instability gradient. Large hail and wind damage were observed with this system, but due to it occurring during the early morning hours, most of the damage reports were not received until the next morning. Heavy rainfall also led to some isolated flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report was received via social media that Morrison Brook overflowed its banks and flooded a portion of West County Line Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Northland bringing snowfall and wintry mix to the region the 20th into the 21st. Lake enhancement along the North Shore of Lake Superior led to heavier snowfall amounts in the Minnesota Arrowhead, with reports of 6 to 10 inches from northern St. Louis County eastward to Lake Superior. A spotter 12 miles north Hovland received 11 inches of snowfall due to lake enhancement.

An anticipated winter storm brought heavy snow across the Upper Midwest. It started late Tuesday afternoon, and quickly exited the region by late Wednesday morning. ||The winter storm was a typical Colorado Low that developed in the central Rockies Monday night, moved out into the plains of Colorado, and tracked east, then northeast across Kansas, northern Missouri, southeast Iowa, and into southern Wisconsin by Wednesday morning. The main forcing with this system was at mid-levels and it was also quite fast. The strongest forcing that contributed  to this system was late Tuesday evening, and lasted around 6 hours. Therefore, snowfall amounts were limited to locally 6 to 9 inches where the best forcing and lift occurred as the surface low tracked across eastern Iowa, and into southern/eastern Wisconsin.  Wind speeds were generally 15-30 mph, with blowing and drifting snow in open areas.||In Minnesota, the highest totals were in the Twin Cities metro, which received 9-11 inches of snow based on public and trained observers.  Some of the higher totals observed include:|New Prague, Minnesota, 11.0 inches. |Savage, Minnesota, 9.5 inches. |Minneapolis/St. Paul Airport, 9.2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather observers measured 4 to 6 inches of snow before the snow tapered off Wednesday morning. The heaviest occurred late Tuesday evening, and into the early morning of Wednesday, November 27th.

A slow moving and powerful winter storm spread snow across the Northland starting the evening of the 29th and brought a few waves of snow into the afternoon of the 30th. The low then deepened and passed to the south of the region, bringing heavy snowfall and high winds the evening of the 30th into the morning of the 1st. A sustained 10 hour period of blizzard conditions was observed in the Twin Ports with widespread snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches. Heavier amounts to around 2 feet fell downwind of the head of Lake Superior and along the South Shore. Additionally, lakeshore flooding occurred in the Duluth area with a prolonged period of northeast winds pushing water up onto shore and waves causing beach erosion along Park Point.

A strong cold front pushing through southeast South Dakota during the evening helped to spark a small complex of thunderstorms. These storms weakened heading into Minnesota, but managed to produce isolated damaging wind before dissipating.

A series of thunderstorms developed within an environment favorable for tornadoes across northeast Minnesota. Storms developed in northern Minnesota and moved east-northeasterly, eventually crossing into Ontario. Several supercell thunderstorms were observed which produced heavy rainfall, hail, damaging straight-line winds and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five pine and aspen trees about 2 feet in diameter were knocked down due to straight line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms developed within an environment favorable for tornadoes across northeast Minnesota. Storms developed in northern Minnesota and moved east-northeasterly, eventually crossing into Ontario. Several supercell thunderstorms were observed which produced heavy rainfall, hail, damaging straight-line winds and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The supercell which produced the Gheen Hill tornado began north |of Togo in far southeast Koochiching County. It traveled toward |the east-northeast and gradually organized. Sporadic straight-line|wind damage was observed a few miles northwest of Gheen and west |of US Highway 53. The tornado developed 1 mile west of US-53 and |tracked toward the east-northeast. We received several photographs|from a spotter who observed the tornado north of Gheen Hill |before it crossed US-53. The tornado crossed Rice Lake and |strengthened to low-end EF-1 intensity shortly after. The tornado |continued east-northeastward across the easternmost portion of |Johnson Road. It weakened and dissipated just to the east of an |ATV trail northwest of Gheen. Aside from the few spotter photos of|this tornado, high-resolution satellite imagery was critical to |assessing the location, path, width, and peak intensity of this |tornado. We are very grateful to the spotter who provided the only|known photos of this tornado. Additional help in identifying this|tornado's path was provided by a private sector meteorologist from Grand|Rapids, MN. This supercell thunderstorm would go on to produce |the Carlson Lake tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms developed within an environment favorable for tornadoes across northeast Minnesota. Storms developed in northern Minnesota and moved east-northeasterly, eventually crossing into Ontario. Several supercell thunderstorms were observed which produced heavy rainfall, hail, damaging straight-line winds and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The supercell which produced the Gheen Hill tornado continued to |move toward the east-northeast and a second mesocyclone developed |a short time after the Gheen Hill tornado dissipated. The Carlson |Lake tornado developed approximately 500 yards west of the Elbow |River and six-tenths of a mile north of the western end of Ban |Lake. The tornado tracked toward the northeast producing sporadic |EF-0 damage to trees. The tornado turned due east approximately |one mile west of Carlson Lake and intensified. The tornado produced|a swath of EF-1 damage, which was a maximum of 300 yards wide and|passed over Carlson Lake. Tree damage corresponding to high-end |EF-1 wind speeds was noted over much of this area. The tornado |turned toward the northeast after crossing Carlson Lake and |crossed over Elbow Lake Forest Road before gradually weakening. |The tornado dissipated shortly after crossing Shuster Road. We are|very grateful for the help identifying this tornado's path |provided by a private meteorologist in Grand Rapids, MN. High-|resolution satellite imagery was critical to assessing the |location, path, width, and peak intensity of this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms developed within an environment favorable for tornadoes across northeast Minnesota. Storms developed in northern Minnesota and moved east-northeasterly, eventually crossing into Ontario. Several supercell thunderstorms were observed which produced heavy rainfall, hail, damaging straight-line winds and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey of a tornado within Voyageurs National Park has |been completed and confirmed a tornado from approximately Locator |Lake, approximately 6.4 miles north of Kabetogama, to Marion Bay |on Rainy Lake, approximately 9.2 miles north-northeast of |Kabetogama. The tornado likely extended farther northeast across |Rainy Lake and possibly into Canada, so the length of this tornado|should be considered an estimate. The end point location is |estimated from video of the tornado moving over Rainy Lake. This |survey was completed using photos and videos from Voyageurs |National Park, the public, and business owners in the region. Very|special thanks to Voyageurs National Park for sharing aerial |images of the damage path which was critical to assessing the |path, width, and severity of this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms developed within an environment favorable for tornadoes across northeast Minnesota. Storms developed in northern Minnesota and moved east-northeasterly, eventually crossing into Ontario. Several supercell thunderstorms were observed which produced heavy rainfall, hail, damaging straight-line winds and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large wooden outhouses were blown over by the winds at the North Star Rodeo grounds north of Effie. One of the outhouses was completely destroyed while the other remained intact.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning, and early afternoon of Friday, July 19th, a surface low moved from northeast South Dakota, eastward across central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin by the evening. There was a tight thermal gradient along the surface low/warm front across central Minnesota. During the afternoon, dew points rose to near 80 in the warm sector, with 50s/60s north of the front. This contrast produced MLCAPEs of near 5000 J/kg south of the front boundary. Effective wind shear parameters were very strong with values of 50-70 knots across central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon. This environment allowed for supercells to form which congealed into a bow echo that raced across east central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin. Very large hail, damaging straight-line winds, and a few tornadoes occurred with this bow echo. ||There was almost a continuous swath of large hail, measured up to golf ball size, that  fell from Stevens County, northeast through Pope, Douglas, and Todd Counties before 1230 CST. Damage occurred to cars, windows, and crops along the storm's path. Another severe storm developed west of Todd County, and moved across northern Todd County, and into Morrison County. There were numerous reports of large hail once again with the largest report of baseball size hail near Cushing. There were also reports of trees and power lines down across central and southwest Morrison County. ||Most of the damage in Mille Lacs, and Kanabec Counties was associated with straight line winds, but there were a couple reports of large hail. The bow echo intensified as it moved across Kanabec County, into southern Pine and northern Chisago Counties. ||The worst damage occurred from near Rush City, southeast into west central Wisconsin. The Chisago County fair had to be closed due to trees and power lines that were blown down throughout the fair grounds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of large hail, up to baseball size, that occurred across parts of Morrison County. Hail was reported near Cushing, then east-southeast to Randall, Camp Ripley area, and north of Freedhem. The same line of storms moved southeast to near Hillman, but no additional reports of hail was observed. However, the emergency managers did have reports of trees and power lines down across central Morrison County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This event was considered a derecho due to the path length, and width as the storms moved from South Dakota, through southern Minnesota, across Wisconsin, and the western part of Michigan, or approximately 860 miles. ||The morning of Saturday, July 20th, thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota, quickly moved eastward along a slowly moving cold front in far southern Minnesota. Thunderstorms developed into a line segment and bowed out, and caused damaging winds in a few areas of southern Minnesota. These storms quickly moved into Wisconsin by noon. There were several reports of measured winds by airport sensors, private weather stations, and the Minnesota Department of Transportation wind sensors.  Some of the measured wind gusts were between 50 and 55 mph, along with numerous blown down small tree limbs throughout the duration of the bow echo segment that weren't included in the storm damage report. The local emergency manager of Blue Earth County had 13 different reports of power lines down, and 28 reports of trees blown downed. ||A few storms developed prior to the line segment over southern Minnesota which across parts of the Twin Cities metro area. A wind gust of 59 knots was measured at the Crystal airport along with a large tree that blew down near the airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power was knocked out in the city of St. James due to blown down power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An afternoon intensifying mesoscale convective system that moved from southeast South Dakota, eastward across southern Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening of Friday, July 5th, produced sporadic wind damage around Arlington, southeast to Henderson, Minnesota. Two brief tornadoes occurred. Part of a roof was blown off in Arlington, but it wasn't enough to determine whether it was a tornado or a severe downburst from a NWS storm survey. Videos and pictures from social media did show a brief tornado touchdown 6 miles ESE of Arlington. The pictures and videos showed a rotating wall cloud throughout the duration of this severe storm as it moved across Sibley, and into southwest Scott, and northern Le Sueur Counties. ||This circulation weakened as it moved across northern Le Sueur County, but spotters did observe a brief, 5-10 second touchdown in a grove of trees near Elysian, which occurred later as the storm weakened.||In addition to the severe thunderstorms, thunderstorms trained over the same areas of south central Minnesota between Mankato, Owatonna, and Rochester. Nearly 4 inches of rain fell in an hour north of Waseca causing several roads in the area to become impassible. Local officials barricaded several roads north of Waseca due to water over the roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser filmed a brief tornado that touched down in an open field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning, and early afternoon of Friday, July 19th, a surface low moved from northeast South Dakota, eastward across central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin by the evening. There was a tight thermal gradient along the surface low/warm front across central Minnesota. During the afternoon, dew points rose to near 80 in the warm sector, with 50s/60s north of the front. This contrast produced MLCAPEs of near 5000 J/kg south of the front boundary. Effective wind shear parameters were very strong with values of 50-70 knots across central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon. This environment allowed for supercells to form which congealed into a bow echo that raced across east central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin. Very large hail, damaging straight-line winds, and a few tornadoes occurred with this bow echo. ||There was almost a continuous swath of large hail, measured up to golf ball size, that  fell from Stevens County, northeast through Pope, Douglas, and Todd Counties before 1230 CST. Damage occurred to cars, windows, and crops along the storm's path. Another severe storm developed west of Todd County, and moved across northern Todd County, and into Morrison County. There were numerous reports of large hail once again with the largest report of baseball size hail near Cushing. There were also reports of trees and power lines down across central and southwest Morrison County. ||Most of the damage in Mille Lacs, and Kanabec Counties was associated with straight line winds, but there were a couple reports of large hail. The bow echo intensified as it moved across Kanabec County, into southern Pine and northern Chisago Counties. ||The worst damage occurred from near Rush City, southeast into west central Wisconsin. The Chisago County fair had to be closed due to trees and power lines that were blown down throughout the fair grounds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of large hail, with a measured report of quarter size near Farwell, occurred across northwest Pope County.

The Chisago County fair grounds was closed due to blown down trees and power lines in Rush City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This event was considered a derecho due to the path length, and width as the storms moved from South Dakota, through southern Minnesota, across Wisconsin, and the western part of Michigan, or approximately 860 miles. ||The morning of Saturday, July 20th, thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota, quickly moved eastward along a slowly moving cold front in far southern Minnesota. Thunderstorms developed into a line segment and bowed out, and caused damaging winds in a few areas of southern Minnesota. These storms quickly moved into Wisconsin by noon. There were several reports of measured winds by airport sensors, private weather stations, and the Minnesota Department of Transportation wind sensors.  Some of the measured wind gusts were between 50 and 55 mph, along with numerous blown down small tree limbs throughout the duration of the bow echo segment that weren't included in the storm damage report. The local emergency manager of Blue Earth County had 13 different reports of power lines down, and 28 reports of trees blown downed. ||A few storms developed prior to the line segment over southern Minnesota which across parts of the Twin Cities metro area. A wind gust of 59 knots was measured at the Crystal airport along with a large tree that blew down near the airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Minnesota Department of Transportation weather sensor reported a measured wind gust of 52 knots.

Several clusters of storms developed during the afternoon of Wednesday, July 3rd across southern Minnesota. Most of the storms were outflow dominant, and were mainly pulse type in nature. One isolated storm became severe near Willmar, Minnesota. The local airport measured a wind gust of 58 mph with this storm.

Several clusters of storms developed during the afternoon of Wednesday, July 3rd across southern Minnesota. Most of the storms were outflow dominant, and were mainly pulse type in nature. One isolated storm became severe near Willmar, Minnesota. The local airport measured a wind gust of 58 mph with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local officials reported several trees blown down in the city of Willmar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An afternoon intensifying mesoscale convective system that moved from southeast South Dakota, eastward across southern Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening of Friday, July 5th, produced sporadic wind damage around Arlington, southeast to Henderson, Minnesota. Two brief tornadoes occurred. Part of a roof was blown off in Arlington, but it wasn't enough to determine whether it was a tornado or a severe downburst from a NWS storm survey. Videos and pictures from social media did show a brief tornado touchdown 6 miles ESE of Arlington. The pictures and videos showed a rotating wall cloud throughout the duration of this severe storm as it moved across Sibley, and into southwest Scott, and northern Le Sueur Counties. ||This circulation weakened as it moved across northern Le Sueur County, but spotters did observe a brief, 5-10 second touchdown in a grove of trees near Elysian, which occurred later as the storm weakened.||In addition to the severe thunderstorms, thunderstorms trained over the same areas of south central Minnesota between Mankato, Owatonna, and Rochester. Nearly 4 inches of rain fell in an hour north of Waseca causing several roads in the area to become impassible. Local officials barricaded several roads north of Waseca due to water over the roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple storm chasers observed and filmed a brief tornado in an open field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storms congealed in central Minnesota during the late afternoon, and early evening across Morrison County. These storms produced severe hail, some up to ping pong ball size near Little Falls.  They also produced widespread areas of downed trees and power lines. There was a measured wind gust of 73 MPH near Pierz. In addition, torrential rainfall led to flash flooding at Camp Ripley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Minnesota National Guard relayed pictures and an account of a flash flood that went through Camp Ripley during the evening of July 14th.  It began approximately 7:15 PM CST. During the height of the event, water flowed through buildings and the latrine. The depth of the water was up to 2 feet deep, and cars were stranded in parking lots. There were also reports outside of the camp of washed out roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two severe storms developed during the early morning of Thursday, July 18th in central, and southern Minnesota. One of the storms that developed in Scott County quickly became severe near Prior Lake and dropped up to baseball size hail from the south side of Prior Lake, northeast through Credit River, Savage, and Burnsville. Another severe storm produced up to golf ball size hail along the Swift, and Pope County line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail, up to baseball size, fell across the southern part of Prior Lake, northeast through Credit River Township, and up to Savage in Scott County.

High winds affected west central Minnesota from the evening of the 21st to the early morning hours of the 22nd. Northwest winds of 30 to 45 mph with gusts to 50 to 60 mph occurred.  A tree was blown down by the high winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado developed 3 miles southwest of Biscay and tracked northeast to just north of Biscay, Minnesota before dissipating. Corn was flattened along the path and some trees were uprooted near Minnesota Highway 22, and 150th Street. The maximum wind speed was estimated at 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon, of Friday, July 26th, thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary moving slowly southeastward across central Minnesota. These storms quickly became severe near St. Cloud and tracked southeast, mainly along the Mississippi River toward the northern suburbs of the Twin Cities. A swath of large hail developed near St. Cloud, where numerous hail stones up to golf ball size were reported. These hail stones rose to the diameter of 4 near Clear Lake. Social media was active and numerous pictures came in supporting the large hail stones which eventually moved across the northern sections of the Twin Cities metro area. Slowly, as the evening progressed, the severe weather abated with only isolated reports of large hail into Washington County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado spun up on the southwest side of Sylvan Lake and continued northeast in a broken path through Bone Lake, and moved into Chisago County on 240th Street North, east of Morgan Avenue. The path length was approximately 4.4 miles across Washington County, with a maximum width of 440 yards. The worst damage occurred on Bone Lake where numerous large trees were uprooted or blown down. Some roof damage was noted, but most of the trees fell away from homes. Other damage along the path was associated with flattened corn, tree damage, a storage shed that had its roof blown off, and damage to a solar panel farm.

These surveys indicated an EF1 tornado formed near Silver Lake, and another between Forest Lake, north of Scandia, and into southern Chicago County. ||The worst damage occurred along Bone Lake near Scandia, where very large trees were uprooted or blown down. Some of the trees caused significant damage to lake homes on Bone Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the continuation path of the Washington County tornado that moved into Chisago County on 240th Street North, east of Morgan Avenue. This EF1 tornado continued for approximately another 3.8 miles before dissipating north of a farm that was damaged on 268th Street. Along the path in Chisago County, the tornado damaged a farm shed, uprooted numerous trees, damaged an outdoor wedding venue near Highway 25, and destroyed an outbuilding before dissipating. Maximum winds were estimated at 90 mph, with a width of 250 yards in Chisago County.

Several clusters of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of Monday, July 15th in central Minnesota. A few storms also developed ahead of the storms in central Minnesota, and became severe west of the Twin Cities. Hail stones as large as 2 inches in diameter were reported around Delano, and the western suburbs of the Twin Cities. By the late afternoon, and into the evening, these storms become more wind producers. Several of the storms produced severe downburst wind gusts and caused damage to several trees throughout the Twin Cities metro area. An EF0 tornado also formed on the southern flank of the storms, east of Belle Plaine, and caused significant damage to a farm. ||In addition to the wind damage and hail, these storms were heavy rainfall producers. As the storms merged and trained over the same areas, rainfall amounts in excess of 3 to 4 inches occurred in portions of Hennepin County of the Twin Cities. ||Other areas in the Twin Cities also had excessive rainfall with numerous reports of 1 to 3 inches of rainfall in a short period of time. Flooding were observed in the typical low spots where runoff was too much to handle.

A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.50 inches at Tracy and 1.15 inches at Windom) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands. County Roads 6 and 44 were inundated with flood water for several days. The Des Moines River near Avoca (gage site AVOM5) reached the second highest stage on record cresting 3.56 feet above flood stage on March 16. The river remained in moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

These storms quickly became severe near St. Cloud and tracked southeast, mainly along the Mississippi River toward the northern suburbs of the Twin Cities. A swath of large hail developed near St. Cloud, where numerous hail stones up to golf ball size were reported. These hail stones rose to the diameter of 4 near Clear Lake. Social media was active and numerous pictures came in supporting the large hail stones which eventually moved across the northern sections of the Twin Cities metro area. Slowly, as the evening progressed, the severe weather abated with only isolated reports of large hail into Washington County.

Several clusters of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of Monday, July 15th in central Minnesota. A few storms also developed ahead of the storms in central Minnesota, and became severe west of the Twin Cities. Hail stones as large as 2 inches in diameter were reported around Delano, and the western suburbs of the Twin Cities. By the late afternoon, and into the evening, these storms become more wind producers. Several of the storms produced severe downburst wind gusts and caused damage to several trees throughout the Twin Cities metro area. An EF0 tornado also formed on the southern flank of the storms, east of Belle Plaine, and caused significant damage to a farm. ||In addition to the wind damage and hail, these storms were heavy rainfall producers. As the storms merged and trained over the same areas, rainfall amounts in excess of 3 to 4 inches occurred in portions of Hennepin County of the Twin Cities. ||Other areas in the Twin Cities also had excessive rainfall with numerous reports of 1 to 3 inches of rainfall in a short period of time. Flooding were observed in the typical low spots where runoff was too much to handle.

Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation from September 10-12 that resulted in 5 to 10 inches of rainfall near the South Dakota border and 2 to 5 inches toward the east, extreme rises occurred on numerous creeks and rivers mid-month, with many basin locations reaching major to record stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After excessive two-day rainfall during September 10-12 (5.70 inches at Marshall), the Redwood River at Russell (gage site RUSM5) reached the sixth highest stage on record of 1.81 feet above flood stage on September 12. Significant agricultural land was flooded, with numerous county roads under water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation from September 10-12 that resulted in 5 to 10 inches of rainfall near the South Dakota border and 2 to 5 inches toward the east, extreme rises occurred on numerous creeks and rivers mid-month, with many basin locations reaching major to record stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After excessive two-day rainfall during September 10-12 (9.08 inches near Edgerton), the Rock River near Hardwick (gage site HRDM5) reached the second highest stage on record of 6.14 feet above flood stage on September 12. Numerous county roads were under water near Edgerton, with significant flooding of agricultural land.  The city park was under several feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation from September 10-12 that resulted in 5 to 10 inches of rainfall near the South Dakota border and 2 to 5 inches toward the east, extreme rises occurred on numerous creeks and rivers mid-month, with many basin locations reaching major to record stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After excessive two-day rainfall during September 10-12 (record 2-day rainfall of 6.75 inches at Pipestone), Split Rock Creek below Jasper (gage site JSPM5) reached the highest stage on record of 9.39 feet above flood stage on September 12. Numerous county roads were under water near Sherman, including 250th Street. Significant amounts of agricultural land were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation from September 10-12 that resulted in 5 to 10 inches of rainfall near the South Dakota border and 2 to 5 inches toward the east, extreme rises occurred on numerous creeks and rivers mid-month, with many basin locations reaching major to record stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After excessive two-day rainfall during September 10-12 (9.08 inches near Edgerton and 4.72 inches at Luverne), the Rock River at Luverne (gage site LUVM5) reached the third highest stage on record of 3.94 feet above flood stage on September 13. Numerous county roads were inundated, and the city park in Luverne was under several feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation from September 10-12 that resulted in 5 to 10 inches of rainfall near the South Dakota border and 2 to 5 inches toward the east, extreme rises occurred on numerous creeks and rivers mid-month, with many basin locations reaching major to record stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After excessive two-day rainfall during September 10-12 (5.35 inches at Slayton), the West Fork of the Des Moines River near Avoca (gage site AVOM5) reached the third highest stage on record of 2.71 feet above flood stage on September 16. Significant agricultural land was flooded, with numerous county roads under water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary boundary again focused widespread heavy rainfall during the late evening on September 11 and early morning hours of September 12. Precipitable water was at or exceeding the record values for the date at nearby locations. As the low-level jet increased during the evening over the outflow pool in the wake of earlier severe convection, training of convection initiated across areas which had received several inches of rainfall during the previous night. Even short duration intense rainfall produced areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Within three hours of the start of heavy rainfall, the river gage on Pipestone Creek at Pipestone rose quickly over flood stage.  Several homes on the northeast side of Pipestone were inundated with flood water.

A frontal zone remained locked in place under southwest flow aloft as a series of mid-level waves moved across the region over a three-day period. Widespread heavy rainfall resulted and amounts reached a two-day record at Pipestone (6.75 inches) as well as totals of nearly 11 inches at Avoca, 9.24 inches at Edgerton, and 6.02 inches at Lake Wilson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding resulted in crop losses across the area. Crop loss estimates provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rainfall occurred across southwest Minnesota during the evening of June 30th, through the early morning of July 1st. Up to 7 inches of rain fell near Morton, Minnesota, where approximately 30 homes were impacted by small streams and runoff. Three homes had 6-8 feet of water in their basements. Minnesota Highway 19 was closed east of Morton from 400 to 800 CST July 1st due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a measured rainfall report of 3.44 inches near Belview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of Friday, July 12th, a small complex of storms developed in South Dakota, and moved eastward into west central Minnesota before noon. The storms  continued to march across southern Minnesota, almost in a straight line from near Redwood Falls, eastward into the southern part of the Twin Cities metro area. Several wind gusts of 50 to 55 mph occurred, and a wind gust of 59 mph was measured in Scott County. Sporadic trees were blown down also along the path, but the majority of the wind damage was minor. The Redwood Falls airport measured a wind gust of 55 mph at 1047 LST. There was also a measured wind gust of 52 mph at the Minneapolis/St. Paul airport at 1234 LST, and a measured wind gust of 50 mph near Elko/New Market at 1226 LST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust from a local trained spotter.

An EF0 tornado also formed on the southern flank of the storms, east of Belle Plaine, and caused significant damage to a farm. ||In addition to the wind damage and hail, these storms were heavy rainfall producers. As the storms merged and trained over the same areas, rainfall amounts in excess of 3 to 4 inches occurred in portions of Hennepin County of the Twin Cities. ||Other areas in the Twin Cities also had excessive rainfall with numerous reports of 1 to 3 inches of rainfall in a short period of time. Flooding were observed in the typical low spots where runoff was too much to handle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local observer measured 3.78 inches of rainfall near Hamel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms moved from eastern South Dakota and southwestern Minnesota the morning of the 17th to the Minnesota Arrowhead by the mid-afternoon hours. These elevated storms were able to tap into some elevated instability and briefly become stronger over northeastern St. Louis County. Marginally severe hail was reported along with a few downed trees in the same area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marginally severe storms moved through the area during the early afternoon hours leading to sporadic downed trees. Following the storms, a homeowner was on his roof removing a downed tree when he lost his footing and fell approximately 20 feet to the ground and landing on a propane tank. First responders attempted life saving efforts upon arrival, but the homeowner died on the scene. The closest observing site to the location of the fatality was the Ely Airport which reported a max gust to 30 mph during the storm, but higher gusts were likely based on the tree damage reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal zone remained locked in place under southwest flow aloft as a series of mid-level waves moved across the region over a three-day period. Widespread heavy rainfall resulted and amounts reached a two-day record at Pipestone (6.75 inches) as well as totals of nearly 11 inches at Avoca, 9.24 inches at Edgerton, and 6.02 inches at Lake Wilson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding resulted in crop losses across the area. Crop loss estimates provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from significant early spring precipitation during late March and early April maintained flooding along the Des Moines River. Much of the basin experienced as much as 2 to 3 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation through April 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from occasional precipitation in late March and April maintained significant flooding of agricultural and pasture lands near the Des Moines River. The Des Moines River near Avoca (gage site AVOM5) crested twice at moderate flood stage during the month. The first crest was at 2.14 feet above flood stage on April 1. The second crest occurred on April 19 at 2.53 feet above flood stage. Moderate flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from significant early spring precipitation during late March and early April resulted in many smaller creeks and tributaries flooding across southwest Minnesota. Much of the basin experienced as much as 2 to 3 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation through April 14.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from occasional precipitation in late March and early April produced considerable flooding of agricultural land.  Other than a brief period from 0600CST April 26 to 1300CST April 27, the river remained in flood through the end of the month.
